visits.text: Start at Lake Voulismeni, the beating heart of Agios Nikolaos. This 64-meter-deep lake of karst origin was long thought to be bottomless—hence its local nickname—and ancient legend held that Athena and Artemis bathed in its waters. Since 1870, a narrow canal has linked it to the harbor. The tavernas and cafés lining its shores make it the perfect spot for a first break. If you're there in July, don't miss the Cliff Diving Festival: divers from around the world leap from the cliffs overlooking the lake—quite an improbable spectacle.

Don't skip the Archaeological Museum of Agios Nikolaos—it's the second most important in Crete after Heraklion. It traces the region's history from the Neolithic (around 5700 BC) through the Roman period. Its most famous treasure is the Goddess of Myrtos: a libation vessel shaped like a female figure with long outstretched arms, discovered at the Bronze Age settlement of Fournos Korifi and dating to approximately 2500 BC. Allow 60 to 90 minutes to see it properly.

On the road to Kritsa, stop 1 kilometer before the village: the Church of Panagia Kera sits tucked on the right side, guarded by an ancient olive tree with a hollow trunk. It's the best-preserved Byzantine monument on the entire island, and the frescoes covering every wall are the reason why. The central nave dates to the 13th century; the two lateral naves were added in the 14th—one bears the exact date of 1348 painted on the wall, along with a portrait of the donor Georgios Mazizanis, his wife, and child. The Palaeologan style, with its expressive figures, clean contours, and vivid colors, is rare for religious art of the period. Photos without flash are permitted.

Kritsa, perched at 300 meters elevation 9 kilometers from Agios Nikolaos, is one of Crete's largest villages and among its most picturesque, with stepped alleyways, stone houses, and weavers still working traditional looms. The local embroidery and textiles are renowned throughout the region—you'll find several artisans in the village center. In the evening, once the tour buses have left, the village becomes surprisingly quiet and authentic again.

From Kritsa, push on to the site of Lato, 3 kilometers away: a Dorian city founded in the 7th century BC, perched on two hills overlooking Mirabello Gulf. You'll find paved streets, monumental staircases, an agora, and temple foundations—and a view of the sea and mountains worth the trip alone. Allow 45 minutes to an hour for a leisurely visit. Bring water: there's no shade on the site.

activities.text: Stroll around Lake Voulismeni in the evening: that's when locals gather on the café terraces lining both shores, connected by a bridge. The buzz builds as night falls—there's real nightlife in the adjacent streets. If you're there early in the morning, the lake is strikingly calm and the reflections of the cliff in the water feel otherworldly.

Explore Kritsa for its craft workshops: weaving, embroidery, pottery, and local products (honey, oil, herbs) are sold directly by artisans in the alleyways. The village also has several modest museums, including the House of Kritsotopoula, a folklore museum housed in a traditional old dwelling, and the Rodanthi museum dedicated to Cretan medicinal plants and herbs.

If you're curious about the most ancient Cretan cuisine, order xinochondros at an Agios Nikolaos taverna: a fermented dried paste made from sheep's cheese and cracked wheat, a winter staple of the Cretan mountains, simmered into a soup with seasonal vegetables. It has a distinctly sour and salty taste—an acquired one, let's say. Pair it with a glass of Vilana white, the indigenous Cretan grape, light and fresh with citrus notes, ideal with fish from Mirabello Gulf.

Swim at Almyros Beach, about 2 kilometers south of Agios Nikolaos: a long stretch of fine sand with calm waters, well-equipped and less crowded than the town center beaches. Several beaches then dot the coast toward Istro and Pachia Ammos along Mirabello Bay—ideal for a late afternoon if you're returning from Lato or Kritsa.

visits.text: The archaeological site of Thermos is among this circuit's least-known destinations—and one of the most important for Greek architectural history. Federal capital of the Aetolian League, the sanctuary was the Western Greek equivalent of Delphi. Its Temple of Apollo Thermos, dated 630-610 BC, is one of the oldest known Doric temples—38 meters long, 12 meters wide, 15 columns per long side. The site reveals in visible layers the evolution of Greek sacred architecture: from the Mycenaean megaron (Megaron A, Mycenaean period) to the archaic Doric temple. The painted terracotta metopes, among Greece's oldest, are displayed in the National Museum of Athens—the small local museum preserves some fragments.

Lake Trichonida, 6 km southeast of Agrinio, is Greece's largest natural lake at 98.6 km². A Natura 2000 zone, it hosts nearly 200 bird species including grey herons, storks and pink flamingos during migration season. The road along its northern shore is pleasant, with fish-tavernas directly on the water serving freshwater fish. From Agrinio, it's 15 minutes' drive.

activities.text: Agrinio is the capital of Aetolia-Acarnania—a lively city with little inherent tourist appeal, but marking the logical circuit end before reaching Aktion Airport (Preveza, 70 km, 55 min) for connections to the Ionian Islands, or resuming toward Athens (240 km, 2h30 by highway). Lunch on Lake Trichonida's shores before choosing your direction.

places.name: Akrotiri

visits.text: Plan at least 1.5 hours for the Akrotiri archaeological site - it's Santorini's most impressive visit and one of Greece's most important. Imagine an entire city buried under ash in 1600 BC and excavated only since 1967: this is the 'Pompeii of the Aegean Sea'. Unlike Pompeii, no bodies have been found here - the inhabitants had fled, probably warned by precursor earthquakes. The city lies more than 10 metres deep under 40 metres of pumice and ash. A bioclimatic roof installed in 2012 maintains pleasant temperatures even in mid-summer: it's one of the island's few comfortable visits in July-August. Arrive by 8:30am to get ahead of the groups.

On the suspended walkways above the ruins, you'll walk over paved streets, multi-storey homes, a complex drainage system and ceramic storage jars still in place - exactly where they sat 3,600 years ago. The city traded with the entire eastern Mediterranean and had a large port. The most spectacular frescoes (the 3.90m-long naval procession, the Young Boxers) were transferred to Athens' National Archaeological Museum, but Fira's Museum of Prehistoric Thera preserves several others. If you purchased the combined ticket on day one, your entry is already paid.

Make the detour to Akrotiri Lighthouse, built in 1892 on the island's southern cliffs - it's one of the Cyclades' oldest and best-preserved. The road leading there edges the caldeira for a few kilometres with views of Thirassia opposite. You'll be almost alone: tourists don't venture here, making it a remarkably restful break after the archaeological site's bustle.

Red Beach (Kokkini Paralia) sits 1.7 km from the archaeological site - allow twenty minutes on foot from the car park. Its ocre and red volcanic rock cliffs plunge into turquoise water: the colour contrast is striking and justifies the trip even if you don't swim. Arrive early: the beach is small, wind-exposed, and crowded in high season. Pebbles increasingly replace sand due to cliff erosion.

If you have late afternoon time, get lost in the lanes of Megalochori, one of Santorini's best-preserved inland villages. Its troglodytic caves carved from volcanic rock, intact Cycladic architecture and shaded main square with cafes give you the feel of an ordinary Greek village - a rarity on such a touristy island.

activities.text: Take your time at the Akrotiri site. Unlike museum displays, in situ objects - jars, staircases, shop fronts - restore the city's human scale. The short film shown at the tour's end contextualises the site well geologically and historically. Arrive at opening time (8:30am) to avoid groups.

White Beach (Lefki Paralia), a few minutes by boat from Red Beach, is even more spectacular but less accessible. It's frequented mainly by small boats and organised excursions departing from Akrotiri port. If you have a vehicle, a rough track leads there but confirm conditions before you leave.

Hunt down a glass of Nykteri in a winery or taverna in the Akrotiri area. This Santorini white is produced by an old method: vintage happens at night (hence the name), then the wine undergoes slight controlled oxidation before bottling. It's rounder and more complex than classic dry Assyrtiko - if you've already tasted one, compare with the other to understand the island's vineyard range.

visits.text: The lighthouse of Alexandroupoli has been the city's emblem since 1880: 27 metres tall, six flights of stone steps, and its beam visible from 44 kilometres out. It's still listed under the city's former name, Dede-Agats, on nautical charts — the town was only renamed Alexandroupoli in 1920 during a visit by King Alexander of Greece, and definitively incorporated into Greece by the Treaty of Lausanne in 1923. Grab a terrace seat facing the lighthouse for your morning coffee — it's the best introduction to the city.

The Ethnological Museum of Thrace occupies a Neoclassical house dating from 1899. Its collections of embroidered costumes, jewellery, craft tools and photographic archives offer a dense snapshot of Thracian culture — a region that was successively Bulgarian, Greek and Turkish in the 20th century before reaching its present configuration. Allow an hour: it's small but thoroughly documented.

Some 28 km west of Alexandroupoli, in the village of Feres, the Monastery of Panagia Kosmosotira is worth the detour. Founded in 1152 by Isaac Comnenus, son of Emperor Alexios III, it's one of the oldest churches still standing in northern Greece. Its layout echoes Hagia Sophia in miniature, with 12th-century frescoes from the Constantinopolitan school. It became a mosque in 1357 and was returned to Orthodox worship in 1920.

activities.text: Excursion to the Evros Delta, designated a national park in 2007 and a Ramsar wetland since 1974: 188 km² of wetlands on the Turkish border where 300 of Greece's 400 bird species pass through or nest. Dalmatian pelicans, white storks, purple herons and greater flamingos are regularly spotted. Start at the Traianoupoli Information Centre (village of Loutra) to obtain your military-zone entry permits and book a boat ride on the Drana Lagoon. The trip lasts about 1 h 30.

Evening walk along the Alexandroupoli seafront: the fish tavernas line up facing the Aegean and the illuminated lighthouse provides the backdrop. Order shrimp saganaki — baked in a tomato sauce with melted feta and ouzo — it's the unmissable local speciality, far better here than in Athens because the prawns come straight from the gulf.

For your last meal, try kagianas: scrambled eggs with fresh tomato and feta, served on village bread. Technically a breakfast dish or an evening meze, though the tavernas in this region serve it at any hour. Simple, filling, and deeply rooted in everyday Thracian cooking.

days_plan.itin_title: Thrace Road Trip: Kavala, Thasos & Philippi

visits.text: Experience the extraordinary biodiversity of Kerkini Lake, one of Europe's most important wetland ecosystems and a birdwatcher's paradise. Over 300 species of birds including flamingos, pelicans, herons, and eagles make their home in this protected reserve near the Bulgarian border. The dramatic mountain backdrop and the sight of water buffalo wading through shallow waters create scenes of remarkable natural beauty.

activities.text: Take a boat excursion through the flooded forests and wetlands of Kerkini to observe wildlife at close range. Expert guides lead visitors through this remarkable ecosystem where submerged trees create surreal landscapes and thousands of birds congregate in vast colonies. The lake's status as a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance ensures protection for species that have vanished from much of Europe.

Photograph the legendary water buffalo of Kerkini, herds that have grazed these wetlands for centuries and become a symbol of the region. These magnificent animals, once essential for agricultural work, now roam freely as a protected population contributing to the ecosystem's health. Sunset excursions offer opportunities to capture these gentle giants silhouetted against the reflective waters and mountain backdrop.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece Travel Guide: Monasteries, Olympus and Ancient Kingdoms

visits.text: Patitiri, the port of Alonissos, takes its name — meaning 'wine press' — from the island's viticultural past. Rebuilt after the 1965 earthquake, it's a functional yet endearing little town with a paved waterfront, a handful of seafood tavernas, and — most importantly — the MOm Information Centre on the Mediterranean monk seal, a small but well-designed exhibition with videos and infographics about the species and the marine park's conservation work. Don't miss it if you're travelling with children or want to understand what you might see on a boat excursion.

The Chora — also known as Palia Alonissos or the Old Village — sits at 200 metres above sea level on a ridge overlooking the entire island. Abandoned after the 1965 earthquake that partly destroyed it, it was gradually restored from the 1970s onward by artists and lovers of authenticity. Today its cobbled lanes lined with whitewashed, blue-shuttered houses, cascading bougainvillea and 360° panoramas over the Aegean make it the most captivating spot on Alonissos. Come in the late afternoon for sunset from the taverna terraces — it's one of the quietest spectacles in all the Sporades.

The Alonissos National Marine Park, established in 1992, is Greece's first marine park and one of the largest in Europe, covering 251,440 hectares encompassing Alonissos, six smaller islands and 22 islets. It was created to protect the Mediterranean monk seal (Monachus monachus), the most endangered seal species on earth. The park's colony numbers between 50 and 60 individuals — roughly 10% of the 500 to 600 still alive worldwide. The core zone around the island of Piperi is strictly off-limits to visitors in order to safeguard breeding sites. The accessible areas, however, offer the chance to spot dolphins, sea turtles, and more than 300 fish species.

Kokkinokastro is a striking spot on the east coast of Alonissos: a pebble beach in shades of rust and ochre beneath cliffs of the same hue, coloured by oxidised iron in the rock. Remains of an ancient settlement and traces of Neolithic occupation have been found here. The colour of the soil makes a dramatic contrast against the blue of the sea — it's one of the most visually arresting sights on the island.

activities.text: The boat excursion through the marine park is the centrepiece of any visit to Alonissos. Departures are from Patitiri, with several options: a full-park trip (6 to 8 hours) taking in the islands of Kyra Panagia and Peristera, swimming stops in exceptionally clear water, and sometimes dolphin sightings. Spotting a monk seal remains rare and depends on luck — but even without one, the quality of the water and the beauty of the deserted islets make the trip more than worthwhile. Book ahead in July–August.

Since 2015, Alonissos has banned single-use plastic bags across the entire island — a pioneering move in Greece that reflects the eco-conscious identity of the local community. The tap water is said to be the purest in the Aegean. If you'd like to learn more about this commitment and about monk-seal conservation, the MOm organisation occasionally runs citizen-science sessions: you can sign up as a volunteer observer for a day to help survey dolphins and seals.

Tyrokafteri is a dip of crushed feta blended with spicy red peppers and olive oil — creamy, punchy, with a hint of sweetness. On Alonissos it's served as a starter with slices of toasted bread to open every taverna meal. Order it with a glass of ice-cold tsipouro and olives marinated in the island's wild herbs for an aperitif that's thoroughly local.

visits.text: Katapola, one of Amorgos's two ports, opens onto spectacular mountain scenery. Amorgos Chora, at 320 meters elevation, is linked to the port by a winding road—take the municipal bus or hike 45 minutes up the switchback trail. At the top, white windmills keep watch over the village from a wind-lashed ridge, and Venetian coats of arms still adorn old doorways.

The Panagia Hozoviotissa monastery is one of the Aegean's marvels. Founded in the 11th century by Byzantine Emperor Alexios I Komnenos to shelter a miraculous icon from Palestine, it's built literally into a cliff face 300 meters above the sea. The building stands 40 meters tall yet only 5 meters wide in places—8 floors carved into rock, a labyrinth of small rooms and narrow staircases. You'll need to climb about 300 steps to reach it from the road. The monks (3 or 4 remain) welcome visitors with Amorgian raki, loukoumi, and Greek coffee. Modest dress required—garments are loaned at the entrance. Luc Besson filmed scenes from The Big Blue here in 1987.

Agia Anna beach lies below the monastery at the foot of the cliffs. Turquoise water, pebbly bottom, access via stairs from the road. This is where the monastery's icon supposedly washed ashore mysteriously on a boat from Palestine. Open sea, sometimes with swell.

activities.text: Waterfront stroll at Katapola and provisioning: Amorgos is known for its honey, local wine, and sun-dried octopus. The harbor market is modest but authentic.
Easy hike between Chora and the windmills for panoramas over the archipelago—on clear days, the view reaches Naxos and Paros.
Swimming at Agia Anna (open sea, possible swell)—cliff diving is an island classic.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: The Lion of Amphipolis has stood beside the Strymon since its reconstruction in 1936 — a colossal 8-metre-tall statue (including its base) in Thasian marble, dated to the 4th century BC. Recent excavations suggest it crowned the Kasta burial mound. It is thought to have been erected in honour of Laomedon of Mytilene, one of Alexander the Great's generals. From the road, the lion's silhouette is clearly visible above the river — stop for the photo.

The Kasta tumulus, 4.5 km away, is the largest funerary monument ever discovered in Greece. Excavations since 2012 have revealed a 497-metre-long, 158-metre-diameter peribolos in Thasian marble, dated to the last quarter of the 4th century BC. In 2014, archaeologists uncovered inside a three-chambered Macedonian tomb with two sphinxes, two caryatids, a pebble mosaic depicting the abduction of Persephone by Hades and, in the third chamber, skeletal remains of at least five individuals. The identity of the deceased remains unknown. A protective building has been erected over the site — check opening times before visiting.

The Archaeological Museum of Amphipolis, in the village, displays finds from local excavations: marble reliefs, gold jewellery, vases, sculptures and liturgical objects testifying to the intense commercial activity of Amphipolis, founded by the Athenians in 437 BC on the Thracian site of Ennea Hodoi. The city was in turn Athenian, Spartan and then Macedonian — Philip II seized it in 357 BC.

activities.text: Stroll along the Strymon in the afternoon — the river was navigable in antiquity and Amphipolis' strategic position at its mouth explains why so many empires fought over it. The banks are quiet and shaded, with views over the surrounding Macedonian hills. The two Byzantine towers flanking the river, the better-preserved of which dates from 1367, are visible from the bridge.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

visits.text: Antiparos Cave is one of Greece's most significant: 85 meters deep, accessible via 411 steps carved into the rock. It harbors an 8-meter-tall stalagmite that's 45 million years old, considered Europe's oldest. The cave was known since antiquity—geometric and classical pottery has been found inside—and it served as a cult site for Artemis. On December 24, 1673, the Marquis de Nointel, Louis XIV's ambassador to Constantinople, celebrated Christmas Mass here before 500 people, using the great stalagmite as an altar. It closes in late afternoon—plan to go in the morning.

Antiparos's Kastro was built in 1440 by Giovanni Lorendano, Venetian lord of the island, to protect inhabitants from pirate attacks. The fortress is unique in the Cyclades: the outer walls of the inhabitants' houses form the castle's ramparts. The single entrance is through a door next to Saint Nicholas Cathedral.

Antiparos's Chora is the antithesis of Mykonos: a quiet, authentic village where local life resumes its rhythm come September. Its lanes flowering with bougainvillea, family-run tavernas, and port facing Paros offer a welcome decompression after the more tourist-heavy islands.

The islet of Despotiko, accessible in 10 minutes by boat from Agios Georgios in southern Antiparos, harbors a 7th-century BC archaic sanctuary of Apollo that's been under excavation since 1997. The islet's deserted beaches are among the Aegean's finest—crystalline waters, white sand bottom, no tourist development whatsoever.

activities.text: The Paros-Antiparos crossing from Punta takes 10 minutes by ferry, with connections every 30 minutes from 6 a.m. to midnight in summer. On the Antiparos side, rent a scooter or bike at the port to explore the island at your own pace—roads are flat and distances short.

The beaches of Soros and Glyfa, on Antiparos's southern coast, are among the island's finest: fine sand, remarkably clear turquoise waters, very few people even in high season. Bring water and provisions—there are no tavernas on-site.

In the evening at Chora, traditional tavernas serve fish caught that very day, grilled octopus, and homemade mezzes in a relaxed, family atmosphere. Prices are well below those of Paros or Mykonos for equivalent quality.

Paros's assyrtiko, a dry, mineral white wine produced on the island's granite soils, pairs perfectly with seafood and grilled fish. Paros wines carry a PDO designation and can be found in the island's groceries and tavernas.

visits.text: Arachova, at 960 meters on the slopes of Parnassus, dominates the Delphi-Athens corridor. Its grey-stone houses with red-tile roofs cling to the mountain in multiple levels. On the central square, centuries-old cafés serve local tsipouro and formaela PDO cheese from Arachova—a semi-hard goat cheese whose production has been regulated since 1996. Shops sell hand-woven carpets and woollen textiles, a living craft tradition. In winter, Arachova is a ski resort favored by Athenians—Parnassus lifts are 14 km away.

Just 1 km below Arachova on the Delphi road, the Sanctuary of Athena Pronaia is the first monument you encounter before entering the main site. The Tholos—a 4th-century BC circular temple with three Doric columns still standing on a 15-meter-diameter drum—is Delphi's most photographed image. No one knows its exact purpose. The Castalian Spring gushes right opposite: here the Pythia and pilgrims purified themselves before consulting the oracle. The spring water still flows into the two ancient basins.

Delphi was 'the navel of the world' according to ancient Greeks—Zeus released two eagles at Earth's extremes and they met here. On Parnassus's slopes, between two red cliffs of the Phaedriades looming vertically, the archaeological site stretches in terraces. Climb the Sacred Way—the path pilgrims from across the Mediterranean followed to consult the oracle. The Temple of Apollo (6th century BC, rebuilt in the 4th) is now only a rectangle of columns, but the view from its foundations—over the valley, olives and Gulf of Corinth—ranks among Greece's finest. The Pythia delivered oracles from an underground chamber in a trance, likely induced by ethylene vapors rising from a geological fissure—archaeologists have confirmed these emanations.

Above the temple, Delphi's theatre (4th century BC, 5,000 seats) is in remarkable condition. From its upper rows, you dominate the entire sanctuary with Parnassus behind—the site's best photograph. Higher still, the Pythian Games stadium (4th century BC, 7,000 spectators) is Greece's best-preserved after Epidaurus: the 177-meter track with its stone starting blocks is intact. Budget 30 additional minutes for the climb.

Delphi's Archaeological Museum is among Greece's finest. Don't miss the Charioteer of Delphi—a bronze statue from 478 BC commemorating a Pythian Games victory, extraordinarily well-preserved with enamel eyes and bronze lashes. The Sphinx of the Naxians (570 BC) and friezes from the Treasury of the Siphnians (525 BC) illustrate the scale of donations made to the sanctuary by Greek city-states. The omphalos—the marble 'navel of the world'—is here too. Plan 1h30.

activities.text: The winding Arachova-Delphi road offers spectacular views over the olive valley descending toward the Gulf of Corinth—according to some sources, Greece's largest olive grove. Dinner at Arachova's central square with its tavernas is one of the most pleasant evenings on the circuit: valley views, mountain air, and excellent local cuisine centered on roasted lamb and Parnassus cheeses.

Late afternoon, descend to Amphissa (15 min) or Itea (25 min) for dinner facing the Gulf of Corinth. Amphissa is a pleasant town with a medieval castle of Frankish lords (13th century) commanding views over the olive plain—30 minutes of climbing. Amphissa's Saturday market is among the region's most authentic.

visits.text: Areopoli — 'the city of Ares' — is the gateway to the Deep Mani. Its former name was Tsimova; it was renamed in honour of Petros Mavromichalis, the last bey of the Mani, who set out from here on 17 March 1821 at the head of 300 Maniots to liberate Kalamata — two days before the official start of the War of Independence. His statue stands in the main square. The Church of the Taxiarches (Archangels), built in the second half of the 18th century, has the tallest bell tower in the Mani — a multi-tiered structure decorated with relief carvings and zodiac symbols on its façade, a testament to the Maniots' peculiar bond with Antiquity.

The Diros Caves, 11 km from Areopoli, are explored by boat on an underground river — one of the most extraordinary experiences in the Peloponnese. A boatman rows you through 1,200 metres of flooded galleries, past stalactites skimming the water's surface, before a final 300-metre stretch on foot. The total mapped network exceeds 14,700 metres, reaching a maximum depth of 80 metres. Speleologists Ioannis and Anna Petrochilos first explored the caves in 1949; they've been open to the public since 1967. Bones of panther, hyena, lion and the largest hippopotamus bone deposit in Europe have been found inside.

Vathia is the ghost village of the Deep Mani: a collective fortress perched on a hilltop, made up of stone towers packed so tightly together they form a continuous rampart against rival clans. Vathia had up to 200 inhabitants in the 19th century; today a few houses have been restored, but the abandoned section — freely accessible — is the most compelling. Wander through the deserted lanes, push open the half-closed doors, climb the towers whose floorboards are giving way. A unique atmosphere, poised between beauty and melancholy.

Cape Tainaron (or Matapan) is the southernmost point of mainland Greece — some say of continental Europe. Leave the car at the parking area and walk 45 minutes along a coastal path between two seas: the Ionian to the west and the Aegean to the east merge here into the Mediterranean. The ancients believed this cape was one of the gates to the Underworld — a local cave was thought to lead straight to the realm of Hades. At the tip you'll find the ruins of a 4th-century BC temple of Poseidon and a small chapel built on its foundations. The white lighthouse stands 41 metres above the sea.

Kardamyli is the most beguiling village in the Outer Mani — Homer mentions it in the Iliad as one of the seven cities offered by Agamemnon to Achilles. The writer and traveller Patrick Leigh Fermor settled here in the late 1960s and wrote 'Mani', the book that introduced this peninsula to the world. Stroll through Kardamyli's old town with its stone houses, and climb up to the Church of Saint Spyridon, whose ornate bell tower rises above the village. The trail down into the Viros Gorge, below, is one of the finest hikes in the Mani.

activities.text: The tower houses of the Mani are the emblem of the peninsula: each clan built a stone tower to dominate its neighbours and defend itself during the vendettas that plagued the region for centuries. Around 800 of them still stand. Wander through the alleyways of Areopoli to spot the Kapetanakos tower and the fortified Koutrakos residence — used in 1826 to resist Ibrahim Pasha's landing at the neighbouring village. Afterwards, drive down to the tiny harbour of Limeni (5 minutes by car): tower houses plunge straight into an emerald cove.

The road through the Deep Mani is the spectacle in itself: landscapes growing ever more barren and lunar, stone towers rising from hilltops, blue sea on both sides. After Vathia, stop for lunch in Gerolimenas — one of the Mani's few harbours, whose name means 'old port'. Waterside tavernas serve fried calamari and shrimp saganaki practically at the water's edge. Continue on to Porto Kagio, the southernmost village on the peninsula, for a swim in a quiet little cove before heading back toward the cape.

The coast between Kardamyli and Stoupa is a string of small, uncrowded coves — some sandy, others pebbly, all with remarkably clear water. Stoupa, the most developed spot on the western coast of the Mani, offers sunbeds and beachfront tavernas. For something wilder, look for the coves north of Kardamyli, reachable by footpaths from the road.

Hiking the Viros Gorge from Kardamyli is a 3- to 4-hour loop through a canyon shaded by plane trees and crossed by a stream — one of the only permanent watercourses in the Mani. The trail is waymarked and accessible. If you don't have time for the full loop, the first hour to the Monastery of Saidona is enough to feel the beauty of the place.

visits.text: Argostoli has been the capital of Kefalonia since 1757 — before that, the population sheltered inside the Venetian fortress of San Giorgio as protection against pirates. The town thrived as one of the busiest ports in the Greek islands, until the earthquake of 12 August 1953 levelled it entirely. What you see today is therefore a rebuilt city, but it has held on to an authentic Mediterranean character with its palm-lined waterfront and the lively Vallianos Square, which comes alive every evening.

The De Bosset Bridge — also called the Drapano Bridge — is the longest stone bridge over the sea in the world, stretching 700 metres. Originally built in wood in 1813 by Swiss engineer Charles-Philippe de Bosset under British command, it was entirely reconstructed in stone a few decades later. Roughly halfway across, you'll come upon an obelisk erected by the British to commemorate their presence — the original plaque reading 'To the glory of the British Empire' was destroyed in 1865, one year after the island was united with Greece. The bridge marks the edge of the Koutavos Lagoon, where you can spot Caretta caretta turtles swimming freely.

The Archaeological Museum of Argostoli, opened in 1960 (architect Patroklos Karantinos), houses finds from across the island spanning prehistory to the Roman era. The Mycenaean collection is especially impressive — highlights include geometric ceramics, gold jewellery and bronzes unearthed during excavations at the island's four ancient cities: Same, Kranioi, Pronnoi and Paleis. Allow about an hour if you want to see it properly.

The Korgialenios Historical and Folklore Museum, founded in 1963 on the ground floor of the library bearing the same name, traces the history of Kefalonia from the Venetian period through to the 1953 earthquake. You'll find antique furniture, silverware, household tools, traditional costumes and close to 3,000 photographs — a tangible way to understand what the town looked like before it was completely destroyed. The entrance is through the library, which also holds 50,000 rare books and manuscripts.

The Lighthouse of Saints Theodore — locally known as Fanari ('the lighthouse') — is a neoclassical circular structure supported by 20 Doric columns, built in 1829 during the British occupation to plans drawn by J.P. Kennedy. Its lantern sits 11 metres above the Bay of Argostoli. Destroyed in the 1953 earthquake, it was faithfully rebuilt by 1960. Come here in the late afternoon: the sun sinking behind the Paliki peninsula on the far side of the bay paints the sea orange — one of the finest sunsets on the island.

The Katavothres are one of the strangest geological phenomena in the Mediterranean: seawater rushes into cracks in the limestone, vanishes underground, crosses the entire island — roughly 15 km — and resurfaces in the Bay of Sami, on the eastern coast, fourteen days later. In 1963, Austrian hydrogeologists Maurin and Zoetl poured 160 kg of fluorescent green dye (uranine) into the sinkholes — and detected it again in the underground lake at Melissani and the springs at Karavomilos. The process remains partly unexplained. As far back as 1835, a British tax collector had the idea of harnessing the current to power corn mills — the ruins of those mills are still visible on site.

activities.text: Lithostroto, the main pedestrian street of Argostoli, is where locals gather every evening for the ritual stroll. Shops, cafés, the Catholic church, the clock tower rebuilt in 1985 — everything is packed into a few hundred metres. Keep walking to Vallianos Square, the large central plaza, for a Greek coffee under the palm trees. Quiet during the day, buzzing and full of life at night.

The Koutavos Lagoon, once a mosquito-infested swamp and a hotbed of malaria, is now a bird sanctuary where migratory species nest. Caretta caretta turtles feed here regularly. You can walk the coastal path or rent an electric boat to get a closer look at the wildlife. Morning is the best time — calm water, soft light, and usually a few fishermen by the harbour.

Kreatopita is the quintessential Kefallonian pie: flaky pastry stuffed with a mixture of meats (lamb, pork, beef), rice and spices, baked until golden. It's a festive dish that families still prepare for special occasions. Track it down in the bakeries and tavernas around the market — it's the best quick lunch in town.

Robola of Kefalonia is one of Greece's most acclaimed white wines. This ancient grape variety grows on the Omala plateau at 700 metres elevation and carries a PDO label. Dry, mineral, taut, with crisp acidity — nothing like the soft, rounded whites from the rest of Greece. Ask for it at any taverna on the waterfront: it pairs perfectly with grilled fish and mezze.

days_plan.itin_title: Kefalonia & Zakynthos: Caves, Myths and Turquoise Shores

visits.text: Astypalea's Chora is a cascade of cubic white houses tumbling down a rocky hillside to the old port of Pera Gialos. At the summit looms Querini Castle, built of dark stone by the eponymous Venetian family after 1413—contrasting spectacularly with the village's white walls below. The ramparts once served as exterior walls for three-story houses built against them inside. The 1956 earthquake left ruins within, but the monumental entrance remains intact and the view from the top over the two bays forming the island's isthmus is extraordinary. Free admission.

The eight windmills aligned along the ridge of Chora, between the village and the castle, are Astypalea's symbol. Their position allowed them to capture north and south winds interchangeably according to season. One has been converted into the municipal library, another into a shop. In the evening, this is the locals' meeting point before descending to the tavernas.

Pera Gialos, the old port at Chora's foot, is Astypalea's most authentic spot: painted wooden fishing boats, tavernas with plastic tables facing the sea, fish market in the morning. This is where the island truly lives, far from the tourist animation of Chora. Stroll the quay in late afternoon when fishermen return.

The Church of Panagia Portaïtissa (18th century), just below the castle, is considered one of the Dodecanese's most beautiful: its carved and gilded wooden iconostasis was built over nine years by Agios Anthimos of Cephalonia, who was blind according to tradition. A copy of the Panagia Portaïtissa icon was brought from Mount Athos.

Pera Gialos's archaeological museum sits directly on the old port waterfront. It houses finds ranging from prehistory to the medieval era, including pieces from the Kylindra necropolis—a unique burial site where 2,700 infant skeletons interred in jars were discovered on the castle hill's flank, spanning a thousand-year period. A local cult whose significance archaeologists still debate. The museum is small but dense: allow 30 to 40 minutes.

activities.text: Astypalea has the shape of a butterfly—two islands connected by an isthmus less than 100 meters wide. Explore the eastern part (Exo Nisi) by scooter or quad: the wild beaches of Kaminakia, Vatses and Agios Konstantinos are accessible via dirt tracks, with electric-blue water and zero tourist infrastructure. Bring water and provisions.

Reserve half a day to reach Vathy by water taxi from Pera Gialos. This natural fjord on the island's east—accessible only by sea or via a poor-condition track—is one of the Dodecanese's most timeless atmospheres: a silent fishing village at the end of a narrow sea arm surrounded by arid hills. A single taverna serves the morning's catch. Boats generally depart late morning.

Have a drink in front of Chora's windmills at sunset—it's the locals' gathering spot. Illuminated Chora gives the impression the village is either climbing the mountain or suspended in the void, depending on the viewing angle. The atmosphere shifts dramatically once night falls.

Astypalea's 'Kopanisti' is a pungent, fermented soft-paste cheese served as mezze. The local 'Pouggia' are filled with almonds and mastiha (mastic resin), a perfumed variation on the specialty you may have tasted in Patmos. Ask for them in Pera Gialos pastry shops.

days_plan.itin_title: Dodecanese Circuit: Patmos, Leros & Karpathos

visits.text: The Parthenon is why you came. Built between 447 and 432 BC by architects Ictinus and Callicrates under the direction of sculptor Phidias, it took 22,000 tons of marble quarried from Mount Pentelicus, 11 miles away, to raise it. Notice that the marble contains traces of iron that have oxidized over time—at sunrise or sunset, it turns a warm honey-gold. Arrive as early as possible, right at opening: the crowds become dense after 9:30am.

The Erechtheion, built between 421 and 406 BC in the Ionic style, houses the famous Porch of the Caryatids. These six statues of young women serving as columns are copies—the originals are in the Acropolis Museum. Take time to walk around the entire building: the north facade, the most ornate, is the best preserved.

The Propylaea forms the monumental entrance to the Acropolis, designed by architect Mnesicles starting in 437 BC, in Pentelic marble. This gateway with five openings was so imposing that ancient travelers described it as rivaling the Parthenon itself. Look up at the ceiling of the central hall—the coffered panels painted in blue and gold are still partially visible.

The Temple of Athena Nike, perched to the right of the entrance, was built between 427 and 424 BC to celebrate victory over the Persians. It's the oldest Ionic temple on the Acropolis, and also the smallest: only 26 feet long. It was completely dismantled by the Ottomans in 1686 to build a cannon battery, then reconstructed stone by stone in the 19th century.

The Theatre of Dionysus, carved into the southern slope of the Acropolis in the 6th century BC, is considered the birthplace of Western theater. This is where Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Aristophanes premiered their plays before 17,000 spectators. The marble seats you see date from the Roman renovation, but the original wooden stage goes back to the great Dionysia festivals.

The Acropolis Museum, opened in 2009 and designed by Swiss architect Bernard Tschumi, houses 4,000 pieces from the sacred hill exclusively. Don't miss the third floor, oriented exactly like the Parthenon: you'll see 525 feet of friezes presented according to their original arrangement. The empty spaces, marked 'BM', remind visitors that two-thirds of the marbles are still in the British Museum. Book your ticket in advance during high season.

If the Acropolis is Athens' symbol, the Ancient Agora is its brain. It was here, on these 62 acres northwest of the sacred rock, that democracy took shape: the Bouleuterion housed the Council of 500 members, the Tholos the standing committee. Socrates walked these very porticos. Plan 1.5 to 2 hours to tour the entire site with the museum—don't skip the latter, which preserves the first voting ballots in history.

Take time to climb up to the Temple of Hephaestus, perched at the northwest corner of the Agora: it's the best-preserved Doric temple in Greece, built between 460 and 415 BC. Its survival is a lucky accident—converted into a Christian church in the 7th century, its walls and roof were preserved. The north and south friezes depict the exploits of Theseus and Heracles. Athenians themselves often call it the 'Theseion' by mistake.

Entering the Stoa of Attalos, you're walking into history's first shopping mall. Built around 150 BC by Attalus II, king of Pergamon, as thanks to the city that had welcomed him during his studies, this 380-foot portico on two levels now houses the Agora Museum. Reconstructed identically between 1953 and 1956 by the American School of Archaeology, it preserves the Doric columns on the ground floor and Ionic above. Look for the ostraka—these pottery shards on which Athenians inscribed the name of the citizen to be banished from the city.

Right next to the Roman Agora, don't miss Hadrian's Library: built in 132 AD by the Roman emperor, it was far more than a library—a vast 400-by-270-foot complex with reading rooms, gardens, and a central pool. Today only the facade with Corinthian columns is clearly visible from the street, but the site merits a brief stop. Admission is included in the combined ticket for Athens' seven archaeological sites.

The National Archaeological Museum, founded in 1829, is Greece's largest museum. If you only have time for one room, go straight to the Mycenaean collection: the gold mask known as 'Agamemnon's,' discovered by Schliemann in 1876 at Mycenae, is one of the most striking pieces of Greek antiquity. Reserve at least 2 hours if you want to cover the Cycladic collection and the bronzes.

The Panathenaic Stadium—Kallimarmaro in Greek, meaning 'of beautiful marble'—is the only stadium in the world built entirely of white marble. Founded in 330 BC by statesman Lycurgus for the great Panathenaia, it was completely rebuilt in Pentelic marble under Herodes Atticus in the 2nd century AD (capacity: 70,000 spectators). Fallen into ruin and then pillaged for its stones in the Middle Ages, it was restored to host the first modern Olympic Games in 1896. You can run on the track: it's included in the ticket. Warning: in summer, the marble gets hot—visit in the morning or late afternoon.

Lycabettus Hill rises to 908 feet, the highest point in Athens. From the summit, you have a 360° view over the Acropolis, Piraeus, and, on clear days, as far as the island of Aegina. Climb on foot from Kolonaki (30 minutes) or take the funicular from Aristippou Street. In the evening, it's the best spot in the city to watch Athens light up.

The National Garden of Athens stretches over 37 acres in the heart of the city, planted by Queen Amalia in the 19th century. It's the green oasis of Athenians—ducks, tortoises, shaded pergolas—a welcome break between visits. At the southern exit stands the Zappeion, a neoclassical palace from 1888 designed by Theophil Hansen, used for official ceremonies and exhibitions.

The Academy of Athens, the University, and the National Library form the Neoclassical Trilogy of Panepistimiou Street, all three designed by Theophil Hansen between 1859 and 1902. The Academy is the most impressive: Ionic columns, colored friezes, statues of Apollo and Athena on their pedestals. It's the iconic photo of neoclassical Athens—and often overlooked by tourists rushing off to the Acropolis.

If you're experiencing a bit of ancient site fatigue, the Benaki Museum is an excellent antidote. Set in a beautiful neoclassical mansion in Kolonaki, it traces Greek history from prehistory to the present day—Mycenaean, Byzantine, Ottoman, modern—in one fluid journey. It's also one of the best-curated museums in Athens. Go up to the rooftop cafe terrace: the view over the gardens and city is worth the trip.

After three days of ancient Greece, take a break at the Byzantine and Christian Museum—you'll discover another Greece, equally rich. Icons, mosaics, frescoes, liturgical embroideries: the collection covers fourteen centuries, from the 4th to the 15th. Some icons in gold and tempera are breathtakingly delicate. Plan about 1.5 hours to enjoy it at your own pace.

Don't miss Kerameikos: it's one of Athens' most moving sites, and one of the least crowded. This ancient potters' quarter was also the city's main necropolis, used from the 12th century BC until the Roman era. You'll find sculpted funerary stelae, marble lions, and the Sacred Way that led from here to Eleusis. The on-site museum holds the most beautiful stelae. On weekdays, you'll often have it to yourself.

Kapnikarea Church, nestled right in the middle of Ermou Street since the 11th century, is one of Athens' oldest Byzantine churches. Its central brick dome and gilded mosaics inside contrast dramatically with the commercial clamor of the street. Push the door—it's often open in the morning.

Syntagma Square is the heart of modern Athens, dominated by the Parliament housed in the former Royal Palace of 1843. In front, the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier is permanently guarded by the Evzones, the presidential guards in traditional costume—white pleated fustanella with 400 pleats, white stockings, and shoes with black pompoms. The changing of the guard happens every hour, and on Sundays at 11am it's done in full dress uniform with music.

The Museum of Cycladic Art houses one of the world's most important collections of Cycladic art, dating from 3200 to 2000 BC. These white marble figurines with streamlined forms—barely sketched silhouettes, smooth faces—fascinated Picasso and Modigliani in the 20th century. The effect is striking: you'd swear you were looking at contemporary sculptures.

activities.text: Climb up to the Acropolis right at opening (8am in summer). The low morning light gilds the Parthenon's marble and the crowds are still sparse—it's the best window of the day to photograph the site without throngs.

After the Acropolis, descend toward Plaka, the historic quarter that sprawls at the foot of the rock. Its stepped cobblestone lanes, neoclassical houses, and bougainvillea make it one of the most pleasant spots in Athens to wander in the shade. Skip the restaurants directly on the tourist thoroughfares—venture into the side streets instead.

Moussaka is the dish you must try in Athens: silky eggplant, spiced ground meat, and golden béchamel gratinéed in the oven. The best versions are found in Plaka or Monastiraki tavernas, not in terrace restaurants with laminated photo menus.
In the evening, head back up toward the Acropolis rock at sunset from the Hill of the Muses or from Filopappou—free view, no crowds, of the Parthenon turning orange. It's the show of the day.

Monastiraki, right next to the Agora, is the flea market district, full of shops of every kind and local bustle. On Fridays and Sundays, the flea market spills over into the surrounding alleys—you can find everything from Byzantine icons to old Greek coins.

Push into the Psyrri neighborhood, just north of Monastiraki. It's the haunt of Athenian artists and night owls: walls covered in graffiti, secondhand bookstores, cafes where people play cards until 3am. The neighborhood doesn't really come alive until after 8pm.

Spanakopita—spinach and feta phyllo pastry—is the ideal snack to keep you going between visits. You'll find it in all the market bakeries, still warm in the morning. Pastitsio is the main dish of the day: baked pasta, ground meat, tomato sauce and gratinéed béchamel—a pasta version of moussaka.

In the evening, try ouzo with a few mezze on a Psyrri terrace: cephalopods, olives, taramosalata. Ouzo is drunk diluted with cold water—it turns cloudy white and the anise flavor mellows. It's been the aperitif ritual of Athenians for generations.

Kolonaki is Athens' chic neighborhood, at the foot of Lycabettus. Its cafes on Kolonaki Square are perfect for a midday break and people-watching affluent Athenians—it's a local sport. Greek designer boutiques cluster in the streets around the square.

Plan your Lycabettus ascent for late afternoon, timing it to reach the summit an hour before sunset. When the light shifts over the Acropolis and the city begins to glow beneath your feet, it's one of the most powerful moments of an Athens stay. The little cafe at the top is open until nightfall.

Dolmades—vine leaves stuffed with rice, fresh herbs, and lemon juice—are often served as mezze in Kolonaki or Plaka tavernas. Greek salad is always better here than anywhere else: thick tomatoes, cucumbers, Kalamata olives, a block of feta, and dried oregano sprinkled over the top.

If you enjoy Greek wines, look for an Assyrtiko from Santorini in the evening restaurants: crisp white, very mineral, with a cutting acidity that comes from the island's volcanic soils. It's the most internationally known Greek white wine, and one of the best for pairing with seafood.

For breakfast or a midday break, look for a cafe on Syntagma Square or the adjacent alleys for a bougatsa—warm phyllo pastry filled with custard cream or fresh cheese, dusted with cinnamon and powdered sugar. It's the traditional breakfast of Thessaloniki residents that became popular throughout Greece.

Ermou Street, pedestrianized between Syntagma and Monastiraki, is Athens' main shopping artery. This is where you'll encounter locals doing their shopping—not just tourists. Kapnikarea Church cuts it in two, a symbol of two millennia layered on top of each other.

If you have the energy for an evening out, Exarcheia is Athens' most alternative neighborhood—bookstores, politicized bars, walls entirely covered in political graffiti, cafes open until dawn. It's a very different Athens from Kolonaki or Plaka, and far more authentic.

For dinner, try keftedes—meatballs flavored with mint and cumin, fried and served as mezze. Mavrodaphne from Patras, a sweet red wine from Greece, is the ideal companion to finish the meal with honey-based desserts.

visits.text: Stop at the historic sliding bridge in the city's heart: you're standing at the narrowest point of the Euripus Strait, where just 38 meters separate island from mainland. Watch the water for a few minutes—the current reverses approximately every six hours, building to speeds of 12 km/h, then achieving eight minutes of eerie stillness before reversing again. This phenomenon, governed by lunar cycles, has mystified scientists since antiquity. Aristotle, whose ancestors hailed from Chalcis, came here to study what seemed to defy the laws of nature. A wooden bridge already spanned this strait in 411 BCE; the current structure dates to 1962.

Karababa Castle towers over the city from the opposite hillside. Constructed by the Ottomans in 1686—just years after they conquered Chalcis from the Venetians—it commands a spectacular vantage over the strait, the bridge, and the terracotta rooftops below. The climb from the center takes about 20 minutes on foot. It's the finest spot for photographing the current phenomenon from above.

Agia Paraskevi Church in the old town is among Greece's rarest surviving examples of Gothic Dominican architecture. Begun around 1250, it housed one of the earliest two Dominican priories approved in the Greek province. The central vault and carved stone sculptures in the southern chapel rank among the finest examples of Italian Gothic in Greece. Don't miss the central arch above the iconostasis.

Chalcis Archaeological Museum holds regional finds: geometric and archaic ceramics, Hellenistic sculpture, and domestic objects from local excavations. The Eubean pottery collection is especially rewarding for understanding Chalcis' pivotal role in Greek colonization. In the 8th and 7th centuries BCE, Chalcidian colonists founded roughly thirty cities on the Chalcidice peninsula and several in Magna Graecia, including Cumae in Italy. Museum labels appear in both Greek and French.

Chalcis' old quarter interweaves Ottoman alleyways, Venetian traces, and a historic Jewish district. The Etz Hayyim Synagogue, one of Greece's oldest surviving Jewish places of worship, testifies to a Jewish presence reaching back to antiquity. Wander the narrow streets behind the waterfront to discover its most characteristic facades.

activities.text: Claim a terrace table facing the old bridge and observe the current shift. Locals do the same—it's free theater and genuinely hypnotic. On "chaotic" days—about 6–7 per lunar month—the current can reverse up to 14 times instead of the usual four. If you hit one of those days, you'll understand why the ancients spoke of Chalcis' "wild waters."

Taste syglino, Eubean smoked pork cured in fat—a highland specialty. Served on local bread with olives, it's the quintessential savory mezze of the region. Pair it with Eubean retsina, that resinous white wine of ancient tradition, here produced locally and superb alongside charcuterie and cheese.

As evening descends, Chalcis' waterfront comes alive. Residents gather at waterfront cafés and tavernas overlooking the strait. Order loukoumades—small fried pastries drizzled with honey and dusted with cinnamon—and watch pleasure boats navigate against the current.

visits.text: Start with a slow stroll along the Venetian harbour to the lighthouse at the jetty's end—it's the best vantage point over the colourful houses lining the basin. This lighthouse built by the Venetians in the 16th century, then rebuilt by the Egyptians in the 19th century, rises 21 metres and is one of the world's oldest lighthouses still standing. The harbour itself was built around 1320: you're walking on infrastructure that has guided merchant fleets for seven centuries. Plan to return at sunset—the atmosphere is of an altogether different order.

On the harbour itself, don't miss the Hassan Pasha mosque—also called the Janissaries' mosque or Yali Tzami. Built shortly after the Ottomans took Chania in 1645, by an Armenian architect, it's the first mosque ever erected in Crete. Its architecture is distinctive: a cubic building topped with a main dome, flanked by a portico with seven small domes. It's now used as an exhibition space. Just beside, the Venetian arsenals (Neoria)—23 vaulted stone halls built in the 16th century to shelter and repair the fleet—are among the few surviving examples of this type in the Mediterranean.

Get lost in the old town lanes beyond the harbour. In the Topanas quarter, look for the Etz Hayyim synagogue, restored in the 1990s after nearly falling to ruin. It recalls a painful chapter: Chania's Jewish community, settled here for centuries, was entirely deported by the Nazis in 1944. Most deportees perished when the ship carrying them was torpedoed by a British submarine that had mistaken it for a warship.

Just behind the harbour, the Splantzia quarter is Chania's best-preserved—tourists come less, the atmosphere is more authentic. Spot Saint Nicholas church, a former 14th-century Dominican church converted to a mosque in 1645, which today presents the unique particularity of having both an Orthodox bell-tower and the old 35-metre Ottoman minaret still standing.

Stop by the Agora, the covered market built in 1913 in the shape of a cross—an ashlar building worth seeing even if you've nothing to buy. You'll find all Cretan products gathered in one place: aged cheeses, thyme honey from the White Mountains, dittany of Crete (the island's endemic aromatic herb), olive oil and spices. It's the ideal spot to stock up before hitting the road to the gorge tomorrow.

The Samaria Gorge is among Europe's longest: 16 km of downhill hiking from the Omalos plateau (1,230 m altitude) to the coastal village of Agia Roumeli on the Libyan Sea. The national park, created in 1962 and recognized as a UNESCO biosphere reserve since 1981, shelters more than 300 plant species including 70 endemic to Crete. The walls rise to 600 m high, and the narrowest passage—the Sideroportes (Iron Gates)—narrows to 2.5 m wide. The gorge is open from May to late October, from 07:00 to 15:00 (last entry permitted). Absolutely plan your return organization: Agia Roumeli is accessible only by sea, and the last boat to Sougia or Hora Sfakion departs at 17:30.

The Omalos plateau, at around 1,100 m altitude, is the last village before departure. Xyloskalo, the gorge entrance point, is 1 km away at 1,230 m. If you're driving, plan to leave Chania around 06:00—the road takes about 75 minutes and Xyloskalo car parks are free. A few tavernas around the plateau serve breakfast before the descent.

Agia Roumeli, the village at the gorge exit, is accessible only by sea or on foot. A few tavernas and guesthouses welcome hikers who want to avoid the race against time for the last boat—a solution we recommend if you're with family or want to enjoy the beach unhurriedly. Otherwise, the evening boat departs around 17:30 for Sougia (45 min) or Hora Sfakion (80 min), from where buses rejoin Chania.

If the gorge is too strenuous or you prefer an alternative, Loutro village on the south coast is accessible only by boat from Hora Sfakion (20 min). White houses beside a turquoise cove, no cars, no road—Loutro is one of Crete's most preserved corners. A night or an afternoon on its beach is worth all the north island's tourist amenities.

activities.text: Devote the morning to exploring the harbour and old town on foot. Chania is smaller and easier to walk than Heraklion. Skrydlof Street, parallel to the harbour, is entirely dedicated to leatherwork—bags, belts, sandals made on the premises. A typical, quality purchase.

Stop by the covered Agora market before 13:00 to see it in full buzz. It's the chance to buy local products at more attractive prices than at the harbour: first-press olive oil, aromatic herbs (dittany of Crete, thyme, oregano), thyme honey and aged graviera cheeses.

For dinner, settle into a taverna on the Venetian harbour by the arsenals—the atmosphere is less touristy than facing the lighthouse. Taste kouneli stifado (rabbit braised long with baby onions, red wine, cinnamon and cloves), one of the most characteristic braised dishes of Cretan mountain cooking. Accompany it with a glass of vidiano, the aromatic local white.

Late evening, wander towards the Splantzia quarter for the more local vibe. The bars and cafés on Splantzia Square are heavily frequented by locals—the atmosphere is distinctly less touristy than at the harbour, and prices a bit more reasonable.

Leave as early as possible—before 07:00 if you can, for two reasons: avoid the crowds (up to 4,000 people daily in high season in July-August) and enjoy the coolness of the first hours in the canyon. The first 7 km cross a shaded forest of pines and cypresses and descend gradually; the last 4.5 km, at the canyon bottom, are the narrowest and most striking. Keep an eye out for kri-kri, the endemic wild goats that scale the walls—they're shy but far more visible in the morning. Bring plenty of water (springs along the route are drinkable), good hiking boots and sunscreen for the last exposed section.

On arrival at Agia Roumeli, the Libyan Sea awaits right ahead. Plunge in—you'll have earned it. The water is clear and the bottom sandy. Take time to lunch in one of the village tavernas before catching the boat.

If you're less sporty or travelling with young children, a reasonable alternative exists: leave from Agia Roumeli by boat, then hike up the gorge for 5 km to the Iron Gates—the most spectacular part—and descend again. Allow 3 hours return without the full logistics.

Rakomelo is the winter version of raki: warmed with honey and cinnamon, it's drunk like a comforting digestif. In the mountain tavernas around Omalos, you'll often be offered it at the start of the meal in the cool morning temperatures—accept without hesitation, it's the local custom.

visits.text: Corfu's old town has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2007 — a maze of narrow cobblestone lanes known as kantounia, lined with neoclassical facades in pastel hues inherited from centuries of Venetian rule. Venice held the island almost uninterrupted from 1386 to 1797, and it's the Republic that shaped this unique urban fabric, which neither the French nor the British managed to erase. Get up early: the lanes are stunning before 9am, empty and gilded by the morning light.

The Old Fortress (Palaio Frourio) sits on a rocky headland separated from the town by a sea moat — the Contrafossa — which the Venetians dug to turn the citadel into an artificial island. It successfully repelled three major Ottoman sieges: 1537, 1571 and 1716. Inside, don't miss the Church of St George, whose golden facade resembles an ancient temple — the contrast is striking. Climb to the lighthouse at the top for the best panoramic view over the sea and the town.

The New Fortress (Fortezza Nuova), built by the Venetians from 1577 on the hill overlooking the harbour, draws fewer visitors than the Old one — take advantage of that. Its underground galleries, bastions and tunnels carved into the rock form a fascinating labyrinth. The view over the harbour and the Ionian Sea from the battlements alone makes it worth the visit.

The Palace of St Michael and St George, built by the British between 1819 and 1824 in neoclassical style, now houses the Museum of Asian Art — a collection of 11,000 pieces from China, Japan and India, bequeathed by a Greek diplomat. An unexpected combination for a Greek island. The entrance from the People's Garden also offers a fine angle on the Spianada.

The Spianada Esplanade is the largest green public square in Greece. The arcaded Liston that lines its western edge was built during the French occupation, directly inspired by the Rue de Rivoli in Paris. This is where the Corfiots still play cricket, a tradition inherited from the British — one of the few places in Greece where the sport is practised. Late in the afternoon, grab a table on the terrace and watch local life unfold.

The Achilleion is one of the most romantic — and most tragic — palaces in the Mediterranean. Empress Sisi had it built between 1889 and 1891 in a neo-Pompeian style, 10 km south of the capital, on a 200,000 m² plot overlooking the Ionian Sea. She had taught herself Ancient Greek to read Homer in the original and wanted to pay tribute to Achilles, whom she saw as the embodiment of the Greek soul. After her assassination by an anarchist in 1898, Kaiser Wilhelm II bought the palace in 1907 and spent every spring there until World War I. In 1981, the first floor served as the casino set for the James Bond film For Your Eyes Only. Allow at least 1h30 for the gardens and the apartments.

The peninsula of Kanoni, 4 km south of the capital, offers Corfu's most iconic view: in the foreground, Vlacherna Monastery perched on its tiny islet connected to the shore by a causeway, and behind it, the islet of Pontikonisi ('Mouse Island') crowned with dark cypresses. Homeric legend says it's the Phaeacian ship turned to stone by Poseidon after bringing Odysseus home to Ithaca. Arrive in the late afternoon for the best light.

Paleokastritsa, 25 km to the northwest, is one of the most beautiful natural sites in the Ionian Islands: six interlocking bays, crystal-clear turquoise waters and limestone cliffs rising 30 to 50 metres. The Monastery of Panagia Theotokos, founded in 1225 on a rocky headland above the main bay, was according to tradition established after the discovery of an icon of the Virgin. The present buildings date from the 18th century. Modest dress is required to enter — cover-up garments are provided at the door. The view from the monastery gardens over the sea is among the finest on the island.

The hilltop villages of Lakones and Makrades, above Paleokastritsa, offer plunging views over the six coves of the bay — a panorama the locals call the 'Balcony of Corfu'. These olive-grove villages have kept their traditional architecture and their unpretentious tavernas. Stop for a coffee or a simple terrace lunch.

Corfu's Archaeological Museum, in town, houses the pediment of the Artemision, a 6th-century BC temple of Artemis discovered on the island: the central Gorgon stands nearly 3 metres tall and ranks among the best-preserved archaic sculptures in Greece. Allow about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Lose yourself in the Campiello, the oldest neighbourhood in the old town, north of the Old Fortress. It's a tangle of kantounia so narrow that two people can barely pass, with carved Venetian wells popping up in tiny squares. Laundry dries between buildings, bougainvillea spills over ochre walls — the atmosphere is light-years away from mass tourism.

On Orthodox Easter evening (in April), the Corfiots celebrate the Resurrection by hurling ceramic jars from their windows at 11pm sharp — a loud, festive spectacle unique to Greece. If you're not here for Easter, the religious processions around the relics of Saint Spyridon, the island's patron saint, are still well worth stopping for.

Pastitsada is Corfu's signature dish: rooster (or veal) braised in a tomato sauce with Venetian spices, served over thick macaroni. The recipe is a direct trace of four centuries of Italian influence. Try it in an old-town taverna — the spots around the Campiello are the most authentic.

Bourdeto is Corfu's other pride: fish (scorpionfish by preference) cooked in a tomato sauce fired up with hot paprika. It's a fisherman's recipe — rustic and bold. Pair it with a glass of Robola, the dry, mineral white from neighbouring Cephalonia, which cuts through the acidity of the sauce.

Book a boat trip from Paleokastritsa to explore the sea caves along the northwest coast. Small boats slip into limestone cavities where sunlight creates blue and green reflections on the water — local guides call one of them the 'Blue Light Cave'. The experience is at its best in the morning, when the sea is calm.

Take a dip in Agios Spyridon bay at Paleokastritsa — it's the most accessible beach in the resort, a mix of sand and pebbles with shallow waters. Several outfitters rent sea kayaks to explore the coves that can't be reached on foot. Expect to pay around €25–35 for a half-day kayak rental.

Sofrito is Corfu's quintessential Venetian dish: thin slices of veal pan-fried in a sauce of garlic, parsley, white wine and vinegar. The recipe dates back to 16th-century Venetian cooks. Pair it with a glass of Verdea, the dry white from Zakynthos — slightly oxidised and full-bodied, it holds its own against the bold flavours of the sauce.

Mantolato, a hard nougat made with roasted almonds and thyme honey, is Corfu's traditional confection — old-town delicatessens have stocked it since the 19th century. It's the ideal edible souvenir to bring home: it travels well and keeps for several weeks.

visits.text: The Sanctuary of Apollo clings to the slopes of Mount Parnassus at 2,000 feet—a site that functioned as an oracle for over a thousand years, from the late 7th century BC through the 4th century AD. According to tradition, this is where the omphalos stood, the stone Zeus designated as the center of the world. Don't expect a peaceful museum experience: tour buses arrive early in summer. Plan your ascent for opening time (8 AM) to catch the site while it's still quiet.

The Temple of Apollo you see today dates from the 4th century BC—actually the sixth temple built here, after an earthquake in 373 BC destroyed its predecessor. A Doric structure measuring 197 feet long by 79 feet wide, with six columns on the facade and fifteen along the sides, it housed the Pythia seated on her tripod inside the cella. This is where the maxim Socrates would make famous was carved: "Know thyself." Three columns still stand—they give you a sense of the original building's scale.

The theater, built in the 4th century BC and restored by the Romans in the 2nd century, seated 5,000 spectators during the Pythian Games, held every four years in honor of Apollo. Climb to the upper tiers: the view over the Pleistos valley, the olive groves, and the Gulf of Corinth in the distance is one of the finest at the entire site. Take time to examine the proscenium's bas-reliefs depicting the labors of Heracles before moving on.

Don't rush past this small building in Parian marble: the Athenian Treasury, built between 510 and 480 BC to celebrate the victory at Marathon, is one of the few Delphi monuments completely reconstructed—French archaeologists reassembled it stone by stone between 1903 and 1906. Its friezes depict Theseus, Heracles, and an amazonomachy. And on the exterior walls, carved into the marble, you can still see two hymns to Apollo with their musical notation—among the only sonic documents to survive from antiquity.

The stadium, at the very top of the site, is the least visited of Delphi's monuments—yet one of the best preserved in Greece. Built in the 5th century BC then remodeled in the 2nd century AD by the benefactor Herodes Atticus, who replaced the earthen stands with stone seating, it measures 584 feet long with capacity for about 6,500 to 7,000 spectators. The runners' starting line is still visible in the ground. Allow 15 minutes to climb from the theater.

The Castalian Spring lies in the ravine between the two Phaedriades rocks, just before entering the sanctuary. Every pilgrim had to purify themselves here before consulting the oracle—washing their hands, or their entire head for first-time visitors. Today it's a basin carved from rock, with water still flowing. The setting, between cliffs rising over 650 feet, has a stark beauty.

Below the main sanctuary, the Sanctuary of Athena Pronaia is often less visited—wrongly so. Its most famous monument is the tholos, a 4th-century BC circular building whose exact function remains unknown. Three Doric columns and three interior Corinthian columns have been re-erected—they give the site an almost photogenic appearance. Allow 20 to 30 minutes to descend from the main site entrance.

The gymnasium at Delphi, midway between the tholos and the main sanctuary, was the training ground for athletes competing in the Pythian Games. You can still make out the running track (xystos) and the cold bath basins. A quiet spot, often deserted, that you may have entirely to yourself.

Arachova (3,150 feet elevation) deserves at least an hour's stop. Its steep alleyways open onto craft shops—hand-woven rugs, cheeses, local liqueurs—and viewpoints over the valley. In winter it's a lively little ski resort; in summer the village is considerably calmer. Look for the overlook above the main church for the best view of the surroundings.

Make the detour to Hosios Loukas, 23 miles from Delphi—it's an hour's drive well worth the displacement. This monastery founded in 945 by the hermit Saint Luke of Steiris has been UNESCO-listed since 1990 and preserves 11th-century gold-ground mosaics among the best preserved in the entire Byzantine world. The great katholikon, built in 1011, introduced an architectural plan—octagonal inscribed cross—that would become standard throughout Byzantine Greece. Inside, look up at the dome: Christ Pantocrator gazes down at you. The site is open mornings; check hours before departing as midday closures often surprise visitors.

activities.text: Take your time walking slowly along the Sacred Way from the sanctuary entrance to the temple. On either side once stood dozens of statues and treasuries offered by Greek city-states competing for prestige. The empty pedestals you see today once bore bronzes long since melted down. This gradual ascent with the oracle at the end creates an atmosphere that even the crowds can't quite dispel.

The Delphi Archaeological Museum deserves half a day on its own. Its centerpiece is the Charioteer (Hêniokhos): a 6-foot bronze discovered in 1896 beneath the Sacred Way, buried during the earthquake of 373 BC. Cast around 470-466 BC to commemorate the tyrant of Gela's victory at the Pythian Games, it's one of only five major classical bronzes preserved worldwide. Look closely at the eyes: irises in brown chalcedony, pupils in obsidian, tear ducts in pink coral—a staggering level of detail for a work 2,500 years old.

In late afternoon, from the heights of the site, the panorama over Parnassus (8,061 feet according to official sources) and the valley of olive trees descending toward the Gulf of Corinth is at its finest. The oblique light brings out the nuances of the temple's honey-colored stone. If you're staying in Delphi or Arachova, you can walk down via the trail along the ravine—allow 30 minutes and wear sturdy shoes.

Formaela from Arachova, a PDO sheep's milk cheese, can be purchased in shops in the neighboring village. Grill it and drizzle with local honey—a combination you won't find everywhere in Greece. Arachova is 6 miles from Delphi via a road offering some beautiful valley viewpoints.

Kleftiko lamb—slow-cooked in a paper parcel with herbs and garlic—is the mountain specialty of the region. You'll find it in most tavernas around Delphi and Arachova. The name comes from the "klephtes," brigands who cooked meat underground so the smoke wouldn't give them away.
Agiorgitiko, a red wine from Nemea (Peloponnese), pairs beautifully with the region's mountain cuisine. Full-bodied with soft tannins, it drinks young. You'll find it by the carafe in local tavernas.

The walk through the Pleistos Gorge, the river flowing below Delphi, is accessible from the village and takes 1.5 to 2 hours round trip. The trail winds through thousand-year-old olive groves—some trees are said to be over a millennium old. The quiet contrasts with the bustle of the archaeological site. Hiking shoes recommended; the path can be slippery.

In the village of Arachova, step into the craft shops lining the main street. Hand-woven rugs and kilims are the village specialty going back several generations—red and black geometric patterns you'll only see here. Local cheeses (formaela, fresh goat cheese) are sold directly from producers.

On the route to Hosios Loukas from Delphi, stop in the village of Distomo—known for the 1944 tragedy when the SS massacred 218 residents in reprisal. A sober memorial marks the village center. This context adds another dimension to the monastery visit that follows, a place of cultural continuity and resistance for a thousand years.
Hilopites—square dried pasta typical of the region—are eaten in soup or baked with cheese. A simple, hearty dish you'll find in mountain tavernas around Arachova.

Feta saganaki, pan-fried in olive oil and served with honey and sesame seeds, is a classic appetizer in the region's tavernas. The sweet-salty balance works particularly well with a glass of dry white wine.

Tsipouro from Thessaly, a grape marc eau-de-vie sometimes lightly anise-flavored, is served ice-cold with mezze—olives, cheese, mountain charcuterie. It's the aperitif drink throughout central Greece. Don't confuse it with ouzo: tsipouro is more concentrated and without added sugar.
