visits.text: On the way to Dikella, make a detour through Kavala — a port city built into a hillside descending to the Aegean, facing the island of Thasos. Its old Ottoman quarter of Panagia is best explored on foot from the 270-metre Kamares aqueduct, built in the 16th century under Suleiman the Magnificent. Climb to the citadel for the sea view. In the quarter's streets, look for the birthplace of Mehmet Ali, an Ottoman officer born here in the 18th century and considered the founder of modern Egypt.

Twenty-five minutes north of Kavala, the site of Philippi is an essential stop. Founded in 360 BC by settlers from Thasos, fortified by Philip II of Macedon in 356, it witnessed the Battle of Philippi in 42 BC where Octavian (the future Augustus) and Mark Antony crushed Brutus and Cassius. In AD 50, the Apostle Paul founded the first Christian church in Europe here. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, the grounds include an ancient theatre still used in summer, early Christian basilicas, a Roman forum and a museum. Allow at least 2 hours.

Dikella beach, on the northern Aegean coast, is a long stretch of golden sand and smooth pebbles with good facilities — sunbeds, showers, a gentle slope into the water — popular with families from the Alexandroupoli area. It's quiet, rarely visited by foreign tourists and makes for a peaceful end to the circuit before the return via Kavala (airport less than 30 km away).

activities.text: Spend the afternoon at Dikella beach — the water is clean and shallow, the Aegean sunset is serene and crowd-free. Mesi beach, a few kilometres along, is even wilder if you're looking for total solitude.

Finish with a seafood dinner at one of the waterfront restaurants in the nearby village of Agia Paraskevi — octopus, Thracian mussels and grilled fish have been served here for decades by local fishing families. The cuisine of Eastern Macedonia blends Greek, Turkish and Balkan influences in a way quite different from the islands.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

visits.text: Osios Loukas Monastery is one of the finest throughout the Mediterranean—and one of the least known to Anglophone travellers. Perched on the flank of Mount Helicon in a cirque of olive and almond trees, it was founded by the hermit Luke of Steiris, born in 896 in Delphi and died in 953 after prophesying the reconquest of Crete from the Arabs (a prediction fulfilled eight years after his death). UNESCO-listed since 1990, its katholikon (completed around 1011) houses the most complete 11th-century gold-ground mosaics in Greece: Pantocrator, Crucifixion, Descent to Hades—an iconographic program of rare coherence and beauty. The polychrome marble, jasper and porphyry pavements are equally exceptional. In the crypt, the saint's relics remain in place. Budget at least 1h30.

Between Thebes and Osios Loukas, a stop in Levadia (Livadia) merits a detour. The town organizes around the spring of Trophonios, an ancient oracle contemporary with Delphi that made consultants enter a terrifying cave—the 2nd-century AD Greek traveller Pausanias left a vivid account. The springs still gush at the foot of a rocky cliff, now developed as a pleasant promenade with water mills and cafés. Levadia's medieval castle (14th century) overlooks the town from the hillside—30 minutes of climbing.

activities.text: In the monastery shop, monks sell their honey, olive oil and preserves. These are exceptional-quality products at very reasonable prices. Before leaving the site, grab a coffee on the small terrace facing the valley—the view of olives descending toward the sea, the silence and mountain air combine into something you won't find anywhere else on this circuit.

visits.text: Marvel at the spectacular waterfalls of Edessa, the ancient capital of Macedonia where waters cascade dramatically through the heart of the city. The largest waterfall plunges 70 meters into a verdant ravine, accessible via atmospheric pathways behind the thundering curtain of water. This unique urban waterfall setting has made Edessa one of northern Greece's most picturesque towns.

Wander through Edessa's charming old quarter where traditional stone houses and Byzantine churches recall the city's role as Macedonia's first capital. The archaeological museum displays artifacts from this historically significant region that shaped Greek civilization. Riverside cafés offer perfect vantage points to appreciate the natural beauty that has attracted visitors for millennia.

activities.text: Soak in the therapeutic waters of Pozar Thermal Springs, naturally heated pools nestled in a forested gorge of exceptional beauty. These mineral-rich hot springs have drawn visitors seeking their healing properties since ancient times, with water temperatures reaching 37°C year-round. The combination of steaming pools surrounded by lush vegetation and dramatic cliffs creates a truly rejuvenating experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece Travel Guide: Monasteries, Olympus and Ancient Kingdoms
Greek Macedonia: Mount Olympus, Ancient Capitals & Byzantine Treasures

visits.text: Edipsos' thermal springs have risen from the depths of the earth for 20,000 years, welling up from 3,000 meters below. More than 80 springs dot the municipality—the largest concentration anywhere in Greece. The waters reach 70 to 84°C at the Damaria source, cooling to 40 to 50°C at the beach springs. The ancients knew these waters well: Plutarch, Aristotle, and Strabo all documented them as the "Springs of Heracles." Legend has it that after each of his Twelve Labors, Heracles himself soaked in these waters to recover. The Roman general Sulla, plagued by a mysterious skin condition, came here and departed healed—a victory that cemented Edipsos' reputation among the empire's elite.

Along the central beach, Roman bathing pools carved directly from the rock still feed from natural 38°C springs. You can wade in for free—search the rocky outcrops north of the waterfront for telltale steam rising from the stones. It's an extraordinary sensation: sulfur-tinged air, steam rising, the Aegean Sea just meters away. History hovers here too: Aristotle, whose family hailed from Chalcis, reportedly visited to study the region. Later arrivals included Winston Churchill, Greta Garbo, Maria Callas, and Omar Sharif, all seeking the waters' restorative magic.

Modern thermal facilities offer medically supervised treatments: thermal baths, hydrotherapy, mineral mud wraps, and programs for rheumatoid conditions. Recognized therapeutic applications include arthritis, sciatica, and circulatory disorders. If you're not taking a formal cure, you can access outdoor thermal pools on a day-visit basis—though advance booking is wise during peak season, as capacity is limited.

Edipsos' waterfront stretches several hundred meters along the North Eubean gulf. Stroll it at late afternoon when the light rakes across the continental mountains across the strait—the panorama is particularly stunning at that golden hour. Tavernas, cafés, and small shops selling local produce line the promenade, where the pace slows to that of a quiet Greek seaside town.

activities.text: Thermal bathing or a structured cure: both are available. A medical cure lasting several days is recommended for rheumatism, arthritis, or sciatica—it requires a local doctor's prescription. For a shorter visit, stick to the outdoor pools or natural beach basins. Water temperatures of 28–34°C are considered optimal for therapeutic benefit.

Half-day excursion to northern Eubean villages: Istiaia and the small port of Oreoi, 20 km to the east. Istiaia is an animated market town; Oreoi has a picturesque jetty and waterfront tavernas beloved by locals. Plan roughly an hour's drive from Edipsos along the coast.

Taste kolokythokeftedes—herb-stuffed zucchini fritters fried golden in olive oil. It's the emblematic vegetarian mezze of the Greek summer, served everywhere along the waterfront. Pair them with local Savatiano, a crisp dry white produced on the island, which cuts beautifully through the richness of fried vegetables and grilled fish.

In the evening, order galaktoboureko: creamed semolina wrapped in crispy phyllo leaves, drenched in warm lemon syrup. It's the ultimate comfort dessert of this region—more tender than baklava, less aggressively sweet, often served still warm.

days_plan.itin_title: Eubea Explored: Ancient Spas and Hidden Villages

visits.text: Elafonisi isn't really an island: in calm weather, you can walk across the shallow lagoon separating it from shore—the water comes up to your knees. The pink sand is real but nuanced: it's not uniformly pink, but sprinkled with pink grains where the waves deposit crushed shells of micro-organisms called Foraminifera. Local legend says the sand was stained by the blood of an Ottoman massacre on April 18, 1824: several hundred Cretans, including many women and children, were killed here while waiting for a rescue boat. The area is protected by the Natura 2000 network—it's strictly forbidden to take sand, shells, or plants; inspections are real. Arrive before 9:30 a.m.: afterward, tourist buses pour in hundreds of people.

The Monastery of Chrissoskalitissa stands on a coastal rock 10 minutes by car from Elafonisi. Its name means 'Golden Step'—legend says a 90-step staircase carved into the rock has one golden step, visible only to those who haven't sinned. The view over the west coast from the terraces is magnificent, and the visit takes only 20 minutes.

activities.text: Walk across the lagoon from the main beach (barely 100 meters, warm and shallow water) and follow the island's northern shore: the beach on the north side is less crowded, the waters slightly deeper and turquoise. Plan to stay until the tourist buses leave around 4-5 p.m.—the site then regains a remarkable calm, ideal for sunset.

If you still have energy in the late afternoon, Falasarna is 30 km north via a beautiful coastal road. This long white sand beach exposed to the wind is ideal for kitesurfing, and the sunsets are spectacular. Two distinct beaches flank a rocky cape with ancient ruins.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Southern Crete: Gorges, Deserted Beaches & End-of-the-World Villages

visits.text: Spinalonga is the most history-laden place in all of eastern Crete. The Venetians began fortifying this 34-hectare rocky islet in 1579 to protect Mirabello Gulf—the fortress was so solid that even after the Ottomans took Crete in 1669, it held out another 46 years before falling in 1715. Entry is through a long dark tunnel that the lepers deported here between 1903 and 1957 nicknamed 'Dante's Gate.' For over 50 years, between 300 and 400 patients lived here as a community—with a baker, a barber, a priest. The last inhabitant, a priest, didn't leave the island until 1962. Allow 90 minutes to explore it fully. From Plaka (5-minute crossing) or from Elounda (20 minutes), boats depart regularly in high season.

Between Elounda and the peninsula, a causeway runs along the ancient city of Olous, submerged since antiquity. At low tide, you can see the mosaics of a 4th-century early Christian basilica with the naked eye—just beneath the surface, among the rocks. Olous was one of the great cities of Minoan and Classical Crete, mentioned as early as 3000 BC. With a mask and snorkel, the walls of the ancient acropolis are visible in shallow water. Swimming in this small lagoon sheltered from the wind is among the most pleasant in the region.

Elounda is the most comfortable base for this day. The resort is upscale—international resorts have set up here since the 1970s—but the waterfront of the original fishing village, the old salt pans between the town and the Olous peninsula, and the fish tavernas at the harbor are worth a detour beyond the hotels. The view of Spinalonga from the lagoon beaches is one of the finest in the region, especially late afternoon when golden light falls on the fortress walls.

Plaka is a fishing village 5 kilometers north of Elounda, facing Spinalonga. It's the closest departure point for the island (5-minute crossing) and also a remarkably preserved village with tavernas on the harbor, colorful boats, and nets drying in the sun. The grilled catch of the day here is impeccably fresh—Spinalonga's residents used to get their supplies here. Reserve a table for sunset over the island.

activities.text: The boat trip to Spinalonga is unmissable—but organize it early in the morning. By 10 AM in summer, groups land in waves and the vaulted passages become packed. Leaving Plaka at 8:30 or 9 AM, you stand a good chance of exploring the fortress in relative calm. Bring water and a hat: there's virtually no shade on the island.

After Spinalonga, swim in the lagoon between the Olous peninsula and Elounda: calm, shallow, and remarkably clear waters. With a mask and snorkel, you can search for the underwater ruins of the ancient city in shallow depths. The sandy beach on the way to the peninsula is one of the least crowded in the area.

For breakfast or brunch, order sfougato at a harbor taverna in Elounda: a thick omelet of zucchini with sheep's cheese and Cretan herbs. Several places serve it in the morning. In season, also look for clusters of octopus drying in the sun outside the Plaka tavernas—order it grilled, it's one of the best things you'll eat on this coast. A white Cretan Assyrtiko, mineral and dry, pairs perfectly with both.

days_plan.itin_title: Crete Road Trip: Agios Nikolaos, Elounda & Spinalonga
Grand Tour of Crete: Minoan Palaces, Gorges and Eastern Coast

visits.text: Potamos beach at Epanomi, 35 km south of Thessaloniki, is a pointed sandy spit jutting into the Thermaic Gulf — its shape recalls Croatia's Zlatni Rat, but with far fewer people. On a clear day, Mount Olympus is visible across the gulf. What makes the site truly unusual is the wreck of a cargo ship that ran aground more than 50 years ago just off the shore: locals say Saint Antonios himself sank the vessel to protect the adjacent 550-hectare wetland, a refuge for migratory birds. Arrive in the morning for the calm before the Thessaloniki families descend.

The Epanomi wetland stretches across 550 hectares between dunes and marshes — a sanctuary for migratory birds, with herons, passing flamingos and numerous aquatic species. A trail runs along the lagoon from the east car park of the beach. The site is especially active in spring and autumn during the migrations.

activities.text: The wreck offshore is reachable by swimming or snorkelling from the beach — the water is clear and shallow here, making it an excellent snorkelling spot suitable for all levels. The west side of the bay, exposed to the wind, is popular with kitesurfers.

The Epanomi area is known for its vineyards: the soil and microclimate produce characterful dry whites. A short drive through the surroundings will take you past several wineries open for visits in summer. End the day at sunset facing the gulf — Olympus turns orange.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

visits.text: The Theatre of Epidaurus is arguably the most spectacular ancient theatre still standing anywhere in the world. Built in the 4th or early 3rd century BC on the slopes of Mount Kynortion, it originally had 34 rows of seats holding 6,200 spectators — the Romans later added 21 more rows, bringing capacity to nearly 14,000. Walk to the centre of the orchestra (the circular stone slab on the ground) and drop a coin: you'll hear it ring from the very last row, 60 metres away. Researchers at the Georgia Institute of Technology found that the limestone seats act as a natural acoustic filter, dampening low-frequency background noise while letting voices travel unamplified. Arrive in the morning to beat the tour groups that flood in after 10 am.

Five hundred metres from the theatre, the Sanctuary of Asklepios served for six centuries as the foremost healing centre in the Greek world. Pilgrims journeyed from across the Mediterranean to practise incubation — sleeping in the abaton (a sacred dormitory) in the hope that the god of medicine would appear in their dreams and cure them. Excavations uncovered votive stelae describing healings — hernias, blindness, paralysis — that would make a modern physician smile, yet they attest to a belief system that endured for a millennium. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988.

The Tholos of Epidaurus, dated to around 360 BC, is the most enigmatic structure in the sanctuary. This circular building featured a triple ring of columns — 6 Doric on the outside, 14 Corinthian on the inside — and its purpose remains hotly debated: healing rituals, a home for the sacred serpents of Asklepios, or an initiation chamber? Don't bother looking for the answer in the museum — it's not there either. Take the time instead to admire the beautifully preserved Corinthian capitals.

The Epidaurus Museum, at the foot of the site, displays sculptures and architectural fragments unearthed during the excavations. Don't miss the scale models that reconstruct the sanctuary — they give a vivid sense of how vast this complex was at its Hellenistic peak, far larger than what you've just walked through. Allow about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Test the theatre's acoustics for yourself: stand on the central slab of the orchestra and whisper a sentence. Ask a companion to climb to the top row — they'll hear you clearly. If you're on your own, drop a coin on the stone. Guides have been doing this demonstration for centuries, and it never fails to astonish. The Epidaurus Festival stages ancient tragedies every Friday and Saturday evening from June through September — if you're visiting in season, book ahead.

Swimming in the coves between Paleá Epídavros and Nea Epidavros, just a few kilometres from the site: small pebble beaches hemmed in by pine trees, with crystal-clear water. The village of Paleá Epídavros has a fishing harbour lined with waterfront seafood tavernas — perfect for dinner.

Try gemista: tomatoes and peppers stuffed with rice, fresh herbs and sometimes minced meat, slow-baked in the oven. It's a summer staple in Peloponnese tavernas and far more flavourful than the name suggests. Pair it with a glass of assyrtiko — a dry, mineral white with a crisp acidity that cuts beautifully through the stuffed vegetables.

days_plan.itin_title: Peloponnese Road Trip: Mycenae, Epidaurus & Olympia

visits.text: Eretria's archaeological site spreads beneath the modern town—you'll circulate among open-air ruins with a free map distributed at the museum. At the site's heart stands the Temple of Apollo Daphnephoros: its earliest phases date to the late 8th century BCE, with the Doric version of 520–490 BCE destroyed by the Persians during the 490 BCE sack. Foundation stones and sculptured pediment fragments remain. Bring water and a hat in summer—the site offers no shade.

The House of the Mosaics, dated to the 4th century BCE, stands among Greece's finest preserved Hellenistic dwellings. Its floors showcase pebble mosaics depicting mythological scenes—Nereids astride sea creatures, griffins, panthers. Check before visiting: travelers report it's occasionally closed early despite posted hours.

Eretria Archaeological Museum, founded in 1960 and expanded by the Swiss School of Archaeology in Greece (excavating since 1964), displays remarkable finds. Don't miss the sculptured group of Theseus and Antiope, attributed to the Athenian sculptor Antenor (circa 510 BCE), and a black-figure funerary amphora dated 560 BCE depicting Heracles battling Centaurs. Labels appear in Greek and French.

Eretria's ancient theater, with earliest phases from the 5th and 4th centuries BCE, is remarkable for an underground tunnel still visible—actors vanished through it for stage effects, a rarity of ancient theatrical machinery. Climb to the acropolis in the north for a commanding overview of the site and the coastline.

The Persians destroyed Eretria in 490 BCE—Darius sought vengeance for the city's aid to the Ionian Revolt. The male population was slaughtered; women and children were enslaved and deported to Persia. The city rebuilt swiftly, sent 600 hoplites to the Battle of Plataea in 479 BCE, flourished in the 4th century, then fell to the Romans in 198 BCE and was finally abandoned after 87 BCE. Marshland reclaimed it until 1824, when refugees from Psara resettled the site.

activities.text: Swimming along Eretria and Amarynthos beaches—a neighboring village to the east celebrated for seafood tavernas and grilled shellfish spreads. Waters here are calm and shallow, ideal for children. If you're motoring about, Amarynthos merits a detour for dinner: locals prefer its fish freshness to Eretria.

Sample fava: a creamy yellow-pea purée dressed with excellent olive oil, capers, and lemon juice. It's among Greece's finest yet most underappreciated mezzes. Pair it with local Savatiano white—a dry, fruity Eubean varietal that harmonizes beautifully with legumes.
Pasteli—sesame bars bound with thick honey—sells in waterfront shops and groceries. It's one of Greece's oldest candies, energy-dense and nourishing, perfect to carry for tomorrow's hiking.

days_plan.itin_title: Eubea Explored: Ancient Spas and Hidden Villages

places.name: Fira

visits.text: Fira, Santorini's capital, clings 250 meters above the caldera. The caldera itself resulted from one of the largest eruptions in human history: around 1600 BC, the island's central volcano exploded and collapsed, creating a 12 km diameter crater now filled with sea. The multicolored cliffs - black basalt, red, white pumice - tell the stratigraphy of this catastrophe. If you're afraid of heights, walk carefully along the edges.

The Museum of Prehistoric Thera in Fira displays objects discovered during Akrotiri excavations - a Minoan city buried under volcanic ash around 1600 BC, comparable to Pompeii. The original frescoes from the city are among the finest in the Aegean Antiquity: fishing scenes, navigation, young men in action. Allow an hour for the visit.

Oia is Greece's most photographed village - its cave houses carved into the cliff, cobalt domes and flowered alleyways make it a worldwide postcard. Arrive early morning, before 9am, to see it almost deserted. Oia's Byzantine castle is the best viewpoint for sunset - plan to arrive 90 minutes early to find a spot.

The Akrotiri archaeological site is often compared to Pompeii: a Middle Bronze Age Minoan city, buried under meters of volcanic pumice around 1600 BC and preserved intact. Unlike Pompeii, no bodies were found here - the inhabitants had time to flee. The two-story alleyways, oil jars still in place, and house structures are protected by a massive covered structure. Allow 90 minutes.

Kamari beach, on the east coast, is Santorini's main black sand beach. This volcanic sand heats up very quickly in summer - wear shoes to walk to the water. The water is clear and quite deep quickly. The beach is long, well-equipped, with seaside tavernas.

Red Beach, west of Akrotiri, is one of Greece's most spectacular beaches: an enclosed cove between bright red lava cliffs plunging into deep blue sea. The footpath from the parking lot takes 10 to 15 minutes on rocky terrain - wear good shoes. The beach is small and packed in summer, arrive early.

Pyrgos is Santorini's best-preserved medieval village, perched on the island's highest point at 350 meters. Built around a 13th-century Venetian kastro, it has retained its labyrinthine alleyways and crenellated houses. 360° view over the entire island from the chapel at the summit. Much less crowded than Oia.

Megalochori is one of Santorini's most authentic villages, built in the 17th century by merchants and shipowners. Its main square with vaulted arches and small church is one of the island's most beautiful. Much less crowded than Fira or Oia, this is where you'll see the island as it was before mass tourism.

Imerovigli, between Fira and Oia, is nicknamed 'the balcony of the Aegean Sea'. Skaros Rock, a former 13th-century Venetian fortress partially collapsed into the sea during the 1956 earthquake, offers a 270° view over the Kameni islets and caldera - less crowded than Oia castle for sunset.

activities.text: In the evening, settle in Oia for sunset. It's the most famous in the Cyclades: in July-August, hundreds of people crowd the cliff edge. Go anyway, but arrive early and position yourself slightly back from the castle for an unobstructed view without fighting for space.

Santorini fava, yellow split pea purée grown on the island since antiquity and protected by PDO, has incomparable sweetness. Tomatokeftedes, fritters made from cherry tomatoes concentrated by volcanic sun, are worth trying as meze. You'll find both in all Fira and Oia tavernas.

Santorini assyrtiko, a white grape variety cultivated in ground-level baskets to protect it from wind, produces a mineral, dry and slightly saline wine - unique in the world. The vines have thrived on the island's volcanic soils for centuries. Several estates offer tastings with caldera views.

The Fira-Oia hike along the caldera is one of the most beautiful in the Mediterranean: 10.5 km on the cliff edge, between 250 and 350 meters height, with constant views over the 12 km diameter crater and volcanic islets at the center. Allow 3h30 to 4h with breaks. Start early morning in summer - midday heat on the volcanic stones is crushing. The trail passes through Firostefani and Imerovigli before reaching Oia.

Amoudi Bay, at the foot of Oia's cliffs accessible by 300 steps, is the island's most authentic spot for fish. A few fishing tavernas serve dried octopus, sea urchins and grilled fish at the water's edge. You can also dive from the rocks directly into the sea. Avoid going between noon and 3pm - the return climb in the heat is exhausting.

Boat excursion to Nea Kameni volcano in the caldera's center: shuttles depart from Fira's old port (accessible by 580 steps or funicular). On the islet, a trail climbs to the volcanic cone's summit at 150 meters - sulfurous fumaroles remind you the volcano remains active, its last eruption dating from 1950. You can then swim in neighboring Palaia Kameni's natural hot springs, slightly ferruginous and sulfurous water.

Wine tasting in Santorini is a unique experience: vines are cultivated in ground-level basket coils to protect from wind, a millennial technique unique here. The mineral, dry assyrtiko white is most renowned - but vinsanto, sweet wine from sun-dried grapes, is also worth experiencing. Several estates offer tastings with caldera views.

visits.text: Folegandros is an island of character—lashed by the meltemi, with 300-meter cliffs plunging into intense blue sea, and a Chora perched on a vertiginous ledge that long served as a place of political exile (until 1969). The main village is entirely pedestrian: three beautiful squares lined up and shaded by plane trees, stone-paved lanes, an inhabited Venetian kastro, and the persistent feeling that time passes differently here. From Chora, the switchback trail (20-30 min) leads to the Panagia church on its promontory—the view over the Aegean and neighboring islands at sunset ranks among the Cyclades' most spectacular.

Agkali (or Angali) beach on the south coast is accessible by car or bus from Chora. Pebble bottom sheltered by a cliff, deep blue water, two tavernas under the tamarisks. No natural shade except at the cliff base—bring an umbrella. From there, a 30-minute coastal trail leads to the wild cove of Agios Nikolaos, a favorite among island regulars.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling at Agkali: the rocky seabed shelters varied marine life. The cove is sheltered enough to be pleasant even in moderate wind.
Sunset from anchorage or Panagia church—the golden light on Folegandros's white cliffs is a spectacle that regulars return for every summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Frangokastello Fortress was built by the Venetians between 1371 and 1374 to keep order in the rebellious Sfakia region. The locals never accepted it and renamed it 'Frangokastello'—the Castle of the Franks—as a sign of contempt. The rectangular plan with four corner towers still bears the coat of arms of the Lion of St. Mark above the entrance. In 1828, during the Greek War of Independence, 385 Greek fighters led by Hatzimichalis Dalianis were massacred here by the Ottomans. A persistent legend says that every year in May, on the battle's anniversary, the shadows of the dead soldiers—the Drosoulites, literally 'dew men'—appear in the morning mist and parade before the castle. The phenomenon has been documented but never fully explained.

activities.text: Imbros Gorge is the southern coast's most accessible hiking route: the descent from the Askifou Plateau to the village of Komitades takes 2 to 3 hours. The walls reach up to fifty meters high in places and the vegetation is dense—a striking contrast to the coastal aridity. The hike is relatively easy, doable year-round.

Frangokastello Beach stretches about 500 meters of fine sand in front of the castle walls—one of the south's most family-friendly beaches, with a very gradual descent into calm, shallow water. 600 meters to the west, Orthi Ammos Beach is wilder, with a rocky bottom suitable for snorkeling. You can combine both in one afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Southern Crete: Gorges, Deserted Beaches & End-of-the-World Villages

places.name: Galaxidi

visits.text: Galaxidi appears around a coastal bend amid olives and Gulf of Corinth like a 19th-century print. This port of 1,700 inhabitants possesses two natural harbors and a collection of neoclassical mansions built by ship captains who made their fortunes in the Mediterranean. In the 18th-19th centuries, Galaxidi commanded a fleet of 350 vessels trading as far as Marseille and the Black Sea—maritime power disproportionate for such a small town. The 1821 War of Independence largely ended this prosperity. Today its cobbled alleys and softly-colored façades make it one of mainland Greece's most photogenic villages.

Galaxidi's Maritime Museum occupies a renovated former shipyard—excellent for understanding the port's history through well-organized galleries. The collection includes 19th-century sailing-ship paintings (Greece's largest selection), navigation instruments, travel journals and marine charts. Outside the museum, the PeraPanta coastal promenade extends to the statue of a sailor's wife waving her handkerchief toward the sea—a melancholic image that encapsulates the village's history.

activities.text: The Gulf of Corinth coves at Galaxidi's foot are calm and the water is clean. The coastal walk between the village's two ports (Hiriolakas and the main harbor) skirts small pebble beaches with views of the Byzantine islets opposite. If you're here on Saturday, Amphissa's market 30 km away merits an early-morning detour before heading down to Galaxidi.

map_keywords: Port of Galaxidi Gulf of Corinth

visits.text: Vathia is the ghost village of the deep Mani: a collective fortress perched on a hilltop, made up of stone tower houses packed so tightly together they form a continuous rampart against rival clans. Vathia had as many as 200 inhabitants in the 19th century; today a few houses have been restored, but it's the abandoned section that is most haunting. The atmosphere is unique — beauty and melancholy intertwined, and not a tourist in sight outside July and August.

Cape Tenaro is the southernmost point of mainland Greece. From the car park, walk 45 minutes along a coastal trail running between two seas: the Ionian Sea to the west and the Aegean to the east merge here. The ancients regarded this cape as one of the gates to the Underworld. You'll find the ruins of a 4th-century BC Temple of Poseidon and a small chapel built on its foundations. The white lighthouse stands 41 metres above the sea.

activities.text: The Diros Caves, 11 km from Areopoli on the road to Gerolimenas, are explored by rowboat along an underground river — one of the most extraordinary experiences in the Peloponnese. The boatman guides you through 1,200 metres of flooded galleries, past stalactites that almost graze the water's surface. The total mapped network stretches beyond 14,700 metres. Book ahead in summer — tickets are limited.

days_plan.itin_title: Greece Grand Tour: 21 Days from North to South

visits.text: Gytheio is the capital of the eastern Mani and the former naval port of Sparta — the Spartans, who scorned the sea, needed a maritime outlet and established their arsenal here. Pastel-coloured houses with wrought-iron balconies tumble down toward the quay, fishing boats bob in the harbour, and tavernas open at dawn for the returning fishermen. It's one of the most authentic small towns in the Peloponnese. Stroll the waterfront in the late afternoon when the light turns golden.

The islet of Marathonisi (formerly Cranae) is connected to the harbour by a narrow causeway. According to Greek mythology, this is where Paris and Helen spent their first night of love before fleeing to Troy — the spark that ignited the longest war of antiquity. On the island stands the Tzanetakis Tower, built in 1829, which now houses the Historical and Ethnological Museum of the Mani. The octagonal lighthouse at the far end offers a fine view across the gulf.

The museum inside the town hall (1890), on the waterfront, displays archaeological finds from Gytheio and the wider Mani: relief ossuaries, carved inscriptions, pottery and Roman-period sculpture. It's a useful complement to understand the commercial history of a town that was one of the main trading hubs of the free Laconians under the Roman Empire.

Make a detour along the Mani coastal road to the village of Vathia — one of the best-preserved settlements on the peninsula. Its medieval stone towers, packed tightly against one another and now almost entirely deserted, tell the story of centuries of blood feuds between rival Maniot clans who built upwards for protection against their own neighbours. The road there is arid, rocky and spectacularly stripped back to bare essentials.

activities.text: Mavrovouni beach stretches for several kilometres north of Gytheio — a long strip of fine sand facing the Laconian Gulf with the peaks of Taygetus as a backdrop. The water is calm in the morning, perfect for a family swim. By afternoon, the sea breeze picks up and turns it into a decent kite-surfing spot. This is the locals' beach: simple, barely equipped, genuinely Greek.

Synglino is the Mani's iconic cured meat — pork smoked over myrtle or orange wood and preserved in lard. Before the age of refrigeration, every Maniot household kept a supply. The harbour tavernas serve it as a starter, often alongside the orange-spiced sausage. Kakavia, a Mediterranean fish soup with saffron and lemon, is the other must-try for an evening on the quayside.

days_plan.itin_title: Laconia Road Trip: Sparta, Mystras & Monemvasia

visits.text: Plan to arrive at Knossos as soon as it opens at 08:00—groups arrive from 10:00 onwards and the site becomes stifling in summer. This Minoan palace built around 1900 BC and remodelled after an earthquake around 1700 BC covered more than 20,000 m² with some 1,300 rooms: administrative offices, sanctuaries, workshops and royal quarters, all linked by labyrinthine corridors that probably inspired the Minotaur myth. Pay close attention: the frescoes visible on site are copies; the originals are in the archaeological museum. British archaeologist Arthur Evans, who excavated the site from 1900 to 1935, reconstructed the palace in reinforced concrete—a highly controversial decision that explains the bright, somewhat artificial colours of certain zones.

Visit the Heraklion archaeological museum before or after Knossos—a combined ticket lets you enter both at a better rate. This museum, founded in 1883 and housed in a building renovated until 2014, is Greece's second museum by visitor numbers and holds the world's largest collection of Minoan objects. Don't miss the four masterpieces: the Snake Goddess in faience (around 1600 BC) discovered at Knossos, the black steatite rhyton shaped like a bull's head with rock-crystal eyes, the fresco of the Bull-Leaping, and above all the Phaistos Disc—a fired clay disc discovered in 1908, covered with 242 signs in a spiral belonging to a script still undeciphered to this day.

The Koules fortress dominates the old harbour entrance, at the end of the jetty. Built by the Venetians between 1523 and 1540 on a site fortified since the 9th century, this massive Rocca al Mare was constructed to counter the Ottoman threat in the time of Suleiman the Magnificent. Its outer walls reach 9 metres thick. It reopened in 2016 with a permanent exhibition—including pieces brought up from the seabed by Cousteau's team in 1976. Climb to the terrace: the view over the harbour and city is Heraklion's best.

Push open the door of Saint Titus Cathedral in the city centre. This church dedicated to Crete's patron saint—Titus, disciple of Saint Paul—has a turbulent history: built in the Byzantine era, converted to a mosque under the Ottomans, it was returned to Christian worship in 1925. It houses a relic of Saint Titus, brought to Venice when the Ottomans took Crete in 1669 and returned to Heraklion in 1966.

Just beside the cathedral stands the Loggia, a Venetian edifice rebuilt in 1628 that served as a meeting place for Candia's nobility (Heraklion's former name). Now the town hall, this building with its Renaissance porticos perfectly illustrates Venice's imprint on the city. Spend a few minutes walking around it—the façade is one of the old town's most elegant.

If Knossos gave you Minoan Crete, the historical museum gives you everything else: Byzantium, Venice, the Ottomans and 20th-century Crete up to the Second World War. Don't miss the room devoted to El Greco—Dominikos Theotokopoulos was Cretan, born around 1541 in Fodele, and the only work by him kept in Crete is here. Also look for the reconstruction of Nikos Kazantzakis's library, the author of Zorba the Greek, born in Heraklion in 1883—an intimate and moving room.

Make the detour to the Martinengo bastion, in the Venetian walls encircling the city—these ramparts rank among the best-preserved in the entire Mediterranean. You'll find Nikos Kazantzakis's tomb, who himself chose his epitaph: 'I hope for nothing, I fear nothing, I am free'. You can climb certain sections of ramparts; the view over the city from the walkway is worth the effort.

Take a tour down 1866 Street, the liveliest covered market in the city, where locals actually do their shopping. You'll find aged graviera, fresh mizithra, mountain thyme honey, aromatic herbs and marinated olives—all at better prices than at the harbour. Plan to pass through in the morning: some stalls close around 14:00, especially at weekends.

If you have a car, the excursion to Phaistos palace (78 km south-west) is truly worth the journey. Crete's second Minoan palace after Knossos, Phaistos is far less crowded and altogether more authentic: no concrete reconstruction here, just open-air ruins on a plateau overlooking the Messara plain with Mount Psiloritis in the background (2,456 m, Crete's highest peak). This is where the famous Phaistos Disc was found, now in Heraklion museum.

activities.text: Leave for Knossos first thing in the morning—allow 90 minutes to 2 hours on site. Absolutely take an audioguide or local guide: without explanations, it's very difficult to distinguish original ruins from Evans's concrete reconstructions, and the site loses much of its meaning. A guide will also stop you from missing the Royal Road, considered Europe's oldest paved road.

Follow up with the archaeological museum—ideally after Knossos, to see the originals where you'd seen only copies. Allow 90 minutes minimum. The practical tip: buy the combined museum + Knossos ticket, valid 3 days—you save on both entries and avoid the queue at the museum ticket office.

In the evening, head back towards the old Venetian harbour for golden hour. The tavernas lining the harbour serve seafood and grilled fish facing the illuminated fortress. If you arrive before sunset, walk to the jetty's end: the view over the city and the hinterland mountains is striking.

Take advantage of your Cretan evening to taste dakos, the cornerstone of local cuisine: a barley rusk soaked in olive oil, topped with fresh tomato and feta, scattered with oregano. Accompany it with a glass of raki (or tsikoudia), the local eau-de-vie distilled from grape pomace—served chilled and often offered at meal's end.

The morning in Heraklion is ideal for leisurely exploring the old town streets around Venizelou Square, with its Morosini fountain from 1628—locals simply call this spot 'Lions Square' because of the carvings on the old basin. The surrounding cafés are perfect for a Greek coffee while watching the morning buzz before hitting the road.

If you make the excursion to Phaistos, plan to stop en route at Agia Triada, just 3 km from the palace. This small Minoan site has yielded some of the finest works in Heraklion museum, including the Agia Triada Sarcophagus and the Harvester Vase. Allow 30 to 45 minutes on site.

Cretan vineyards merit a stop: indigenous grape varieties like vidiano (aromatic white with notes of citrus and white flowers) or kotsifali (fruity, gentle red) are found practically only on the island. If you pass through the Messara plain on the way to Phaistos, several cellars offer tastings—ask at your hotel for current addresses.

In Heraklion, taste kalitsounia, little parcels of fine pastry filled with mizithra and aromatic herbs. They're found in both sweet versions—with honey and cinnamon—and savoury. Town-centre bakeries generally have them at all hours.

visits.text: Ierapetra holds a title few cities can claim: Europe's southernmost, nicknamed 'the bride of the Libyan Sea.' Its position at the southern tip of Crete's isthmus has made it a strategic crossroads since antiquity—the ancient city of Hierapytna was already famous in the 3rd century BC for its piratical tendencies, before being destroyed by the Romans in 67 BC. Climb up to Kales Fortress, rebuilt in 1626, for a view over the fishing harbor—allow 30 minutes. And if you want a local oddity: Ierapetra is also one of Crete's wealthiest towns, thanks to the 13 square kilometers of greenhouses supplying all of Greece with off-season vegetables.

Twelve kilometers offshore from Ierapetra, the island of Chrissi—'the golden island'—is a protected, uninhabited nature reserve, except for a few seasonal caretakers. It's famous for its forest of two-hundred-year-old Lebanon cedars, the only one in Europe, and for its beaches of white sand and intensely turquoise waters. Facing an influx of 200,000 visitors per year, the Greek government imposed access restrictions in summer starting in 2022—prefer May-June or September-October to find it nearly empty.

Wander down into the alleyways of Kato Mera, Ierapetra's old town: a medieval quarter of narrow lanes and dead-ends leading to the fishing harbor. You'll find the Ottoman mosque, a former Christian church converted under Turkish rule, and 'Napoleon's House'—an 18th-century dwelling where, according to local legend passed down since 1798, Bonaparte spent one night during his Egyptian expedition. Undocumented, but locals will tell you the story with infectious pride.

The Archaeological Museum of Ierapetra is small, calm, and often empty—the anti-Heraklion. You'll find collections spanning the Minoan era through the Greco-Roman period: amphorae, decorated sarcophagi, statuettes, and ceramics testifying to Hierapytna's importance in antiquity. Ideal for an hour mid-day, before the Chrissi excursion or after.

If you have time late in the day, the coastal road between Ierapetra and Makrygialos offers several wild and uncrowded beaches—particularly around Xerokambos and Agia Fotia. This is also the route you'll take if heading to Sitia airport (52 kilometers, about 1 hour) or Heraklion for an international flight (minimum 1 hour 45 minutes—leave mid-morning so you're not pressed for time).

activities.text: The boat trip to Chrissi Island is the day's highlight in Ierapetra. Book the morning crossing (departure around 10 AM) to maximize your time on the island. Once ashore, follow the marked trails to the cedar forest—the scent of resin beneath the centuries-old trees, with the turquoise sea glittering between the trunks, is striking. Belegrina Beach, covered in a carpet of bleached fossil shells, is a unique natural curiosity. Bring your lunch: the island's only taverna is rudimentary.

Walking exploration of the old town: start from Kales Fortress, then into the alleyways of Kato Mera, the Ottoman mosque, Napoleon's House, the 17th-century Venetian fountain, and the lanes leading to the waterfront. The waterfront promenade runs along a long beach of gray-gold sand to the modern neighborhoods—the perfect spot for a final Cretan swim late in the day.

For dinner, settle into a waterfront taverna and order chtapodi krasato: octopus simmered slowly in red wine with onions and herbs until it's tender—the house specialty at most local establishments. Finish with loukoumades, fried doughnuts served hot and drizzled with honey, dusted with cinnamon. Tsikoudia (Cretan raki) usually arrives unbidden as an end-of-evening digestif.

Return to the airport: if you're departing by flight from Sitia (small terminal with seasonal flights to Athens and some European destinations), count on 52 kilometers and about 1 hour from Ierapetra. For an international flight from Heraklion, allow minimum 1 hour 45 minutes—leave mid-morning so you're not pressed for time. The faster northern coastal road runs through Agios Nikolaos.

days_plan.itin_title: Crete Road Trip: Agios Nikolaos, Elounda & Spinalonga
Grand Tour of Crete: Minoan Palaces, Gorges and Eastern Coast
Grand Circuit of Crete

visits.text: Take the boat from the pier facing the fortress—the crossing takes five minutes and departures are regular. Nisi Island, in the middle of Lake Pamvotis, is smaller than it appears but concentrates several Byzantine monasteries and a gripping history: it was in the monastery of Agios Panteleimon that Ali Pasha took refuge in 1822, hunted by Ottoman troops who had withdrawn the sultan's confidence. He was executed there, at age 82, and his decapitated body buried within the grounds. His head was sent on a silver platter to Constantinople. Walking around the island takes 20 minutes.

Ioannina's fortress occupies a rocky promontory jutting into Lake Pamvotis—three sides out of four are surrounded by water, making it a naturally defensible position. Justinian had laid the first stones in the 6th century; Ali Pasha almost entirely rebuilt it in the early 19th century, completing the work in 1815. He made it what you see today. Wander the alleyways of the old town inside the walls: at the entrance to the plane tree where the Turks tortured rebels, cafes and silversmiths' shops coexist with the ruins of Ali Pasha's palace. One of the rare still-inhabited Ottoman town centers in Greece.

In the fortress's northeast citadel, don't miss the Aslan Pasha Mosque—built in 1618 on the site of a Byzantine church after expulsion of the Christian population. It now houses the Municipal Ethnographic Museum, documenting the cohabitation of Ioannina's three communities: Christian, Muslim, and Jewish. What few people know is that the city was long one of the great Sephardic Jewish communities of the Balkans. The costumes, silver jewelry, and weapons on display give a concrete picture of what the city was before the 20th century.

Three miles from the center, Perama Cave is worth the detour. Discovered by chance in 1940 by villagers seeking shelter during the war, it extends over more than half a mile of galleries covering 159,000 square feet. Archaeologists found the skeleton of a cave bear inside—the species is extinct, no worries. The visit is done in guided groups (Greek and English), lasts about 45 minutes. Bring a sweater: even in midsummer, 59°F inside surprises. Last entry around 5:30 PM.

Head down to the alleyways of the old bazaar, around the fortress, and step into a goldsmith's workshop if you find one open. Ioannina was nicknamed "city of silversmiths"—an Epirote tradition dating back at least to the Ottoman period and whose techniques a few artisans still perpetuate old-school. The silverwork museum, inside the citadel, displays worked silver pieces from the Ottoman period that give an idea of the region's craftsmanship. The bazaar street is also the best place to buy homemade pita to go.

activities.text: Late in the day, settle on a terrace by Lake Pamvotis and order local fish: smoked eel, carp, trout—fishermen supply the lakefront tavernas directly. Ioannina is also known for its frogs, exported as far as France. Stay until sunset: the lake reflects the mountains turning violet, and the fortress silhouette stands out against the water.

The walk along the fortress ramparts lets you follow the walls lakeside with a spectacular view over Pamvotis and the mountains beyond. In the evening, the reflection of the city lights on the water is striking. The cafe set up in the Its Kale acropolis, up high, is a good place to pause.

Bakaliaros skordalia, fried cod served with garlic-potato purée, is the signature fried dish of Greek popular cuisine. You'll find it in traditional tavernas around the fortress, often on Fridays—a legacy of the Orthodox calendar.

Ioannina's pita is different from industrial flaky pitas: a thick homemade phyllo pastry pie, filled with spinach and local cheeses, baked in a wood-fired oven. Every taverna has its recipe. This is THE specialty eaten standing or seated by the lake, wrapped in paper.

Epirus tsipouro differs from Thessaly's by the total absence of anise: it's dry, almost neutral, and gives center stage to the mezze. In the kafeneion (traditional cafes) around the fortress, you'll be served a small carafe accompanied by a few slices of cheese and mountain charcuterie—a local institution.

visits.text: Ios has a dual identity you'll grasp on arrival: Chora perched on its hill is an authentic Cycladic maze of whitewashed lanes adorned with bougainvillea and 12 windmills—but come nightfall, the bars take over and the island has been one of the Aegean's party hotspots for over 40 years. If you're seeking tranquility, climb to Chora early and head down before noon. For history buffs, the prehistoric site of Skarkos, 10 minutes from Chora, is one of the best-preserved Minoan settlements in the Aegean. The hill said to hold Homer's tomb is accessible via trails north of the island.

Mylopotas is Ios's largest beach—a sand crescent over 1 km long facing due west, Blue Flag and crystalline waters. It's also the liveliest: beach clubs, wakeboarding, diving, evening concerts. The eastern part of the beach is calmer than the western end where nightlife concentrates. The bay offers well-held anchorage on sandy bottom, but very busy in July–August.

activities.text: Swimming and paddleboarding in Mylopotas bay—SUP and canoe rental on the beach. Waters stay shallow for 5 to 10 meters from shore.
Anchorage in the bay (good holding on sand, high seasonal crowding). In season, better to drop anchor arriving early morning.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Welcome to the Lesser Cyclades. Iraklia counts just 150 permanent residents across 18 km² and electricity only arrived in 1982. The port of Agios Georgios is endearing: the entire village descends to greet the ferry, collect deliveries, and chat under the tamarisks. Livadi beach, 1.5 km from the port, is the island's finest—a long crescent of blonde sand lapped by turquoise water where you'll find space even in high August. No umbrellas for rent on site, bring your own.

activities.text: Snorkeling and swimming in Livadi bay: the crystalline waters let you spot fish, sea urchins, and if you're lucky, sea turtles that regularly visit the bay.

Hike to the village of Panagia (Chora), 4 km from the port via a marked trail. This inland hamlet is the island's traditional capital, with its Cycladic stone houses and whitewashed church. Those with energy left can continue to the Agios Ioannis cave, considered the largest in the Cyclades with 2 km of stalactite-adorned galleries—bring a flashlight and closed-toe shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

places.name: Ithaque

visits.text: Vathy, the capital of Ithaca, is a small town of 1,700 people tucked at the end of a horseshoe-shaped gulf — one of the best natural anchorages in the Ionian Sea. Most of the Venetian-style buildings were reconstructed faithfully after the 1953 earthquake; it is said that one of El Greco's earliest works hangs in the local cathedral. Stroll along the harbour, stop at one of the quayside tavernas — the atmosphere is gentle, worlds away from mass tourism.

The Archaeological Museum of Vathy holds over 1,000 objects unearthed on the island, covering the Mycenaean through Roman periods. The star exhibit is a pottery fragment inscribed with the name 'Odysseus,' found during excavations at the Alalkomenes site. There is also a small bronze statuette that some scholars identify as a portrait of Odysseus himself, along with Mycenaean votive tripods. Admission is free. The museum is closed on Tuesdays.

Aetos Hill, 4 km from Vathy, rises to 380 metres. From the church of Ai Giorgios, a steep trail climbs for 20 minutes to the ruins of the ancient city of Alalkomenes — this is where Heinrich Schliemann dug in 1868 and 1878, convinced he had found the palace of Odysseus. Modern archaeologists are more cautious: the fortifications date from the 7th century BC, well after the events described in the Odyssey. But the view from the top — spanning both coasts of the island and Kefalonia across the water — is worth the effort on its own.

Kathara Monastery sits perched at roughly 600 metres elevation on the mountain that Homer called Neriton. Founded in the late 17th century, it offers a full 360° panorama over Vathy, the southern half of the island and Kefalonia beyond. Even if the monastery itself doesn't interest you, make the drive for the view — it ranks among the most beautiful in all the Ionian Islands.

The Cave of the Nymphs, locally known as Marmarospilia, lies a few kilometres north of Vathy. According to the Odyssey, this is where Odysseus hid the gifts from the Phaeacians upon his return home. The cave contains stalactites and a small ancient altar, with a natural opening in the ceiling — the smoke from sacrificial offerings supposedly rose through it. It is not always open to visitors due to ongoing excavations — check before making the trip.

activities.text: Ithaca is tailor-made for gentle hiking. Signposted trails connect the villages through olive groves and scrubland of kermes oak. The island is home to just 3,000 people, and the paths are often deserted — one of the most authentic experiences in the Ionians. Ask in Vathy for details on the marked routes.

Revithokeftedes are chickpea fritters laced with fresh herbs, typical of the Ionian Islands. In Ithaca, they're usually served with yoghurt or skordalia (garlic mash). A simple, delicious vegetarian starter — one of the best things to order in a harbourside taverna in Vathy.

The village of Stavros, in northern Ithaca, deserves a stop: the central square features a bust of Odysseus and a model of his 'idealised' palace, and a small archaeological museum displays finds from excavations led by the British School of Archaeology in the 1930s. Not to be confused with the museum in Vathy — this one focuses on the northern part of the island and its ties to the Odyssey.

days_plan.itin_title: Kefalonia & Zakynthos: Caves, Myths and Turquoise Shores

map_keywords: Cave of the Nymphs Vathy Ithaca

visits.text: Perched on the hill above Kalamata, the Castle of Villehardouin is well worth the climb. Built after 1205 by Geoffrey I of Villehardouin on the site of an ancient acropolis, it served as the family stronghold for two centuries before passing to the Venetians and then the Ottomans. The ruins themselves are modest, but the sweeping panorama over the Gulf of Messenia and the snow-capped peaks of Mount Taygetus rising behind the city makes the visit unforgettable. Allow roughly an hour for a leisurely stroll around the fortifications.

The Archaeological Museum of Messenia, housed in the former municipal market hall, is a pleasant surprise with its clean, modern design. The collection traces the region's story from prehistory to the Byzantine era through finds from Kalamata and across Messenia — pottery, mosaics, gold jewellery including a Mycenaean ring dating to the 16th century BC. English information panels are well done throughout. Allow about 45 minutes.

The church of Agioi Apostoloi (Holy Apostles) hides a founding moment of modern Greece: it was inside this 13th-century Byzantine church that the oath of the Greek Revolution was sworn on 23 March 1821 — two days before the date recorded in official history books. The facade is unassuming, but step inside and you'll find an intact Byzantine chancel and an atmosphere of rare stillness in such a lively city.

Kalamata's seafront stretches 2.5 km along the Messenian Gulf. Walk it in the late afternoon when the low-angled light gilds the neoclassical facades — you'll spot German, Italian and Venetian influences in the old town's architecture. Every Wednesday and Saturday morning, one of the finest open-air markets in the region sets up along this same waterfront: piles of vegetables, wedges of local cheese, dried figs and glistening olives everywhere you look.

activities.text: Kalamata olives have held PDO status since the 1990s — their elongated shape, fleshy texture and deep purple colour set them apart from every other variety in the Mediterranean. The region also produces a superb extra-virgin olive oil from the Koroneiki cultivar, prized for its high antioxidant content. Ask at the specialty shops in the market or at nearby estates for a tasting — chances are you'll leave with a few bottles tucked into your luggage.

Kalamata's open-air market (Wednesday and Saturday mornings, along the waterfront) is one of the richest food markets in the Peloponnese. Messenia ranks among the most fertile territories in all of Greece: expect dried figs, Taygetus honey, pasto (locally smoked pork) and, of course, the famous black olives. Arrive early for the best selection and the liveliest atmosphere.

Try Kalamata's pasteli — a dense, crunchy bar of toasted sesame seeds and honey that Greeks have been making since antiquity. Local bakeries also serve imam baildi, aubergines stuffed with tomato and onion, slowly baked in olive oil until meltingly soft, alongside the classic marinated olives paired with crumbled feta. Find a waterfront taverna for dinner and the evening is set.

Laconia Road Trip: Sparta, Mystras & Monemvasia

visits.text: When you arrive in Kalambaka and see these sandstone pillars rising from the plain for the first time, you understand why monks chose this place in the 11th century. These rocks formed 60 million years ago from river sediments, uplifted then sculpted by erosion into pinnacles over 1,300 feet tall. At their peak in the 15th century, 24 monasteries occupied the summits—built without roads, with materials hoisted in nets by pulley. Six remain active today. A 10.5-mile circuit from town allows you to drive the loop with stops; count on a full day.

Start with the Great Meteoron—the largest, oldest, most visited, and for good reason. Founded in the 14th century by Saint Athanasios the Meteorite, its main church was rebuilt between 1545 and 1582 on the Mount Athos model, with frescoes from the Cretan school covering the walls floor to ceiling. Plan on good shoes: you'll climb 300 steps carved into rock from the parking lot. Arrive at opening (9 AM in season) or late afternoon—between 11 AM and 2 PM tour buses unload hundreds of people simultaneously. Don't leave without glancing at the museum of manuscripts and icons from the 14th through 20th centuries.

Varlaam Monastery, perched at 1,224 feet on its pinnacle, is Meteora's second-largest. Founded in the 16th century by the brothers Theophanes and Nectarios Apsarades, it preserves in its katholikon frescoes painted in 1548 by Frangos Katelanos, one of the great masters of post-Byzantine painting. Don't miss the old winch system and the net—still on display—that were used to hoist people and provisions before the stairs were built. And look at the enormous 16th-century wooden barrel: 3,170-gallon capacity.

Roussanou is a convent of nuns founded in the 16th century, built on a narrow rock and accessible by bridge. Its Cretan school frescoes, still vividly colored, are among Meteora's finest. The garden extending to the cliff edge is well-tended and peaceful. This is often the least crowded monastery on the loop—visit it last, when the tour buses have departed.

Agios Nikolaos Anapafsas is the first monastery on the route from Kastraki, the smallest and often least visited. Founded in the late 14th century, it's famous for its frescoes created in 1527 by Theophanes the Cretan, considered the founder of the Cretan school of post-Byzantine painting. The interior space is tiny but the paintings are of exceptional quality. The terrace offers one of the best views of the rock pillars.

Agios Stefanos is the most accessible of the Meteora monasteries—no stairs, just a bridge spanning the gap between parking and entrance. Founded in the 14th century, it was converted to a female convent in 1961 after suffering significant damage during World War II. The nuns have carefully restored the spaces. The view from the terrace across the Thessaly plain is clear and impressive.

Agia Triada (Holy Trinity) is the most difficult monastery to access: 147 steps carved into rock to reach a narrow pinnacle where gardens advance right to the void's edge. This is where the 1981 James Bond film "For Your Eyes Only" was shot—filming equipment had to be helicoptered in. The view from the gardens is vertiginous. If you only do one difficult monastery, make it this one.

The Cathedral of the Dormition of the Virgin in Kalambaka is often ignored by visitors rushing straight to the monasteries. Built on the foundations of an ancient temple, it preserves 12th-century Byzantine frescoes and a marble pulpit placed in the center of the nave—a unique arrangement in Greece. Allow 30 minutes and it's a fine introduction to the region's religious art.

The Kalambaka Natural History Museum presents Meteora's geology and explains how these sandstone pillars formed over 60 million years from river deposits then were sculpted by erosion. A clear, well-illustrated exhibition, particularly useful if you have children. Allow 30 to 45 minutes.

The Pineios Gorge, east of Kalambaka, is accessible by car and offers preserved nature very different from Meteora's mineral landscape. The river flows between wooded walls, with several old Ottoman stone bridges along the route. Traditional villages hide in perpendicular valleys. A half-day excursion to get off the classic circuits.

activities.text: The Meteora scenic road runs 10.5 miles and connects all the monasteries with spectacular viewpoints between each stop. Certain overlooks let you see several monasteries simultaneously silhouetted against the sky. In late afternoon, the golden light on the sandstone rocks is photographically remarkable. The village of Kastraki, at the foot of the rocks, is calmer and more photogenic than Kalambaka for overnight stays.

Hiking trails connect some monasteries via ancient paths—those the monks used before roads were built. One of the most interesting descends from the Great Meteoron to Kastraki (about 1 hour). Warning: some passages run along cliffs without guardrails. Maps available at the Kalambaka tourist office.

In Kalambaka and Kastraki, artisans offer icon painting workshops following traditional Byzantine technique—gold background, natural pigments, egg-based binder (tempera). These workshops often work on commission but sometimes accept visitors. Ask at your hotel or the tourist office.

Kontosouvli, pork skewers spit-roasted over charcoal, is Thessaly's meat specialty. You'll find it in Kalambaka tavernas, often served with country bread and Greek salad. A simple dish but particularly successful in establishments that build their own fire.

Boureki is a savory pie with zucchini, potatoes, and feta, enclosed in crispy phyllo pastry. It varies from house to house depending on cheese proportions and herbs used. In Thessaly, it's often made thicker than in Crete, where it's also popular.

Xinomavro from Naoussa, a red wine from northern Greece, is tannic and full-bodied with aromas of black cherry and spice. It pairs well with roasted meats and aged cheeses. Producers in the Naoussa region often age their vintages several years in oak barrels.

Sunrise at Meteora is an experience unto itself. Several viewpoints below the monasteries (notably near Kastraki) let you watch the rocks emerge from valley mist with golden, low-angle light. Plan to be there before 7 AM—in summer the sun rises early. It's the ideal moment for photography and absolute silence.

The Kalambaka market (Saturday mornings) offers products from the monasteries and neighboring farms: mountain honey, dried herbs from the Pindus, olive oil, green walnut preserve. Shops around the main square also sell icons and handcrafted items.

If you have a car, leave early morning to explore the 31-mile loop departing Kastraki toward the mountain villages of Agios Dimitrios, Gavros, and Vlachava at 3,280 feet elevation. The ridge road offers a panorama over Meteora from above—a completely different perspective from below. Along the way: beekeepers, goat herds, cave chapels, and vineyards from Liakou estate.

Galaktoboureko is central Greece's signature pastry: vanilla-scented semolina custard, enclosed in phyllo pastry and drenched in lemon syrup. You'll find it in all Kalambaka pastry shops, often still warm late morning.

Stifado, a beef stew with pearl onions, red wine, cinnamon and clove, simmers for hours and is served in an earthenware pot. It's a seasonal dish that Kalambaka tavernas often cook in the evening. Order it ahead if you want to be sure to find it.

Retsina Malamatina is a resinated white wine produced in Attica following a tradition dating back to antiquity—originally pine resin was added to amphorae to preserve them. Highly distinctive, it rarely pleases on first tasting but grows on you with rich Greek cuisine. Regional tavernas always serve it by the carafe.
