visits.text: Sithonia, Halkidiki's middle peninsula, is a different world from Kassandra. Here, pine forests plunge straight into emerald coves, winding roads reveal a new bay at every turn and the fishing villages haven't been swallowed by hotels. Drive the loop starting from the east coast — the views across to Mount Athos are breathtaking.

Karydi Beach, near Vourvourou, regularly ranks among the finest beaches in northern Greece: powdery white sand, turquoise water barely knee-deep, set against pines and sculpted rocks. It's a wild beach — no sunbeds, no umbrellas — so bring everything you need. Arrive before 10am in summer; it fills quickly.

Kavourotripes, also known as Orange Beach, is a chain of small coves separated by white cliffs tinged with orange. The water shifts from azure blue to emerald green. It's the most famous organised beach on Sithonia — sunbeds are available at the main cove, the others remain wild. If you're worried about the parking situation (it's chaos in August), arrive early.

Neos Marmaras is Sithonia's liveliest port — dozens of waterfront tavernas, excursion boats and a marina. It's a practical base for exploring the peninsula, but also the place to enjoy fresh seafood in the evening under the harbour lights.

activities.text: Rent a boat (no licence required) from Neos Marmaras or Vourvourou to explore the coves that can't be reached by road — particularly the beaches of Platanitsi and Armenistis, which look even wilder from the sea. Organised excursions from these ports also offer views of the Mount Athos coastline opposite.

Porto Koufo, at Sithonia's southern tip, is an almost enclosed natural harbour: two sheer cliffs leave a 300-metre opening onto the sea. The effect is dramatic and the waters inside are perfectly still. Stop here for a lunch of grilled fish at a waterside taverna.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

visits.text: Stop at the Corinth Canal, linking the Ionian Sea to the Aegean Sea, impressive work dug in the 19th century.
Discovery of the village of Kalavrita, marked by the history of World War II and surrounded by picturesque mountains.

activities.text: Visit to the Kalavrita Memorial and its Orthodox church with the frozen clock.

days_plan.itin_title: Peloponnese, Greece Road Trip: Ancient Olympia, Byzantine Mystras & Coast

visits.text: Pothia, Kalymnos's capital, welcomes you with its amphitheater of colorful houses as soon as you leave the ferry. Along the waterfront, stalls displaying natural sponges of all shapes and sizes immediately remind you which island you're on. Homer already mentioned this tradition in the Iliad—Kalymnos was long the world's leading exporter of natural sponges. Wander through Pothia's lanes to see the neoclassical buildings inherited from this prosperity, and spot the coats of arms of the Knights Templar engraved on certain facades.

The Maritime Museum of Pothia (Vouvalio district) is the best introduction to the island's history. You'll see the archaic diving suits used by divers until the 1960s to descend to depths of several dozen meters, archival photos of seasonal expeditions to North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean, and sponge-processing equipment. Decompression accidents were so frequent that the island had an abnormally high number of disabled men—the museum doesn't hide this reality.

Chrysocheria Castle dominates Pothia from a promontory above town. Byzantine in origin, it was reinforced by the Knights of St. John. The climb on foot from the center takes 20 to 30 minutes—the view over the harbor and sea is well worth the effort. Two small churches with post-Byzantine murals stand within the walls.

Vathy, on the island's east side, is one of Kalymnos's most unusual sites: a narrow fjord of turquoise water cuts inland between limestone cliffs, opening onto a peaceful village surrounded by orange and mandarin groves. The road leading there is winding but passable. Avoid lunchtime when excursion boats from Kos and Bodrum discharge their groups—the village regains its calm by mid-afternoon.

activities.text: Since the early 2000s, Kalymnos has become one of the world's premier sport climbing destinations, with over 3,500 equipped routes across roughly eighty sectors. The Grande Grotta sector, near Myrties, is the most photographed: its stalactites overhang the Aegean directly. Even without experience, you can try it out—several local centers offer guided sessions on beginner sectors like "Afternoon," with sea views. French-speaking instructors are active on the island. A Climbing Festival is organized every autumn, attracting climbers from around the world. Book the day before in high season.

Take a taxi-boat from Myrties to reach the island of Telendos, directly opposite—10 minutes crossing. Since an earthquake in AD 554, Telendos has been separated from Kalymnos, and local legend has it that the vestiges of an ancient city still rest on the seabed between the two islands. On site: a few tavernas, a quiet beach, and rare peace. Ideal for sunset.

In Pothia, waterfront tavernas serve grilled octopus with vinegar, fresh squid, and fava (split pea purée) drizzled with olive oil and raw onion—fishermen's cuisine at its most authentic. As a souvenir, leave with a natural sponge bought from one of the harbor shops or a processing workshop.

days_plan.itin_title: Dodecanese Journey: Rhodes, Symi & Kos

visits.text: Kamari is the main beach resort on Santorini's east coast. Its black volcanic sand beach stretches nearly 2 km at the foot of the imposing Mesa Vouno mountain (369 metres). The sand, of volcanic origin, heats very quickly: bring sandals or water shoes if you're visiting in the height of summer. The pedestrian promenade running the beach's length is lined with tavernas and bars. The atmosphere is more family-friendly and affordable than Oia or Fira. Santorini airport sits 3 km to the north - for your final day, it's a practical base.

Ancient Thera, perched atop Mesa Vouno at 369 metres altitude, is an archaeological site founded in the 9th century BC by Dorian colonists led by King Theras. It was continuously inhabited until the Byzantine era. You'll find remains of an Apollo temple, a theatre, a gymnasium and numerous inscriptions. The climb from Kamari takes roughly 30 to 45 minutes on foot. There's also a winding road open to vehicles from 7:30am to 5:30pm, or a shuttle from the village. The combined ticket purchased in Fira is valid here.

On the path climbing to Ancient Thera from the Perissa side, don't miss the rock-cut chapel of Panagia Katefiani, carved directly into the cliff. The stop takes ten minutes and the view over the east coast and Kamari below is well worth the detour. Few visitors stop here - it's a calm and unexpected spot on an otherwise well-trodden path.

Perissa, on the far side of Mesa Vouno, is Kamari's sister beach - same black volcanic sand, slightly younger and more festive atmosphere. The two villages are separated by the mountain and linked only by the inland road (roughly 25 minutes' drive) or a paid boat shuttle. If you have time, make the round trip to compare the vibe.

activities.text: The hike to Ancient Thera from Kamari is the finest walk on the east coast. The path switchbacks through dry, aromatic vegetation, with progressive views over the bay. Leave early in the morning, bring water and take your time on site - the ruins are scattered across a windy ridge with panoramas of both the island's coasts.

If you haven't yet done the caldeira volcano excursion, now is the time: boats depart from Athinios port (12 km from Kamari) or Fira Skala port. You'll walk on the crater of Nea Kameni - still active, with visible fumaroles - then swim in the sulphurous hot springs of Palea Kameni, whose water takes on an ochre tint around the hydrothermal vents. Allow roughly 4 hours. Book in advance in July-August: spots go quickly.

Kamari's open-air cinema, operating since 1987, screens from May to September under the stars with blockbusters in original language subtitled in Greek. The place is convivial, with loungers and a snack bar. Arrive early for the best seats - it's a far more local way to end an evening than Fira's crowded bars.

Mavrotragano is Santorini's grand red, produced from an almost-extinct indigenous grape saved by a handful of passionate winemakers. It yields wines of deep colour, firm tannins and remarkable structure - very different from the light reds typical of the Cyclades. If you find it by the glass in a Kamari taverna, seize the chance.

Swimming at Kamari is a good option early or late in the day. The water is clear, supervised, and facilities (loungers, fresh-water showers) are well-organised. Heads up: black sand absorbs heat enormously - avoid walking barefoot between 11am and 4pm.

days_plan.itin_title: Santorini Road Trip: Caldeira, White Villages and Minoan Sites

visits.text: Lake Plastiras — a 25 km² artificial lake created in the 1950s by damming the Tavropos river — has every appearance of a natural Alpine lake: clear blue water, fir and oak forests along the banks, the Agrafa mountains as a backdrop. The 70 km loop road around the lake connects villages perched between 850 and 1,000 m altitude. Neochori, the largest (500 inhabitants), offers a spectacular lake view from its belvedere — and a few decent tavernas.

The Monastery of Panagia Pelekiti is one of the strangest attractions in the area: it's literally carved into the vertical cliff face overlooking the lake. Founded in the 19th century, its vertiginous position is comparable to the Meteora monasteries — only much smaller and far less visited. The access path winds along the rocks above the lake.

Morfovouni, a hillside village built in an amphitheatre layout on the northern shore, houses a small museum dedicated to General Nikolaos Plastiras — the man who ordered the construction of the dam in the 1950s and after whom the lake is named. Plastiras was born in this village in 1883 and wanted to protect his home region from seasonal flooding. About 12 km away stands the Monastery of Agia Triada, a rare example of domed Byzantine architecture outside the monastic republic of Mount Athos.

Messenikolas, nicknamed "the balcony of Thessaly", is built in an amphitheatre on a promontory with views in two directions: on one side Lake Plastiras, on the other the Thessalian plain. With fewer than 500 inhabitants, the village has kept its traditional stone houses and an end-of-the-world atmosphere. It's also the starting point for the Megalo Rema gorge, a moderate-level hike through a narrow ravine.

Fylakti, a viewpoint village on the southern shore, offers one of the finest panoramas on the circuit. From its square, the winding dam road is visible below — a 1950s structure that's still impressive, passable by car in a single lane. The Tavropos Dam is the engineering feat that created this entire landscape: 82 m high, 275 m long.

activities.text: The lake is ideal for kayaking and canoeing — calm waters and wooded banks. Local operators rent out boats from the lakeside villages, particularly Kalyvia Pezoula. The atmosphere is a world away from the coastal beaches: quiet, uncrowded, with nothing but wind in the fir trees for company. If you're here in spring, the seasonal waterfalls in the Megalo Rema gorge are worth a detour.

Drive the full 70 km loop around the lake at your own pace, stopping at viewpoints and villages that catch your eye. Morning is the best time — the low-angle light on the lake is perfect and the roads are empty. Finish in Karditsa for lunch: the town is a lively agricultural market, and its tavernas serve game from the Agrafa mountains — wild boar, roe deer — depending on the season. From Karditsa, the road to Volos (1h30) and Larissa (45 min) offers a convenient return to the airports.

days_plan.itin_title: Thessaly & Meteora
Thessaly & Meteora

visits.text: Olympos is arguably the Dodecanese's most spellbinding village. Founded between the 7th and 9th centuries on the heights of Mount Profitis Ilias to escape Saracen raiders, it remained cut off from the rest of the island for centuries—the paved road only opened in 2011. The result: women still wear traditional embroidered costumes daily (not for tourists), the local dialect preserves Doric Greek phrasings, and single-room houses are built in intact medieval architecture. The 75 windmills along the ridge, many still operational, are the island's symbol. Plan a full day.

Pigadia, Karpathos's capital and main port, is the island's logistical hub. Its natural semicircular harbor is one of the Dodecanese's most sheltered. Take time to visit the archaeological museum housed in the Prefecture Palace: Mycenaean ceramics, ancient coins, Roman statues—the collection documents 3,000 years of human presence on the island.

Aperi, Karpathos's former capital until the 19th century, clings to the hillside with stone houses and flower-filled courtyards. The village is famous for its grand 19th-century mansions built by wealthy shipowners—some feature neoclassical facades that seem transported from another Mediterranean entirely. It's also one of the island's best-maintained villages.

Menetes, at 350 meters elevation in the southern hinterland, is the birthplace of the Karpathos lyre—the traditional stringed instrument that has accompanied dances for centuries. The Church of the Dormition of the Mother of God is considered the island's most beautiful. The view across the sea from the village edge at sunset alone justifies the detour.

Apella Beach is one of the Dodecanese's most photographed: a fine-sand arc framed by red schist cliffs and bathed in turquoise water. Access is via a track from the main road (about 15 minutes on foot from the parking area). There are no tavernas or parasols for rent—bring water and a picnic.

activities.text: If you're in Olympos on August 15th, don't leave under any circumstances: the Feast of the Dormition of the Virgin is celebrated here with epic songs transmitted orally for generations, costumed dances, and music played on lyre and tsambouna (local bagpipe) until dawn. Diaspora residents return from abroad just for this.

The hike between Olympos and the port of Diafani descends about 8 km via a mountain trail that overlooks the sea. The view is vertiginous and the vegetation changes at every elevation. Count on 2.5 to 3 hours of walking. Boats connect Diafani to Pigadia for the return journey.

'Makarounes' are Olympos's signature handmade pasta: shaped by hand through rolling between the palms, they're served with melted butter, fried onions and grated mizithra. You'll find them in Olympos tavernas—one of the rare places where they're still made fresh on site every morning. 'Kserotigana,' ribbons of fried dough drizzled with honey, are served at festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Dodecanese Circuit: Patmos, Leros & Karpathos

visits.text: Bourtzi Castle stands harborside in Karystos, steps from waterfront cafés—a hexagonal Venetian fortress from 1350 built partly with marble salvaged from a nearby Roman mausoleum. It's among southern Eubean's finest-preserved fortifications. In summer, the courtyard hosts concerts and exhibitions. Entry is free.

From town, gaze toward the heights 4 km away: Castello Rosso (Kokkino Kastro) crowns a rocky spur. Early Byzantine fortifications date to 1030. The Lombard baron Ravano dalle Carceri rebuilt the castle between 1209 and 1216. It later passed to Franks, Venetians, and Ottomans before 1470. A ten-minute car journey from the center rewards you with vistas spanning the bay and surrounding mountains.

Karystos Archaeological Museum, housed in the Yokaleio Cultural Foundation, presents local excavation finds including ceramic sherds recovered in 1959 during Professor Moutsopoulos' excavation of the Drakospito on Mount Ochi. Pieces related to ancient quarries and maritime history appear as well. Small but well-documented.

About 20 kilometers northwest of Karystos, above the village of Myloi, lie the ancient "Kolones" quarries: monolithic cipollino columns (marmor Carystium) in green marble, some reaching 12 meters, abandoned in situ two thousand years before shipping. This green marble with characteristic veining was quarried from the 1st century BCE and exported to Rome for imperial forums, temples, and bathhouses. Hadrian's Library in Athens still displays Karystian columns. The site is accessible on foot from Myloi.

The Drakospita—Dragon Houses—rank among Eubean's most enigmatic monuments. Twenty-three exist across southern Eubean; the largest and best-preserved crowns Mount Ochi's 1,389-meter summit. These megalithic structures, raised without mortar from slabs weighing up to 10 tons, likely date to the Classical or Proto-Classical period (5th–4th centuries BCE). Their purpose remains baffling: temple to Zeus and Hera? Watchtower? Quarryman's lodge? Astronomical observatory? One doorway slab measures 4 meters long, 2 meters wide, yet weighs 10 tons. Their first scholarly mention came October 21, 1797, when British geologist John Hawkins ascended Mount Ochi and documented what he found.

activities.text: Mount Ochi hike to reach the summit Dragon House: this is the day's grand excursion. Depart from the parking area above Myloi village (via the forest road ascending from the main highway). Pass the refuge at 1,050 meters and reach the Profitis Ilias chapel. The Dragon House stands meters from the summit. Plan 4–5 hours round-trip. Leave early, carry water, bring a windbreaker—wind can be brisk at the summit even in summer. The trail traverses the Kastanologos chestnut forest, a rare ecological sanctuary.

Visit the Myloi quarries (alternative to the Ochi hike or complementary short excursion): chisel marks in living rock, abandoned columns, and ancient transport systems remain perfectly legible. The open-air, unfenced site commands spectacular vistas over Karystos Bay and the Aegean.

Taste matsata—homemade Karystos pasta served in sauce with braised cock and tomato: this is the town's festive specialty, rustic and generous. Seek it in central tavernas, preferably at dinner. Wash it down with Karistia Gi white, a local blend of Savatiano, Malagouzia, and Assyrtiko.

Before departing toward Rafina or Athens via ferry from Marmari (10 km from Karystos, regular service to Rafina in 1h15), take one last café on the waterfront facing Bourtzi. This ranks among the island's most tranquil waterfronts—fishermen, a handful of pleasure boats, waves lapping stone beaches. The whistled language of nearby Antia village (10 km away) is one of the world's last remaining systems of long-distance vocal communication: a detour there is delightfully offbeat if time permits.

days_plan.itin_title: Eubea Explored: Ancient Spas and Hidden Villages

visits.text: Discover the Kassandra Peninsula, the westernmost finger of Halkidiki renowned for its stunning white sand beaches and turquoise Aegean waters. This popular resort area offers some of the finest swimming and sunbathing in northern Greece, with beaches ranging from lively resort strips to secluded coves. The warm, crystal-clear waters and gentle coastline make Kassandra ideal for water sports and family beach holidays.

Explore the picturesque village of Afitos, a traditional settlement perched on cliffs overlooking the sea with panoramic views across the Toroneos Gulf. Charming stone houses, artisan workshops, and tavernas line cobbled streets that have preserved their authentic Macedonian character. The village's elevated position provides spectacular sunset vistas over the Aegean that draw visitors from across Halkidiki.

activities.text: Swim in the pristine waters of Halkidiki's beaches, consistently ranked among the cleanest and most beautiful in the Mediterranean. The three peninsulas jutting into the Aegean like Poseidon's trident offer hundreds of kilometers of coastline, from organized beaches with water sports to hidden coves accessible only by boat. The region's clear, warm waters and pine-fringed shores create a quintessential Greek summer experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece Travel Guide: Monasteries, Olympus and Ancient Kingdoms

visits.text: The fortress of Kavala commands the entire peninsula from the summit of Panagia hill. Its current form dates from the early 15th century, though it rests on Byzantine foundations subsequently reworked by the Venetians and the Ottomans. Climb up to the outer walls for the view: at your feet, the red-tiled roofs of the old town, the turquoise harbour, and in the distance the silhouette of Thasos on the horizon. The open-air theatre nestled within the walls hosts performances in summer — check the programme if you're here in July or August.

The Kamares Aqueduct is the most photographed monument in the city, and it deserves every shot. This double-tiered structure — 11 large arches below, narrower arches above — stretches 270 metres long and stands 25 metres high. It was built between 1520 and 1536 on the orders of Ibrahim Pasha, Grand Vizier to Suleiman the Magnificent, after the city was destroyed in 1391. Made of local granite with Ottoman brick detailing, it supplied Kavala with water from Mount Pangaion until 1911. The best angle for a photo: from Lahana Street in the late afternoon, when the golden light brings out the stone.

In the old quarter of Panagia, look for the house of Mehmet Ali (1769–1849) — the founder of the last dynasty to rule Egypt, born right here in this modest cantilevered stone building. The house is preserved as a museum and gives a good sense of Ottoman domestic architecture of the era. Across from it, the square bearing his name features an equestrian statue of the pasha — one of the rare depictions of a human figure tolerated within Islamic tradition.

The Panagia quarter, perched on the peninsula between the fortress and the sea, is the historic heart of Kavala. Its cobbled alleyways, colourful houses with cantilevered balconies (sachnisia), and hidden courtyards make it one of the best-preserved Ottoman neighbourhoods in northern Greece. Don't miss the Halil Bey Mosque, built on the foundations of an early Christian basilica — during the population exchange of 1922–1923, it served as temporary shelter for Greek refugee families.

The Imaret, on the western flank of the Panagia peninsula, is one of the largest Ottoman-era Islamic complexes still standing in Europe. Built in the early 19th century by Mehmet Ali to serve as a soup kitchen, madrasa and mosque, it blends Ottoman arches with slender columns around a remarkable inner courtyard. The interior has been privatised, but the exterior and gardens can be explored freely from the lane.

The site of Philippi has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2016 — the 18th Greek site to join the list. Founded in 360 BC by colonists from Thasos under the name Krenides, the city was conquered in 356 BC by Philip II of Macedon, father of Alexander the Great, who gave it his name. Allow at least two hours to cross the entire site — it covers 67 hectares, slightly larger than Pompeii.

The theatre of Philippi was built by Philip II in the mid-4th century BC and remodelled under the Romans in the 2nd century AD into a theatre with a monumental three-storey stage building. In the 3rd century it was converted into an arena for gladiatorial combat: the wild animal pit beneath the orchestra is still visible. Since 1957 it has hosted the Philippi Festival every summer — one of the oldest ancient-theatre festivals in Greece.

The Roman forum of Philippi is one of the best-documented in the eastern provinces of the Empire: a vast square surrounded by temples, shops, the orators' tribune, and a meeting hall for the decurions. A monumental inscription at least 20 metres long, with metal letters set into the pavement at a height of 62 cm, honoured the flamen of the divine Augustus. Look for the ruts left by chariots on the Via Egnatia — they're still legible in the stone.

The early Christian basilicas of Philippi are among the most impressive in Greece. Basilica A (late 5th century) measured 130 by 50 metres, with a transept, galleries and atrium. The Octagon, an episcopal complex built on the presumed site of Saint Paul's house of prayer, went through three construction phases from the 4th to the 6th century. It was here, in AD 49–50, that the Apostle Paul founded Europe's first Christian community — and was briefly imprisoned in a Roman cistern traditionally identified as the 'Prison of Paul'.

The Archaeological Museum of Philippi, spread over two floors, presents the site's collections chronologically: sculptures, vases, coins and gold jewellery from prehistoric through Roman times on the ground floor, Christian heritage and the city's decline upstairs. Allow 45 minutes to an hour — the Macedonian jewellery and coins from the era of Philip II are particularly worth your time.

activities.text: Stroll along the Kavala waterfront down to the fishing harbour: the blue-and-white boats, nets drying on the quay, and fish tavernas lined up along the water give the city the feel of an authentic Greek port, well away from the mass-tourism circuit. This is where Saint Paul landed on his first journey to Europe — a commemorative plaque near the harbour marks the event.

Sample sardeles pastes: salted, dried and pressed sardines are Kavala's signature culinary speciality, going back centuries. They're eaten on toasted bread, simply drizzled with lemon juice, alongside a glass of Macedonian tsipouro without anise — the local spirit served chilled with olives and feta. The tavernas along the harbour serve both without ceremony.

In the evening, order a saganaki: a slab of graviera cheese, breaded and pan-fried in olive oil, served still crackling with a wedge of lemon. Simple, no-nonsense, and far better than it sounds — it's the go-to appetiser across the entire region.

Side trip to the Baptistery of Saint Lydia, a few hundred metres from the archaeological site, on the bank of the Zygaktis River. Lydia, a wealthy purple-cloth merchant from Thyatira, was the first European baptised by Paul in AD 49–50 in this river. The modern octagonal baptistery dates from 1974, but an open-air baptism ceremony takes place every 20 May — if you happen to be here on that date, you can attend.

Try kavourmas: pork or veal slow-cooked in its own fat with spices — a traditional Macedonian winter preservation method you'll find in tavernas around the Kavala area, served as a hot or cold meze. Pair it with an assyrtiko from Drama: this mineral white wine, produced in the highland vineyards to the west, is dry, bright and sharply acidic.

The evening drive back to Kavala follows the Philippi plain, dominated by Mount Pangaion to the south-west — it was from its gold and silver mines that Philip II bankrolled his conquests. If you have time, stop at Krinides, the small modern village on the site of ancient Krenides, to see the remains of the Hellenistic city walls on the outskirts of the UNESCO site.

visits.text: Kimolos takes its name from the Greek 'kimolia'—chalk—and its houses are indeed whiter than white. The island covers just 36 km² and is easily explored on foot or by scooter from Psathi port. The village of Chorio, perched on a hill 2 km from the port, is dominated by a 14th-century Venetian kastro, some of whose houses are still inhabited. The metropolitan church of Panagia Odigitria (1592) is its religious heart. Wandering Chorio, look for the syrmata at tiny Goupa beach: these are former boat garages carved into rock, intact maritime heritage.

Prassa beach (also called Agios Georgios) is unanimously considered Kimolos's finest—some say of all the Cyclades. Almost surreal white sand, turquoise water of exceptional clarity. A beach bar, sun loungers, and sea caves within flipper range. Near Prassa, natural hot springs with reddish waters supposedly have healing properties according to locals. Spotting a monk seal in the neighboring waters isn't rare.

Skiadi rock is worth the 20-30 minute walk from the main road: erosion has sculpted a formation shaped like a giant mushroom, unique in the Mediterranean. Look for the marked path between Chorio and the west coast.

activities.text: Swimming at Prassa and snorkeling around the rock formations: corals, sea urchins, and colorful reef fish in remarkably clear water. The nearby Vromolini sea caves will please freedivers.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Falassarna beach is actually five consecutive beaches stretching over 3 km — the main one, Pachia Ammos, runs for over a kilometre. The sand is thick and white, the water an intense turquoise, and the full-west orientation produces the most beautiful sunsets in all of Crete: the sun drops straight into the sea with nothing in its way. Settle in late afternoon for the show. Be aware that the beach can be windy — on meltemi days, the waves attract windsurfers but make swimming less enjoyable.

Take 30 minutes to explore the ruins of the ancient city of Falasarna at the northern end of the beach — it's a story few people know. The city thrived from the 4th to the 1st century BC as a naval power, boasting the only natural harbour in western Crete. In 69 BC, the Romans destroyed everything and sealed the harbour to put an end to what they called a pirate stronghold. Excavations begun in 1986 have uncovered the docks, the acropolis, and tombs from the 6th century BC — you're literally walking on 2,500 years of history between beach towels.

Kissamos — also known as Kastelli — doesn't look like the other tourist towns in Crete, and that's exactly why it's worth a stop. No restored Venetian harbour for the tourists, no souvenir-shop alleyways: just a genuine waterfront with fishermen's tavernas serving whatever they pulled from the sea that morning. Enjoy a simple dinner by the water before sorting out your Balos excursion for the next day — Kissamos port is where the ferries depart.

activities.text: Ferry excursion from Kissamos port to the Balos lagoon and Gramvousa island. The Balos lagoon can be reached either by this boat trip (departing around 10 am, returning late afternoon) or via an unpaved 8 km track by car (note: rental contracts typically don't cover damage on dirt roads) followed by a 20- to 30-minute walk. From the heights overlooking the lagoon, the view is genuinely breathtaking: a tongue of white-pink sand between two arms of intensely blue sea. The sand owes its pink tint to the same micro-organisms (foraminifera) and crushed shells as Elafonisi. Get there early — the first ferries let you arrive before the midday rush. Book ahead in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Crete Road Trip: Gorges and Beaches

visits.text: Konitsa is built amphitheater-style on a Pindus slope, overlooking the confluence of the Aoos and Voidomatis rivers. Its stone bridge is one of the largest single-arch bridges in the Balkans: 20 meters high, nearly 40 meters span, built between 1823 and 1871 by master mason Zioga Frontzo from the village of Pyrsoyianni. Cross it on foot—the wind blows hard at the arch's summit, and the masons hung a small bell underneath to keep rhythm during construction.

The Panagia Stomiou monastery sits 5 km downstream from Konitsa bridge, clinging to the rock wall of the Aoos Gorge at 650 meters altitude. Built in 1774, it was inhabited for several decades by monk Paisios, who lived there as a hermit before joining Mount Athos. The trail leading to it follows the turquoise river in a grandiose setting—swimmers bathe there in summer, though the water is ice-cold and the current strong.

activities.text: The Aoos River below Konitsa is one of Europe's last wild rivers: clear waters, pebble beds, steep gorges. In summer, local operators offer rafting on the rapids downstream from the bridge—a 2 to 3-hour descent suitable for beginners. If you prefer a calmer approach, kayaking on the peaceful stretches lets you observe the cliffs and riverside vegetation from the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Epirus & Zagori: Gorges, Stone Villages and the River of the Underworld

places.name: Kos

visits.text: The Asclepeion of Kos sits 4 km southwest of town, and it's the island's most important archaeological site. Founded in the 4th century BC, it was simultaneously a religious sanctuary, healing center, and medical school—one of history's first hospitals, capable of receiving up to 4,000 patients according to some ancient sources. Hippocrates, a native of Kos, supposedly taught here. The sanctuary spreads across three terraces connected by marble staircases: Roman baths and consultation rooms on the first level, temples of Apollo and Asclepius on the second, grand Doric temple and porticoes on the third. From the top, the view sweeps across Kos town, the sea, and the Turkish coast opposite. Bring a hat and water—there's little shade on site.

Opposite the Knights' castle, on Platanos square, stands the Hippocrates Plane Tree. The tree measures 4.7 meters in diameter and spreads more than 12 meters in radius—it's one of Europe's oldest and largest plane trees, even if botanists estimate its age at around 500 years rather than the legendary 2,400. No matter: the spot is pleasant, shaded, and tradition says Hippocrates taught medicine beneath its branches.

The Castle of the Knights of Kos (Neratzia), built from 1315 directly atop the ruins of an ancient fort, controls the harbor entrance from its promontory. The Knights of St. John reused ancient marble blocks for its walls—you can still clearly spot them in the lower courses. The interior houses a collection of ancient and medieval sculptures. Until the early 20th century, the castle was separated from town by a moat filled with seawater.

The ancient agora of Kos, south of the castle, is one of the region's best-preserved ancient urban centers. Founded in the 4th century BC on a geometric plan, it features a massive colonnaded stoa, ruins of a sanctuary to Aphrodite, a 2nd-century BC Temple of Hercules, and a 5th-century Christian basilica. The site is generally open and lightly monitored—ideal for peaceful exploration.

The Archaeological Museum of Kos, on Eleftherias square, houses the collection of discoveries made on the island since antiquity. The centerpiece: a marble statue of Hippocrates, and several remarkable mosaics from the Hellenistic and Roman periods. Combined ticket with the Asclepeion available.

activities.text: Kos is one of the Dodecanese's most bikeable Greek islands—flat, well-served by bike paths, and crossed end-to-end by a coastal road. Rent a bike in the morning to reach the Asclepeion (4 km), then head toward the beaches of Tigaki or Marmari on the north coast, with their fine sand and shallow waters. The total journey from Kos town represents about twenty kilometers round-trip—very accessible for any level.

In the evening, explore the lively center of Kos town with its blend of Ottoman architecture (mosques, fountains) and Greek and Roman remains around street corners. Apellou street and the areas around Eleftherias square concentrate tavernas, bars, and restaurants for ending the day on a terrace.

Try patatato, Kos's traditional dish: veal slowly braised with potatoes, white wine, and cumin. Simple, generous, rooted in the island's rural tradition. For dessert, sample the local baklava, more generous with walnuts and cinnamon than the neighboring Turkish version.

visits.text: Koufonissia—actually two islands, Ano and Kato Koufonissi—is the rising star of the Lesser Cyclades. Ano Koufonissi's Chora, the only inhabited village, sprawls around a colorful harbor dominated by the church of Agios Georgios. But what makes the island unique is its southeastern coast: a 45-minute coastal path from Chora links the beaches of Finikas, Fanos, and Italida—surreal turquoise waters on golden sand—before reaching Pori, the archipelago's finest beach. At Pori's northern tip, the Ksilobatis caves offer limestone arches bathed in emerald water. Budget 3 hours round trip for the full circuit from Chora, no shade along the way: leave early and pack water.

Gala Beach, between Pori and Italida, is a hidden cove whose Greek name means 'milk'—the cave's wave foam does indeed give the water a unique milky tint. Just beyond lies the Eye of the Devil, a natural rock pool with a diving platform. These two curiosities sit 5 minutes' walk from Pori.

activities.text: Sea kayaking from Ammos beach toward the cliffs of Kato Koufonissi: paddling along the sheer walls reveals arches and coves invisible from land. Kayaks are for rent at the main beach. Check the wind (meltemi) before setting out.
Anchorage in Pori bay: sandy bottom, good holding depending on conditions. Water taxis from Chora make 4 runs per day—handy for alternating between swimming and exploring Chora.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Kymi perches 200 meters above the Aegean Sea, hidden behind forested hills—locals call it "the Balcony of the Aegean." Shipowner mansions from the 19th century, when Kymi commanded 400 sailing vessels and ranked second only to Chalcis in island prominence, lend the center a rare architectural character. Climb to the main square for vistas across the open sea. A statue in the square commemorates the town's most celebrated son.

Kymi's folk museum gathers embroidered costumes, maritime documents, traditional tools, and daily-life artifacts chronicling this sailors' town. It also houses archives connected to Dr. George Papanicolaou, born here May 13, 1883. This Greek physician, who emigrated to the United States in 1913, developed the cytological test known as the "Pap smear"—a screening method for early cervical cancer detection that has saved millions of lives worldwide. His birthhouse is visible nearby.

Descend the serpentine 5-km road to Paralia Kymis at the clifftop's base. This is where ferries depart for Skyros, Skopelos, and Alonissos. The atmosphere is that of an animated fishing harbor: seaside tavernas line the quay, blue-and-white boats bob at anchor, nets dry in the sun. If timing allows, dine here rather than in the upper town.

The Monastery of the Transfiguration (Metamorphosis), founded in the 16th century by monks from Mount Athos, nestles in greenery along the route between Kymi and the sea. It's a peaceful sanctuary with characteristic monastic architecture and small-batch honey production sold on-site. The nuns who inhabit it today welcome visitors outside service hours—dress appropriately.

activities.text: Kymi's dried figs are among Eubean products most prized: fumigated with sulfur by the local cooperative, they acquire a pale hue and slightly tart edge distinguishing them from standard dried figs. Search for them in central-town groceries—they're the edible souvenir worth bringing home.
Hike to Maniakiatis Falls: the Maniakiatis River originates on Mount Kserovouni. A 50-minute trail leads to a 15-meter waterfall amid lush vegetation. Start early to enjoy the cool morning air.
Savor Kymi's baklava: the local pastry craft is celebrated for its quality, crafted with Eubean walnuts and honey. Pair it with amygdalota—tender almond cakes found in every pastry shop in town.

days_plan.itin_title: Eubea Explored: Ancient Spas and Hidden Villages

visits.text: Kolona is one of the Cyclades' great surprises. A slender sand tombolo—a natural tongue barely a few meters wide—connects the main island to the islet of Agios Loukas, creating two back-to-back beaches with different water temperatures: the right one is slightly warmer because hot springs surface nearby. It's rare to find such a formation in the Mediterranean. Access via dirt track (prefer the water taxi from Merichas, 15 min)—no shade on the beach, bring sun protection.

Dryopida, Kythnos's second village, deserves a 20-minute detour from Kolona. Its red-tiled lanes break from the usual Cycladic white—this is where the island's characteristic tiles have been made for centuries. Katafyki cave, beneath the village, sheltered residents during World War II. It's open in season.

activities.text: Swimming between two seas: swim on one side then the other of the tombolo to feel the temperature difference. Snorkeling possible around the islet's rocks.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Approaching from Heraklion—about 90 minutes by car—or from Agios Nikolaos (75 minutes), you emerge onto a sight utterly unexpected after the coastal scrubland: the Lasithi Plateau, a green basin 850 meters above sea level, 11 kilometers long, ringed by the Dikti Mountains that soar above 2,100 meters. What strikes you first are the cultivated fields and, dotted here and there, restored windmills. The Venetians once built up to 5,000 of them to irrigate the land through a network of canals called linies. Stop at Seli Ambelou at the plateau's entrance to see them lined up against the sky—it's the most photographed viewpoint in Lasithi.

The Dikteon Cave, known as the Cave of Zeus, clings to the mountainside at 1,025 meters above the village of Psychro. According to Greek mythology, this is where Rhea hid her infant son Zeus from his father Cronos, who had a nasty habit of devouring his own children. Descending the 314 steps into the mountain's belly is a bit of a workout, but the payoff is real: a forest of stalactites and an underground lake in an atmosphere that feels genuinely mystical. Bring a sweater—it's cool inside year-round, no matter how scorching it is outside. The cave draws over 200,000 visitors annually; arrive early in the morning to explore without the crowds.

On the road climbing toward Lasithi, stop at the Kera Kardiotissa Monastery, perched at 650 meters on the northern slopes of Mount Dikti. Its first written mention dates to 1333. What sets this place apart is the story of its miraculous icon: stolen three times by the Turks, it returned three times on its own. The third time, they chained it to a marble column to prevent another escape—it came back anyway, bringing the column with it. You'll see the column in the courtyard and the chain beside the icon. The original has been in Rome since 1498; the copy that replaced it in 1735 is also considered miraculous.

Make a stop in Tzermiado, the plateau's 'capital.' Its folklore museum reconstructs two Cretan interiors: one traditional mountain dwelling, the other a neoclassical home from the post-Ottoman period. If you have time, head to the Kronion Cave at the village entrance—a Minoan cult site frequented since the Neolithic, far less known than the Dikteon Cave and often completely deserted. The plateau is one of the rare places in Crete where people live year-round, even under winter snow: take the time to chat with locals in the village cafés.

Agios Georgios houses a museum dedicated to daily life on the plateau—better curated than its modest size might suggest. You'll find a carefully reconstructed Cretan mountain house and a room devoted to Eleftherios Venizelos, the great Greek statesman whose family hailed from the region. Between May and October, the museum is open Monday to Saturday from 10:30 AM to 5 PM. Nominal entrance fee.

activities.text: The drive up to the plateau from Heraklion or Agios Nikolaos is a pleasure in itself: the road winds through panoramic mountain passes and perched villages. From Agios Nikolaos (about 75 minutes), the approach from the south side of the Dikti massif is particularly beautiful. If you're coming from the north coast, stop at the Seli Ambelou viewpoint to see the windmills lined up with the valley below—and in the distance on clear days, the Aposelemis reservoir and the submerged village of Sfendyli.

The Seli windmill loop is an easy 3-kilometer walk among the 19th-century stone windmills and orchards. The E4 trail also crosses the plateau for those who want to push on to the Minoan ruins at Mount Karfi, with spectacular views over the coast. Bring sturdy shoes and water.

In the tavernas of Psychro or Tzermiado, ask for stakovoutyro: a fermented mountain butter from the Lasithi highlands, made by churning sheep's milk, that you eat spread on grilled bread. Rare—you'll find it almost nowhere else in Crete. If you're there in season, the taverna tables overflow with local products: fresh cheeses, wild thyme honey, mountain potatoes.

For dinner, order ofto at a plateau taverna: a whole lamb slow-roasted in an earthen oven with potatoes, garlic, and wild herbs—meat so tender it falls apart, juices concentrated and rich. Lasithi tavernas serve it mostly on Sundays as a traditional dish. Pair it with a glass of Romeiko, the rustic and ancient red grape of eastern Crete, vinified naturally by local producers.

days_plan.itin_title: Crete Road Trip: Agios Nikolaos, Elounda & Spinalonga
Grand Tour of Crete: Minoan Palaces, Gorges and Eastern Coast

visits.text: The Kastro of Panagia, perched on Apityki hill 200 meters above the sea, is visible from the ferry long before you dock at Leros. Built by the Byzantines in the 11th century and reinforced by the Knights of Rhodes in the 15th, it shelters the miraculous icon of the Virgin who, according to legend, emerged from the sea of her own accord before being installed in the powder magazine chapel. Climb the 300 steps from Platanos—the view of Agia Marina and Panteli bays spreading on either side of the hill is one of the Dodecanese's finest. The 1745 iconostasis and the bronze-and-silver bell cast by Russian artisans are worth the ascent.

Platanos, Leros's former capital, is a quiet village squeezed between two hills, with sand-colored neoclassical houses and small squares shaded by plane trees. This is where the island's administrative heart beats, far from the port bustle. Take time to wander its lanes—several buildings date from the 19th century, when local shipowners were at the height of their prosperity.

Lakki is one of Greece's most bizarre architectural curiosities. Founded in the 1930s by the Italians under the name Porto Lago, this port was designed from scratch according to fascist rationalist principles: wide boulevards, buildings with rounded facades, a cinema and town hall resembling beached ocean liners. Lakki Bay is one of the Mediterranean's largest natural harbors. It's hard not to feel disoriented strolling these nearly deserted streets off-season—like wandering a forgotten 1930s film set.

The War Museum, installed in an authentic underground tunnel at Merikia, is unique in Greece. The Italians excavated it as a munitions depot; in 1943, the Battle of Leros left scars here when the Germans bombarded for 42 days before seizing the island from the British, Greeks and Italians. The museum, opened in 2005, preserves weapons, uniforms, munitions and maps from the 1912-1943 period. Allow 45 minutes.

Alinda's Belleni Tower, built between 1925 and 1927 by expatriate Parisi Belleni, is a neo-Gothic turreted stone building that now houses the island's folklore museum. Exhibits include the watch from the destroyer 'Queen Olga,' sunk in the harbor during the 1943 battle, and artwork painted by political prisoners interned on Leros under Greece's dictatorship (1967-1974).

activities.text: Leros's beaches are perfect for families: Alinda, the longest, is lined with tamarisks and its shallow waters suit children. Vromolithos offers a beach bar and crystalline water. Panteli, at the castle's foot, is the ideal spot in late afternoon when light settles on the ruined windmills along the ridge. For diving, Xirokampos in the south is renowned for its World War II wrecks—a good dozen ships and planes rest at 10 to 30 meters depth.

'Koukouvades,' fish marinated with rosemary, are Leros's signature seafood specialty. 'Pitakia,' pies stuffed with fava beans, and 'Kakavia,' bouillabaisse prepared with fish pulled from caïques that very morning, are essential at tavernas in Panteli or Agia Marina. For dessert, 'Melopita'—honey-and-mizithra-cheese cake—is the local sweet touch.

Ride a scooter (ideal for this reasonably sized island) up to the castle at sunset, when the last visitors have left and bats begin circling the battlements. The panorama of both bays in orange light is one of the Dodecanese's most memorable views. Scooter rentals are available at Agia Marina and Lakki ports.

days_plan.itin_title: Dodecanese Circuit: Patmos, Leros & Karpathos

visits.text: The castle of Agia Mavra (Santa Maura), standing at the edge of the channel that separates the island from the mainland, is the first thing you'll see as you cross the bridge. Built in the 14th century by the Orsini counts, it gave its medieval name to the entire island — Lefkada was known as Santa Maura until the 19th century. The Turks held it from 1479 to 1684, before the Venetians took it back. The grounds are freely accessible and the view over the lagoon and salt flats is beautiful in the late afternoon.

Cape Lefkatas, at the island's south-western tip, is one of the most myth-laden sites in all of Greece. The white cliff drops 72 metres straight into the Ionian Sea. In antiquity, tradition held that those suffering from unrequited love would leap from this promontory into the sea — surviving the jump meant being cured of their passion. The poet Sappho (c. 630–580 BC) is said to have died here for the love of Phaon, according to legend. A lighthouse built in 1913 now stands on the site of the ancient temple of Apollo. On a clear day, you can see the coast of Epirus.

Nydri, on the east coast, is the launching pad for boat trips to the small islands of the archipelago: Meganisi, Madouri, Sparti and, most famously, Skorpios — formerly owned by Aristotle Onassis, where he married Jackie Kennedy in 1968. Water taxis and organised excursions leave from the harbour throughout the day in summer. The marina is lively, with plenty of waterfront tavernas.

Faneromeni Monastery, perched on a wooded hill 3 km north of the capital, offers the finest bird's-eye view of Lefkada town and the channel separating it from the mainland. The present buildings date from the 17th–18th centuries. Allow 20 minutes on foot from the car park.

The town of Lefkada itself is worth an hour's wander through its lanes: the facades are built of painted corrugated iron on wooden frames — an earthquake-resistant technique honed over centuries of seismic experience, which gives the houses a Caribbean feel unique in the Ionians. The archaeological museum, in town, displays finds from the excavations of Dörpfeld, the German archaeologist who believed he had found the homeland of Odysseus here.

activities.text: Rent a boat from Nydri and explore the islets off the east coast at your own pace. Skorpios remains private property, but you can anchor nearby; Meganisi has several superb coves accessible only by sea. The east coast of Lefkada is sheltered from the prevailing winds — ideal for a relaxed day on the water, even without sailing experience.

Eglouvi lentils are Lefkada's most prized agricultural product — this small mountain village at 700 metres altitude grows tiny, flavourful lentils that carry a Protected Designation of Origin (PDO). In soup with local olive oil or as a cold salad, they're simple and outstanding. Look for them in the grocery shops of the capital.

Ladopita is a rustic Lefkadian cake made with olive oil, flour and raisins — no butter, no milk. It's a Lenten recipe handed down from the Venetian centuries. Dense and mildly sweet, it pairs perfectly with a Greek coffee on a terrace. You'll find it in the traditional bakeries of the old town.

Agios Nikitas, 12 km northwest of the capital, is the most charming seaside village on the coast: its pedestrian lanes lead straight down to a small sandy beach, the tavernas sit at the water's edge and the crowds are more manageable than in Nydri. It's a good option for dinner or a late-afternoon swim.

days_plan.itin_title: Ionian Islands Road Trip: Corfu, Paxos & Lefkada

visits.text: Stand in the shadow of Mount Olympus, the legendary home of the ancient Greek gods rising 2,918 meters as Greece's highest peak. This mythical mountain where Zeus and the Olympian deities held court dominates the landscape of Pieria with its snow-capped summit visible from Thessaloniki on clear days. Olympus National Park, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, protects pristine forests and rare alpine flora surrounding the sacred mountain.

Explore the Archaeological Site of Dion, the sacred sanctuary of the ancient Macedonians dedicated to Zeus at the foot of Mount Olympus. Philip II and Alexander the Great celebrated their military victories at this sacred site where the Greeks believed the gods descended to earth. Remarkably preserved temples, theaters, and Roman baths reveal the religious importance of this pilgrimage destination.

Wander through the enchanting village of Palaios Panteleimonas, a hidden gem perched 700 meters on the slopes of Mount Olympus. This 18th-century settlement of traditional stone houses draped in flowers offers spectacular views across the Aegean to the snowy peaks above. Time seems suspended in the cobbled lanes where local tavernas serve traditional cuisine on terraces overlooking the sea.

activities.text: Cool off in the crystal-clear pools of Orlias Waterfalls, a paradise of 25 cascading waterfalls hidden in a forested canyon near Dion. Natural swimming pools formed beneath each waterfall offer refreshing respite during warm months, surrounded by lush vegetation. The easy hiking trail through this verdant canyon reveals waterfall after waterfall in an increasingly magical setting.

Explore the Byzantine Castle of Platamonas, a crusader fortress dramatically positioned on a hill overlooking the Aegean Sea. Built between 1204 and 1222 to guard the entrance to the Temple Valley, this impressive stronghold features distinctively medieval European architecture rare in Greece. The imposing donjon and well-preserved walls command sweeping views over the coastline and toward Mount Olympus.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece Travel Guide: Monasteries, Olympus and Ancient Kingdoms
Greek Macedonia: Mount Olympus, Ancient Capitals & Byzantine Treasures

visits.text: Hora Sfakion is the embarkation point for Loutro—leave your car in the harbor parking lot and buy your ticket at the ferry company counter before boarding. The crossing takes about 20 minutes and runs along sheer cliffs that plunge directly into the Libyan Sea. It's already a spectacle in itself.

Loutro is one of Greece's most unusual villages: no road reaches it. White houses with blue shutters cascade down to a small bay of emerald water, completely sheltered from the wind. The village was once the port of Anopolis, a prosperous city in Roman and Byzantine times—the Bible even mentions this anchorage as the place where the Apostle Paul took shelter during his journey to Rome. Today, no cars, no engines—just the sound of waves and taverna terraces.

activities.text: Before taking the ferry, drive up to the village of Aradena (20 minutes by car from Hora Sfakion). The Vardinogiannis Bridge spans an impressive precipice several dozen meters deep—kids generally love it, drivers a bit less so. In the abandoned village, a 14th-century Byzantine chapel dedicated to the Archangel Michael stands at the gorge's edge.

From Loutro, take a water taxi to Glyka Nera beach—the sweet water beach. Natural springs gush between the pebbles directly into the sea, creating slightly cooler water with a curiously sweet taste. The 500-meter cliffs looming above cut it off completely from the world. Marmara Beach, the starting point for Aradena Gorge, is accessible the same way.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Southern Crete: Gorges, Deserted Beaches & End-of-the-World Villages

visits.text: Portaria, 13 km from Volos, is the first village on western Pelion — its nickname "lady of welcome" is well earned: centuries-old plane trees, fountains, stone houses with carved wooden balconies. During Ottoman rule (15th–19th c.), Pelion was a refuge for Greek intellectuals fleeing Constantinople and for merchants who traded freely with Europe. That era left behind exceptional architecture, libraries, and secret schools in every village.

Makrinitsa — "the balcony of Pelion" — lives up to its reputation. At 650 m altitude, its cobbled lanes open onto a central square with views stretching across Volos, the Pagasetic Gulf and, on clear days, the Sporades islands. The 18th-century houses in local stone, with their carved wooden overhangs, are among the best preserved in Greece. On the square, the church of Agios Ioannis Prodromos (Saint John the Baptist) holds frescoes by the folk painter Theophilos, who lived and worked in the Pelion villages in the early 20th century. The four-lion-head fountain of 1809, known as the "Spring of Immortal Water", still flows. Allow at least 2 hours to wander.

The Tapalis House Museum, built in 1844 and now the Folk Museum, is a window into Pelion's bourgeois past: furniture, jewellery, costumes, weapons, and mountain utensils. While you're there, visit the church of Agia Magdalini (1737) with its icons and wall paintings — the interior is unexpectedly rich for such a small village.

Tsagarada, on Pelion's east side facing the Aegean, is one of the most beautiful villages on the peninsula. Its central square is dominated by a plane tree that locals say is over 1,000 years old — its circumference exceeds 14 m and its shade covers half the square. It was under this tree that villagers gathered for centuries to hold the agora, settle village affairs, and debate politics. Have your morning coffee here, facing the Aegean Sea glimpsed between the trees — it's one of the finest terraces in Greece.

Damouchari, below Tsagarada, is one of those tiny Pelion harbours that look like a postcard of the ideal Mediterranean: a bay split in two by a headland topped with the ruins of a medieval castle, fish tavernas right on the jetty, water so clear you can see the bottom at 5 metres. Several scenes from the film "Mamma Mia" were shot here — the producers didn't have to look far.

activities.text: The shops in Makrinitsa sell local Pelion produce — mountain herbs, honey, fruit preserves (apple, chestnut), liqueurs, handmade pasta. Try the spetsiotiko (spiced Pelion sausage) and mountain tea (tsai tou vounou) in a taverna overlooking the bay. It's a distinctly Thessalian food experience — a world away from the tourist spots on the islands.

Mylopotamos beach, reached from Tsagarada via 7 km of side road followed by a 15–20 minute walk along a forest trail, is one of the most beautiful on the Greek mainland: white sand, rocks splitting the beach into two coves, turquoise water, and pine trees reaching down to the shore. The walk through Pelion's dense forest is as good as the beach itself. Plan on spending the afternoon — the descent is easy, the climb back takes a good 30 minutes of effort.

The inter-village trails of Pelion (monopatia) are paved with grey schist slabs and date largely from the Ottoman period — they connected villages when there were no roads. The Damouchari–Tsagarada section (roughly 45 minutes one way) is one of the finest: dense forest, waterfalls, and sea views. The trail is waymarked and manageable for everyone.
