Thessaly & Meteora

visits.text: Exploration of the Etolikou lagoon, paradise for migratory birds and nature lovers.
Stroll through the coastal village of Marathias, a typical fishing port offering a beautiful view of the Peloponnese.

activities.text: Stroll at the port and observation of the sunset over the Peloponnese mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Peloponnese, Greece Road Trip: Ancient Olympia, Byzantine Mystras & Coast

visits.text: The caves of Matala are carved straight into the golden limestone cliff that closes off the bay to the south — some date back to the Neolithic era, while others served as Roman tombs in the 1st and 2nd centuries AD. But it's their later chapter that made Matala famous: in the 1960s and 70s, hundreds of hippies from around the world moved in, living rent-free in the seaside caverns. Joni Mitchell stayed here and immortalized the place in her song "Carey" (from the album Blue, 1971). Allow 30 to 45 minutes to explore the accessible caves from the beach — admission to the main complex is €2. On the harbour wall, look out for the painted slogan that captures the spirit of the place: "Today is life. Tomorrow never comes."

Matala beach stretches 250 m long and 45 m wide — not the biggest in Crete, but the setting is one of a kind: tamarisk trees on one side, cave-riddled cliffs on the other, and in the background, the snow-capped peaks of Crete in winter. The water is perfectly clear, and the westward orientation delivers genuinely stunning sunsets. Come in late afternoon to watch the sun drop right between the two rocky arms of the bay — one of the finest moments on the south coast. The beach holds a Blue Flag label.

To escape the crowds on the main beach and take in some scenery, walk to Red Beach, reachable in 15 to 20 minutes along a coastal trail from the far end of Matala beach. Its reddish sand — formed from iron-rich sandstone eroding off the surrounding cliffs — creates a striking contrast with the blue of the Libyan Sea. The cove is quieter because it weeds out the less motivated: bring proper shoes, the path is rocky. It's also one of the most popular nudist beaches on this coast.

If you still have energy, drive to the Palace of Phaistos, 11 km east — the second-largest Minoan palace in Crete after Knossos, built around 2000 BC. What made the site world-famous: the Phaistos Disc, a clay tablet covered in spiral symbols discovered in 1908 that no one has managed to decipher to this day. Go in the late afternoon: the low-angle light over the Messara plain is gorgeous, and the crowds thin out noticeably compared to midday.

Take some time to wander the village lanes: psychedelic murals on the facades, driftwood sculptures, cafés where Bob Marley plays late into the night. The hippie soul lives on in tourist form, but the vibe remains laid-back — you won't find the designer shops of Mykonos here. Every June, a three-day festival of seventies rock and reggae takes over the beach to keep the myth alive. If you're around at that time, don't miss it.

activities.text: Snorkeling around the rocks at the edge of Matala bay offers clear water and diverse marine life — grouper, octopus, sea urchins. The best spots are at the southern end of the beach, where the cliff plunges straight into the sea. Bring your own gear if you can — rental shops on site aren't always open outside peak season.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Southern Crete: Gorges, Deserted Beaches & End-of-the-World Villages
Southwest Crete Road Trip: Gorges and Beaches
Grand Circuit of Crete

visits.text: Messolonghi is the city of heroes. In 1826, after two successive sieges, the exhausted Greek garrison attempted a desperate breakout against Ottoman besiegers—the Exodus of Messolonghi. More than 5,000 people, soldiers and civilians, perished. The Garden of Heroes, created in 1830 by Governor Kapodistrias, holds their graves and that of poet Lord Byron, who died here in April 1824 of fever, age 36, after financing and supporting the Greek cause. His heart is buried here; his body was returned to England. It has become a pilgrimage site for Greeks.

Just outside town, Kleisova Lagoon is a remarkable ecosystem: 320 km² of lagoons and salt marshes hosting nearly 270 bird species and constituting one of Greece's largest salt reserves. The 'pelades'—small fishermen's houses on stilts in the lagoon—are a unique Mediterranean landscape. The Salt Museum, housed in a restored former salt works, recounts this salt economy that has structured the region since antiquity.

Messolonghi's Museum of Art and History, in a well-restored 1932 neoclassical building, displays archaeological objects from the region (prehistory to Roman era) and a complete section on the 1821-1826 War of Independence with documents, weapons and portraits of combatants. Four Greek prime ministers born in this town signal Messolonghi's importance in modern Greece.

activities.text: A boat ride in Kleisova Lagoon, arranged by local fishermen, is among the circuit's most singular experiences—stilt fisheries, pink flamingos in season and the silence of this water expanse at sunset provide total contrast to the previous days' archaeological sites.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Greece & Gulf Coast

visits.text: The citadel of Methoni is one of the finest fortresses in Greece — 9.3 hectares on a peninsula surrounded by the sea on three sides, cut off from the mainland by an artificial moat. The Venetians built it from 1209 after seizing it from Geoffrey de Villehardouin, turned it into a vital staging post on the route to the East, and held it until 1500 when the Ottomans took it after a bloody siege. At the end of a 70-metre causeway, the octagonal Ottoman Bourtzi tower — topped with a dome — offers stunning views of the sea at sunset. Chateaubriand landed here on 10 August 1806 for his only journey to Greece. Allow at least 2 hours.

The castle of Koroni, 35 km to the east, spreads across 62,000 m² on a low peninsula overlooking the Gulf of Messenia. It's one of the rare medieval castles in Greece that is still inhabited: a few families live inside, olive trees grow within its walls, and the Timios Prodromos convent occupies the original Byzantine acropolis. The church of Agia Sophia, built on the ruins of a temple of Apollo and an early Christian basilica, carries within it every layer of the site's history. The view from the ramparts over the Gulf of Messenia with the snow-capped peaks of Mount Taygetus behind is magnificent.

activities.text: Methoni's beach, east of the castle, is a long strip of fine sand at the foot of the ramparts. The water is clear and shallow. The evening is ideal for a swim facing the illuminated fortress walls — one of the most photographed sunsets in the Peloponnese.

days_plan.itin_title: Western Peloponnese: Olympia, Three Venetian Fortresses and the Wild Mani

visits.text: Milos is the volcanic island par excellence—over 70 beaches, each different in sand color and cliff shape. Kleftiko, on the southwest coast, is the showstopper: ancient white limestone formations riddled with sea caves, accessible only by water. It was a Saracen pirate lair, and the name recalls that (kleftiko = bandits' place). Today, boat excursions depart each morning from Adamas. The caves are swim-accessible from the boat—filtered underwater light creates mesmerizing blue and green hues. Dolphins, turtles, and seals frequent the area.

Adamas, Milos's main port, sits on one of the Mediterranean's largest natural bays. The waterfront is lively and well supplied. From the port, boat excursions connect Kleftiko, Sarakiniko (the lunar scenery of the north), and the sirmata—these colorful rock-carved boat garages on the shore, Milos's symbol.

activities.text: Snorkeling at Kleftiko: the sea caves are swim-accessible from boat excursions. Bring mask and fins. Don't land from your sailboat as access is regulated—daytime anchorage only.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Monemvasia announces itself the moment it appears from the road: a 300-metre-high rock thrust into the Aegean Sea, joined to the mainland by a single 130-metre causeway. Its Greek name — moni emvasia, "single entrance" — says it all. Founded in 583 AD by Laconians fleeing Slavic invasions, it was successively Byzantine, Frankish (withstanding a 3-year siege), Venetian, Ottoman, Venetian again, then Ottoman once more. In 1821 it became one of the first fortresses liberated during the War of Independence. Pass through the main gate and let the shadowy cobbled lanes unfold before you.

The church of Christos Elkomenos (Christ in Chains) is the cathedral of the lower town, founded in the 13th century by the Byzantines, converted into a mosque under Ottoman rule, then restored to Orthodox worship after 1821. Its Venetian bell tower is a hallmark of the layered influences that define this rock. Since 2011 it has housed one of the most important Byzantine icons in Greece: a 14th-century Crucifixion that had been stolen, recovered, and displayed for years at the Byzantine Museum in Athens.

Climb to the upper town via the paved path that winds along the cliff face — allow 30 to 45 minutes. Be warned: the cobblestones are slippery, especially on the way down. Along the way you'll pass the remains of a 17th-century Ottoman hammam before reaching the Church of Agia Sofia, built in the 12th century under Andronikos I Komnenos, right at the cliff's edge. Successively Orthodox, a Venetian Catholic convent, then Orthodox again, it embodies the layered history of the rock. The view from its forecourt over the Aegean Sea and the Peloponnese is arguably the finest panorama in all of the southern Peloponnese.

The archaeological museum, set inside a 16th-century Ottoman mosque in the lower town, displays artefacts that trace the city's story: mosaics, sculpted fragments, everyday objects from its many successive occupations. The building itself — one of the few surviving mosques in the Peloponnese — is worth a look in its own right.

activities.text: Lose yourself in the lanes of the lower town without a map — there's only one main commercial street (Mesi Odos) and a tangle of alleys that all lead either to the sea or to the cliff. Pick up local olive oil, Malvasia wine or mead from the small shops. At dusk, once the day-trippers have left, Monemvasia reveals its most authentic self.

Pori beach sits just outside the old town, on the north side of the rock — reachable on foot in a few minutes. Small and well-sheltered, it offers crystal-clear water with a view of the fortress walls. If you want more space, Plaka beach is a little further along the coast. Swimming here is ideal around midday, either before or after the climb to the upper town.

Malvasia wine is one of the great wine stories of the Mediterranean. The Malvasia grape takes its name from Monemvasia itself — it was through this fortress port that the Venetians shipped the sweet, fragrant wine to the rest of Europe throughout the Middle Ages. Legend has it that George Plantagenet, sentenced to death in 1478 by his brother Edward IV, chose to be drowned in a barrel of Malvasia — Shakespeare immortalised the scene in Richard III. Local winemakers have revived production on its original terroir. You can taste the wines in the old town's shops.

Patatato is Laconia's Sunday family dish — meat slow-braised in a clay pot with potatoes, local wine and tomatoes. Gongues, handmade pasta similar to gnocchi but made with flour, and saitia — fried cheese pies with fresh herbs — are local specialties you won't find anywhere else. Settle in for dinner at one of Monemvasia's tavernas.

days_plan.itin_title: Laconia Road Trip: Sparta, Mystras & Monemvasia
Greece Grand Tour: 21 Days from North to South

visits.text: Monodendri perches at 1,060 meters altitude at the southern entrance to the Vikos Gorge. The Agia Paraskevi monastery, clinging to the cliff face 800 m from the village via a paved path, was built in 1412 by local lord Michael Therianos in gratitude to the saint for curing his daughter. From its terraces, the gorge floor—900 meters below—stares straight back at you. Take care: there's no guardrail at the precipice edge.

The Oxia viewpoint, 7.5 km north of the village via a drivable track, offers the best view of the gorge from the Monodendri side. On the way, you pass through the 'stone forest'—a field of limestone columns eroded over millennia, some reaching several meters high. Once at the viewpoint, you gaze straight down into the vertiginous chasm: the narrowest point of the gorge is barely a few hundred meters wide and over 1,000 m deep.

The Vikos Gorge is listed in the Guinness Book of Records as the world's deepest canyon relative to its width: 20 km long, walls rising to 1,000 meters, and a width sometimes reduced to just a few hundred meters. The classic trail descends from Monodendri to the village of Vikos along the Voidomatis River—allow 5 to 7 hours depending on pace. Vikos-Aoos National Park, which encompasses the gorge, is a UNESCO Geopark hosting over 1,800 plant species and 186 bird species, including golden eagles and griffon vultures.

The Vradeto steps, starting from Kapesovo, are a masterpiece of engineering: roughly 1,100 steps carved into the cliff face of the Mezaria canyon over a 400-meter elevation gain. Until 1970, this was the only access to the village of Vradeto, perched at 1,340 meters—the highest village in Zagori. The ascent takes 45 minutes to an hour and offers increasingly spectacular views of the gorge.

activities.text: For a first taste of the gorge without crossing it completely, descend the trail from Monodendri to the Voidomatis riverbed via the old kalderimi (paved path). The descent takes 1h30, the river is at the bottom—ice-cold water even in August (rarely above 8°C). The return via the same path is more demanding. The turquoise karst springs halfway down make a perfect rest stop.

Late in the day, wander the lanes of Monodendri and neighboring villages Vitsa and Ano Pedina. All houses are built from local stone and roofed with stone slabs—Zagori's designation as a national historical monument prohibits any other materials. Tavernas serve pites (savory pies) with wild mountain mushrooms and grilled local lamb.

The complete traverse of Vikos Gorge from Monodendri to the village of Vikos is one of Greece's great hikes. The O12 trail is marked but requires good fitness and hiking boots. Once at the bottom, you follow the Voidomatis between limestone walls covered with forests. Stop at the Voidomatis karst springs: water surges from the limestone in an unreal turquoise blue, perfect for an ice-cold swim after the effort. It's possible to arrange a return transfer from Vikos village.

days_plan.itin_title: Epirus & Zagori: Gorges, Stone Villages and the River of the Underworld
Epirus & Zagori: Gorges, Stone Villages and the River of the Underworld

visits.text: Chora, Mykonos's capital, is a whitewashed labyrinth said to have been designed deliberately to confuse marauding pirates. Wander its narrow lanes early in the morning before the cruise ship crowds descend on the port—after 10 a.m., the town belongs to the masses. The whitewashed houses with blue doors and balconies overflowing with bougainvillea set the quintessential Cycladic scene.

The seven windmills of Kato Mili overlook the port of Alefkandra—built in the 16th century by the Venetians to grind wheat shipped from neighboring islands, they turned thanks to the Meltemi, the north wind that blows nearly 300 days a year over Mykonos. Go in late afternoon when the orange light hits their whitewashed walls—it's the island's postcard shot. Watch out for gusts if you climb up to them; the wind can catch you off guard.

Little Venice gets its name from the 18th-century sea captains' houses built right at the water's edge, their colorful balconies practically dipping into the Aegean. It's one of the few places in the Cyclades where you can have sunset drinks with your feet almost in the waves. If you're here in the evening, book a table ahead in summer—the terraces fill up by 7 p.m.

Panagia Paraportiani is an oddity across the entire archipelago: not one church but five, built between 1425 and the 17th century, stacked and melded together to form a uniquely asymmetrical white mass. Listed as a Greek historical monument since 1936, it's usually closed inside—but it's the exterior you come to see. Visit at different times of day; the rounded forms change dramatically with the light.

The Archaeological Museum of Mykonos holds a selection of the ceramics and sculptures recovered from the island of Delos during excavations—notably amphorae, red-figure vases, and some funerary stelae. If you're planning to visit Delos the next day, this museum provides a good preview to help you understand what you'll see on-site.

Delos, reachable in 30 minutes by boat from Mykonos's old port, is one of the Mediterranean's most important archaeological sites: the entire 3.5 km² island has been a UNESCO site since 1990, and no modern construction is permitted. According to Greek mythology, this is where Apollo and Artemis were born. The French School of Athens has been excavating here since 1873, and only a third of the site has been uncovered. Plan at least 3 hours on-site.

The Terrace of the Lions is Delos's most famous image: a row of marble lions from Naxos, offered by the Naxians in the 7th century BC to guard Apollo's sanctuary. Originally numbering 16, only 5 replicas remain on-site—the originals, worn by erosion, are preserved in the island's museum. One lion that ended up in Venice still sits outside the Arsenal, where it's been since the 17th century.

The Hellenistic houses in the theater quarter preserve some of the finest floor mosaics in the Greek world, notably the famous mosaic of Dionysus on a panther. These mansions belonging to wealthy 2nd-century BC merchants give a concrete sense of Delos at the height of its glory—one of the most active cosmopolitan ports in the Mediterranean.

The ancient theater of Delos, built between 314 and 250 BC, could seat between 5,000 and 6,500 spectators. Sit in the marble tiers and imagine the festivals honoring Apollo that took place here—the Delia, a panhellenic festival comparable to the Olympic Games, occurred every four years.

Mount Kynthos rises to 113 meters—not a major climb, but the view from the summit sweeps across the entire island and the surrounding Cyclades on clear days. This, according to tradition, is where Apollo was born. A sanctuary dedicated to Zeus and Athena once stood at the top. Bring good shoes—the path is rocky.

activities.text: Stroll through Chora's lanes in the morning to catch the fish market near the old port—fishermen sell their dawn catch there at prices far more reasonable than in the tavernas. It's one of the last authentic moments of the town before mass tourism takes over.

The beaches of Paradise and Super Paradise, about ten kilometers south of Chora, are the island's party playgrounds: loud music, beach clubs, international vibe. Buses run there from Chora in summer. If you're after more peace, head to Agrari just next door—fine sand, fewer people, water just as clear.

In the evening, settle into Little Venice for aperitivo, then explore Chora on foot: luxury boutiques rub shoulders with jewelers and art galleries in a maze designed for getting lost—and that's intentional. Mykonos's nightlife is legendary across the Mediterranean; if that's your thing, the night here rarely starts before midnight.
Try kopanisti, Mykonos's sharp PDO cheese—a fermented soft paste with an intense flavor, spread on toasted bread with a drizzle of olive oil. You'll find it in local groceries and some tavernas.
Louza, Mykonos's spiced dried pork charcuterie, is eaten as mezze with cheese and olives. It's a Cycladic tradition you won't easily find elsewhere in Greece—ask for it in the old port tavernas.

Take the boat early in the morning from Mykonos's old port—first shuttles depart around 9 a.m. The site closes at 3 p.m. in low season, so check the hours before departing. There's no restaurant or water for sale on the island once the site opens: bring water and a snack, as the sun is merciless in summer.

The Archaeological Museum of Delos, built in 1904 by the Archaeological Society of Athens and expanded twice since, houses the originals of the terrace lions, a 6th-century BC kouros torso, and a collection of mosaics, jewelry, and inscriptions. Allow 45 minutes for the nine rooms.

Amygdalota, small almond cookies coated in powdered sugar, are Mykonos's signature sweet, made in the monastic tradition. You'll find them in artisan bakeries in Chora when you return from Delos—they keep for several days.

visits.text: Mystras is one of the great revelations of the Peloponnese: an entire Byzantine city, built amphitheatre-like across the slopes of Mount Taygetus at 620 metres above sea level, founded in 1249 by William II of Villehardouin and finally abandoned in 1832. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1989, it was at its peak the second city of the Byzantine Empire after Constantinople, home to 20,000 inhabitants. The philosopher George Gemistos Plethon, who taught here in the 15th century, directly influenced the Italian Renaissance. Get there early in the morning — the climb is tough in the heat.

The Metropolis (Cathedral of Agios Dimitrios), founded from 1264 onwards, is the first major building you'll encounter when entering through the lower gate. Its 13th- and 14th-century frescoes adorning the pillars and walls are remarkably refined. On the marble floor, a carved double-headed eagle marks the exact spot where Constantine XI Palaiologos — the last emperor of Byzantium — was crowned in 1449, just two years before the fall of Constantinople.

The Monastery of Peribleptos, literally clinging to the rock face inside a hollow in the cliff, is the site's best-kept treasure. Its 14th-century frescoes — particularly the Christ Pantocrator surrounded by the Virgin and the Apostles in the dome — possess a rare dramatic intensity and rank among the masterpieces of late Byzantine art. The atmosphere inside is almost mystical: deep shadow, colours that emerge gradually as your eyes adjust, and the smell of damp stone.

The Monastery of Pantanassa, inaugurated in September 1428, is the last building erected on the site and the only one still inhabited — a small community of Orthodox nuns lives here and tends the grounds. Its intricately carved stone facade and 15th-century frescoes in vivid, striking colours are breathtaking. The nuns welcome visitors with quiet discretion. Modest dress required — cover your shoulders and knees.

The Palace of the Despots, in the upper town, is the most imposing palatial complex on the site. Its two recognisable wings — the Cantacuzene wing and the Palaiologos wing — speak to the power of the governors of the Morea. On the second floor of the Palaiologos wing stands the throne room known as the Chrysotriklinos. From the terraces, the view across the plain of Sparta and the Eurotas valley is stunning. Climb further to the Villehardouin fortress at the very top for a full 360° panorama.

activities.text: Plan to spend the entire day on the site: a thorough visit of the lower town (Metropolis, Peribleptos, Brontochion) and the upper town (Palace, Pantanassa, fortress) takes roughly 3 to 4 hours of walking with significant elevation gain. Wear sturdy closed-toe shoes — the cobblestones are uneven and can be slippery. Admission is just a few euros.

In the evening, the plain of Sparta and the peaks of Taygetus are magnificent when seen from the heights of Mystras. If you're staying nearby (a handful of guesthouses sit at the foot of the site), the early morning hours before the gates open offer a special atmosphere, as golden light begins to stream through the Byzantine windows and illuminate the frescoes.

Order the beef kokkinisto for dinner — a slow-cooked stew simmered in tomato, onion and red wine sauce, a Peloponnesian classic. For dessert, try diples: delicate ribbons of fried dough drizzled with honey and crushed walnuts, the festive sweet of Laconia. Pair it all with a glass of Xinomavro from Naoussa, a bold, tannic northern Greek red that holds its own against hearty stews.

days_plan.itin_title: Laconia Road Trip: Sparta, Mystras & Monemvasia

visits.text: Naoussa concentrates all of Paros's charm in one village. The fishing harbor is ringed by whitewashed lanes climbing toward the Kastro—a 15th-century Venetian fortification partially submerged, its round tower still standing guard at the harbor entrance. The old Venetian warehouses have transformed into elegant restaurants serving the morning catch. Naoussa actually boasts the largest fishing fleet in all the Cyclades: watch fishermen mending their nets early in the morning before the terraces fill. Come evening, the kastro lanes morph into an open-air lounge.

Kolymbithres, 4 km northwest of Naoussa, is Paros's most photographed spot. Giant granite boulders, polished by millennia of wind and sea spray, have carved out natural basins filled with translucent saltwater—somewhere between swimming pools and stone sculptures. The water often sits less than a meter deep and takes on milky-blue reflections against the pearl-gray stone. In July–August, the pools fill by 11 a.m.: arrive before 10 for first dibs or come late in the day. Caïques make the run from Naoussa harbor.

activities.text: Anchorage at Naoussa marina or Ormos Plastira depending on conditions—good holding on sandy bottom, sheltered from the north.
Snorkeling in the coves of Kolymbithres: the granite formations create tunnels and underwater cavities accessible with just a mask. Colorful fish guaranteed.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Naupacte's port is among Greece's most picturesque—a circular basin ringed by 15th-century Venetian ramparts, with a lighthouse, century-old plane trees and cafés spilling onto the quays. Here, under its Venetian name of Lepanto, occurred on October 7, 1571 the greatest galley-battle in history: the Holy League (Spain, Venice, Rome) versus the Ottoman fleet. 300 ships on one side, 330 on the other. The Ottoman fleet was destroyed—187 of its 251 vessels sunk, over 20,000 dead. Among the Holy League soldiers was a 23-year-old Spanish youth named Miguel de Cervantes, who took two arquebus shots to the chest and lost use of his left hand. His statue now stands on the port.

The climb to the Venetian citadel is steep but rewarding: walls descend from the hilltop in several concentric rings to the sea, a defensive system among Greece's best-preserved. From the summit, views encompass the Gulf of Corinth, the cable-stayed Rio-Antirio Bridge (2004, one of the world's longest with 2,252 meters of suspended span and 2,880 meters total) and on clear days, the Peloponnese mountains. The Botsaris Tower on the ascent is a 15th-century structure housing a museum dedicated to the Battle of Lepanto.

activities.text: The coastal road from Galaxidi to Naupacte hugs the Gulf of Corinth for 70 km—one of mainland Greece's finest drives, between sea and wooded cliffs. Psani and Gribovo beaches at Naupacte hold Blue Flags and the water is excellent. For evening, the port terraces beneath plane trees are ideal for seafood dinner—dried octopi hanging on washing lines above fishing boats epitomize the port.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Greece & Gulf Coast

visits.text: The Palamidi Fortress towers over Nafplio from 216 metres above sea level. The Venetians built it between 1711 and 1714 under the direction of two French engineers, Lasalle and Levasseur — but the Ottomans under Ali Douad Pasha captured it as early as 1715, before it was even finished, slaughtering the entire garrison. Climb the stairs from the old town: legend says 999 steps, though actual counts range from 857 to 913. Bring water and avoid midday in summer. The view from the ramparts over the Argolic Gulf, the rooftops below and the tiny Bourtzi island is the finest panorama in the eastern Peloponnese.

The Bourtzi is the small fortress perched on the islet at the harbour mouth, built by the Venetians in 1473 under the name Castello dello Soglio. Its history is worth a closer look: a chain stretching from Akronafplia to the islet once blocked enemy ships from entering the port — hence the Venetian nickname Porto Cadena. The Ottomans renamed it Bourtzi ("fortress"). Later, it served as the residence of the executioner from the Palamidi prison — a character the town wisely kept at arm's length. Small boats leave from the harbour roughly every 30 minutes in season.

Nafplio's old town may be compact, but its Venetian alleyways, stone staircases and cascading bougainvillea make it one of the loveliest towns in the Peloponnese. Nafplio was the first capital of independent Greece in 1828 — it was here that Ioannis Kapodistrias, the country's first governor, was assassinated on the steps of the Church of Agios Spyridon in 1831. The commemorative plaque is still visible. Don't miss Syntagma Square with its cafés tucked under arcaded buildings: this is where the first legislators of modern Greece held their sessions.

The Nafplio Archaeological Museum, housed in an 18th-century Venetian building on Syntagma Square, holds Mycenaean, Geometric and Classical collections from across the Argolid. Its star exhibit is a nearly complete Mycenaean bronze suit of armour dated to the 14th century BC, discovered at Dendra near Midea — one of the rarest surviving Bronze Age suits of armour in the world. Allow about 45 minutes.

Mycenae lies 25 km from Nafplio — an easy half-day trip from your base. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1999, it sits at the heart of the Mycenaean civilisation that dominated the eastern Mediterranean from the 15th to the 12th century BC. Right at the entrance, the Lion Gate greets you: built around 1250 BC, this monumental doorway is topped by an 18-tonne lintel and a relief of two lionesses in heraldic pose flanking a column. It is the oldest monumental sculpture in Europe, and the original bronze heads vanished in antiquity. Wear sturdy shoes — the site is steep and the stones are polished slippery by the centuries.

Inside the Cyclopean walls, Grave Circle A is where the self-taught archaeologist Heinrich Schliemann unearthed royal tombs in 1876, finding gold death masks, inlaid swords and jewellery of staggering richness. It was in Shaft Grave V that he found the famous so-called "Mask of Agamemnon" — actually predating the Homeric king by three centuries, as we now know, but no matter: the original is in the National Archaeological Museum in Athens, and the replica in the Mycenae museum is well worth seeing.

The Treasury of Atreus, a few hundred metres from the citadel, is one of the most audacious structures of the ancient world. This tholos (beehive) tomb dates to around 1250 BC — its dromos (entrance corridor) stretches 36 metres long and 6 metres wide. Once past the threshold (the lower lintel alone weighs 120 tonnes), you step into a circular chamber 14.6 metres across and 13.5 metres high, built entirely by corbelling. This dome remained the largest in the world until the Pantheon was raised in Rome in the 2nd century AD. It had already been looted in antiquity — but the sheer scale of the space is breathtaking.

The Mycenae Museum, opened in 2007, stands at the entrance to the site. Modern and well-documented, it displays painted Mycenaean pottery, bronze weapons, jewellery and terracotta figurines recovered from local excavations. Look out in particular for the Linear B tablets — this syllabic script, the earliest form of written Greek, was only deciphered in 1952 by the British architect Michael Ventris.

activities.text: Climb to the Palamidi Fortress on foot from the gate on Arvanitia Street in the old town. Start early in the morning (before 9 am in summer) to avoid the heat and catch the soft morning light over the gulf. If you'd rather spare your knees, the eastern access road (3.6 km by car) drops you right at the entrance.

Stroll along Arvanitia Beach, a 10-minute walk from the old town along the coastal path beneath the Akronafplia fortress. It's a small pebble beach popular with locals, with transparent water and a direct view up to the cliffs and ramparts. Ideal for a late-afternoon swim after a day of sightseeing.

Dinner in the old town — order the youvetsi: lamb braised slowly with orzo pasta in a thick tomato sauce, baked in a clay pot. It's the classic celebratory dish of the Argolid. Pair it with a red agiorgitiko from Nemea — the signature grape of the Peloponnese, velvety and fruity, grown just 40 km away.

Explore the Mycenaean acropolis: climb to the royal palace at the top of the hill. The megaron (great hall) is recognisable by its four column bases. From up here, the view sweeps across the Plain of Argos and, on a clear day, all the way to the Argolic Gulf — a reminder of why this spot was chosen: absolute strategic command over the entire plain. The Cyclopean walls, up to 8 metres thick in places, are built of blocks so massive that the Classical Greeks attributed them to the Cyclopes — the only beings strong enough to lift them.

On the way back to Nafplio, stop at Tiryns, the other Mycenaean citadel inscribed by UNESCO (just 5 km from the centre of Nafplio). Less dramatic than Mycenae but boasting even better-preserved Cyclopean walls up to 8 metres thick. The interior corbelled galleries — carved entirely from unmortared blocks — rank among the most astonishing feats of prehistoric Greek architecture.

Try the real thing: homemade tzatziki in an old-town taverna in Nafplio — thick Greek yoghurt, grated and drained cucumber, garlic and dill — fresher and lighter than the tourist versions. Served as a mezze with warm pita bread and Kalamata olives, it's the opening ritual that signals a good evening ahead.

visits.text: Naxos' Portara is visible from the ferry long before docking: a massive marble doorway perched on Palatia islet at the port entrance. Its four blocks each weigh 20 tons - too heavy for the Venetians to move, which explains why it's the only standing remnant of Apollo's temple begun around 530 BC under tyrant Lygdamis and never completed. According to mythology, this islet is where Theseus abandoned Ariadne. Go at sunset: the sun descends exactly through the doorway's axis.

Chora Naxos' Kastro, founded in 1207 by Marco Sanudo, first Duke of Naxos, dominates the port from a hill. Its outer walls are formed by the backs of Venetian palaces, many retaining their coats of arms. Inside: the 13th-century Catholic cathedral, an archaeological museum housed in the former 1627 Jesuit lyceum - where Nikos Kazantzakis, author of Zorba the Greek, was a student - and a Venetian museum.

The Temple of Demeter at Sangri, 15 km south of Chora, is a 6th-century BC Doric temple partially reconstructed with its original materials. Small site museum with carved fragments found. The place is quiet and non-touristy - worth seeing if you have a vehicle.

Halki, the island's former capital, is a marble village in the heart of Tragea plain, surrounded by olive groves and vineyards. Its main square is animated by a few tavernas and the Vallindras distillery, producing kitron since 1896 - a unique liqueur distilled from the leaves of a particular citrus tree that grows only in Naxos.

Apiranthos is one of Naxos' best-preserved villages: alleyways entirely paved with marble, Cycladic stone houses, four small museums. The inhabitants largely descend from Cretan refugees who arrived in the 17th century. Don't leave without stopping at the café on the main square - local life unfolds there effortlessly.

The Kouros of Apollonas, at the island's northern tip, is an 11-meter, 80-ton giant statue abandoned in its quarry in the 7th century BC - probably due to a crack in the marble. It's lain on its back in the rock for over 2,700 years. Access is free and open, roadside.

activities.text: The beaches on Naxos' southwest coast - Agios Prokopios, Agia Anna, Plaka - are among the Cyclades' finest: fine, pale sand, shallow turquoise water. Agios Prokopios is the most equipped; Plaka stretches 5 km and is wilder. All are accessible by bus from Chora.

Arseniko (Naxos graviera), a hard aged cheese with powerful flavor, is produced under PDO on the island. Taste it with local honey at Chora cheese shops. Naxos potatoes - small, flavorful, PDO - are renowned throughout Greece: roasted with olive oil and lemon, they accompany all taverna dishes.

The route between Chora, Halki, Apiranthos and Apollonas crosses the mountainous heart of Naxos - olive trees, vineyards, marble villages and Aegean Sea views. It's the best way to see the island in its reality. Allow half a day with stops.

Aliko beach, southwest of the island, is one of Naxos' most isolated and wild: fine sand, dunes covered with cedars, crystal-clear waters and very few people. A sand path leads through the dunes to the water's edge. Bring supplies - there's nothing on site.

Late afternoon, return to Chora for the Portara at sunset - it's even more beautiful than on arrival, from the pedestrian causeway crossing the port. Then settle into a waterfront taverna for your last evening in Naxos.

Cyclades Journey: Athens, Santorini, Mykonos & Naxos
Cyclades Journey: Athens, Santorini, Mykonos & Naxos

visits.text: The Portara greets you at the entrance to Naxos harbor—this monumental white marble gateway standing 6 meters tall is all that remains of a temple dedicated to Apollo, whose construction began around 530 BC under the tyrant Lygdamis. The building was never completed, and the Venetians carted off all the blocks to build the kastro, except these four 20-ton giants, too heavy to budge. The Portara faces toward Delos, Apollo's birthplace, and legend places Theseus's abandonment of Ariadne here. Sunset from the causeway is a popular classic—for crowd-free photos, go early in the morning.

The Kastro of Naxos Chora was erected in 1207 by Marco Sanudo, the Doge of Venice's nephew, after the Fourth Crusade. He moved the island's capital from the heights down to the coast. The inner precinct, accessible only through two gates—one still sporting its original 13th-century wooden door—shelters noble residences bearing carved Venetian coats of arms, the Catholic cathedral, the archaeological museum housed in the former Jesuit school (Nikos Kazantzákis studied here), and vaulted lanes nearly deserted. A time warp 15 minutes on foot from the harbor.

The Bourgos quarter, between the harbor and the Kastro, packs in artisan workshops, marble studios, tavernas, and galleries. Naxos has been a marble island since antiquity—the unfinished kouros statues abandoned in the quarries during the Archaic period still bear witness. Push open the workshop doors: sculptors still work the local white stone.

activities.text: Sunset facing the Portara from anchorage or the islet causeway—the white marble turns amber, the sea turns gold. One of the Aegean's most photographed spectacles.

Waterfront stroll and provisioning at the harbor market: Naxos is the largest island in the Cyclades and its hinterland produces renowned cheeses, honey, citron, and potatoes. The market is well stocked.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Stefanos Crater, at the center of Nisyros's caldera, is one of the Aegean's most striking geological experiences. The caldera measures 4 km in diameter and Stefanos itself spans 300 meters across and 30 meters deep. The last phreatic eruption occurred in 1888, but hydrothermal activity is very present: sulfur fumaroles, ground hot and yellow underfoot, bubbling mud pots—all with a frank rotten-egg odor. Arrive before 9 am: in peak season, up to 1,000 day-trippers from Kos land in the morning. Closed shoes mandatory—the ground burns in places.

Mandraki, Nisyros's capital, is a compact village with white houses and lanes paved with pebble mosaics—a technique you'll find in squares and even private courtyards. The archaeological museum covers more than 3,000 years of island history, from the Neolithic to the Byzantine era. The waterfront with its tavernas comes alive in the evening, when the day-trippers have departed and the island regains its calm.

The Monastery of Panagia Spiliani—'Our Lady of the Cave'—is built directly inside the ruins of the Knights of Saint John castle, on a 30-meter rock above Mandraki. Access is via a staircase of more than 130 steps. The monastery is partly carved into natural rock: the atmosphere inside the cave-sanctuary, cool and dark, contrasts with the blinding whiteness of the Aegean. The 1725 iconostasis and library preserve manuscripts and liturgical objects of great value. From August 6-15, celebrations for the Dormition of the Virgin draw pilgrims from across the Dodecanese.

Nikia, perched on the caldera's rim, is one of Greece's most photogenic villages. Its central square is entirely paved with pebble mosaics, bordered by colorful-shuttered houses. From here, the plunging view onto Stefanos Crater below is vertiginous—this is the ideal spot to grasp the volcano's scale. Two small tavernas serve on the square.

Paleokastro, Mandraki's ancient acropolis, is remarkably well-preserved from the Classical era (6th-4th century BC). Its black volcanic stone walls run nearly 2 km in perimeter and some blocks weigh several tons. From the ramparts, the view embraces the port, the sea and neighboring islands. The climb takes about twenty minutes from Mandraki.

activities.text: Hike between Nikia and the caldera on the trail that descends from the village rim to the crater floor—about 45 minutes down on a marked path that crosses landscapes of yellow sulfur and black rock. You transition from a white village with colored shutters to a near-lunar landscape in under an hour. The ascent is strenuous: count on 1 hour and bring water.

The baths at Loutra, 1 km from Mandraki on the road to Pali, exploit sulfurous hot spring waters. The old-fashioned 19th-century thermal station has something quaintly authentic that grand spa complexes have lost. The waters are reputed for joints and skin. Access is free for outdoor baths.

'Soumada,' a beverage made from bitter almond extract mixed with cold water, is Nisyros's signature non-alcoholic specialty—refreshing and fragrant. 'Pitia,' focaccia topped with tomatoes, onions, olives and herbs, is sold in Mandraki bakeries from morning. For a heartier meal, 'Bogana' (lamb braised with garlic and potatoes) and 'Kapamas' (goat stuffed with rice and cooked in tomato sauce) are the island's two traditional main dishes.

Emborios, semi-abandoned on the caldera rim, has been reinventing itself in recent years: ruined houses are being restored one by one by artists and Athenians. A small café terrace overlooks the volcano directly—it's one of the island's most dramatic viewpoints. Come in the evening when raking light makes the fumaroles smoke.

days_plan.itin_title: Dodecanese Circuit: Patmos, Leros & Karpathos

places.name: Oia

visits.text: Oia - pronounced 'ee-ah', not 'oya' - deserves an entire morning before tourists arrive. What you traverse isn't a medieval village: it's a rebuilt merchant town. In 1890, Oia had 130 ships and its own shipyard, trading between Russia and Alexandria. The 1956 earthquake destroyed everything and emptied the village, whose residents left for Piraeus. The renaissance dates only to the 1980s. Come to walk the lanes before 9am: the atmosphere is completely different from what you'll find afterwards.

The Castle of Agios Nikolaos, also called the Kastro, is the 15th-century Venetian ruin perched at Oia's north-western tip. Built by the Dargent family, descendants of Byzantine emperor Romanos Argyros, it served as an observation point over the caldeira. The 1956 earthquake toppled most of it, but the remaining wall panels are enough to offer Greece's most famous sunset. Arrive at least an hour before sunset for a good spot - crowds gather here every summer evening. Access is free.

Push open the door of Oia's Maritime Museum, housed in an old captain's mansion tucked down an alley. Ground floor: navigation instruments and maritime equipment; upstairs: uniforms, shipowner portraits and impressive ship models. It's modest, but it explains better than any guide why Oia was a commercial powerhouse in the eastern Mediterranean - and gives meaning to the 'captain's houses' you'll see throughout the village.

The Church Panagia Platsani is one of Oia's oldest and probably Santorini's most photographed, with its characteristic blue domes silhouetted against the caldeira. Push the door open if it's unlocked - tradition says it shelters a miraculous icon of the Virgin retrieved from the caldeira. Steps away, the Atlantis Books library, run by volunteers, is regularly cited among the world's most beautiful bookstores: a detour is worthwhile even if you buy nothing.

Descend the 300 steep steps to Ammoudi port - it's a walk not to be missed, even if the return climb is warm work. At the bottom you'll find a tiny fishing haven with red cliffs, coloured boats and tavernas serving this morning's catch. You can also swim from the rocks at the port entrance: the water is absolutely clear. For the climb back, donkeys await you at the foot of the steps if your knees have a say.

activities.text: Oia's sunset is a mass phenomenon: every summer evening, hundreds of people crowd the Kastro with selfie sticks. If you want the spectacle without the battle, opt for a taverna terrace set back from the main street, or head down to Ammoudi - the view is almost as fine and the atmosphere far more serene. Outside high season, the Kastro remains the best spot.

The Fira-Oia hike edges the caldeira for roughly 10 km. It crosses Firostefani and Imerovigli before reaching Oia, with permanent views of the volcanoes and white villages clinging to the cliffs. Allow 3 to 4 hours depending on pace. Start early, bring water and sunscreen - there's virtually no shade on the trail.

Venture into Oia's secondary lanes, far from the main street and its generic souvenir shops. You'll find open artist studios and art galleries where local painters display watercolours of the caldeira - a more personal souvenir than a fridge magnet. Take your time: these lanes are far more photogenic than the high street, and almost empty before 10am.

If you dine at Ammoudi, order the grilled octopus: sun-dried on a line in the morning, charred over coals in the evening, it's rarely as good anywhere else on the island. Pair it with a salad of Santorini's wild capers - their piquant, vegetal taste bears no resemblance to the brined capers you know, and their production is also PDO-protected. Avoid tables on the front row of Oia's main street where prices are indexed to the view, not quality.

End the evening with a glass of Vinsanto, Santorini's grand sweet wine. It's produced from Assyrtiko and Aidani grapes dried in the sun for 12 to 14 days after harvest - the sugar concentration is such that fermentation yields an amber, honeyed wine, sometimes aged several decades in barrel. Ask for a vintage of at least 5 years to appreciate its depth. It pairs naturally with honey-based desserts or a fresh cheese board.

days_plan.itin_title: Santorini Road Trip: Caldeira, White Villages and Minoan Sites

map_keywords: Castle of Agios Nikolaos Oia Santorini

visits.text: The archaeological site of Olympia, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1989, is the birthplace of the Olympic Games — held here without interruption from 776 BC to 393 AD, a span of 1,169 years. The River Alpheios flows alongside, and the pines and olive trees lend the site an atmosphere unlike any other major Greek sanctuary. Set aside at least two hours for a full visit. Enter from the east and walk through the Altis (the sacred grove), heading uphill towards the Temple of Zeus.

The Temple of Zeus, built around 460 BC by the architect Libon of Elis, measured 64 metres long by 28 metres wide — one of the largest Doric temples in Greece. All that remains are the columns lying where they fell, toppled by earthquakes in the 6th century AD. Inside once stood the chryselephantine statue of Zeus by Pheidias (c. 435 BC): 12 to 13 metres tall, made of gold and ivory over a wooden frame, and ranked as the third Wonder of the Ancient World. It was taken to Constantinople in the 5th century and destroyed in a fire.

The ancient Olympic Stadium is reached through the vaulted tunnel that athletes once used to make their entrance — slip through this passage and picture the 40,000 spectators waiting on the other side. The track measures 212 metres long by 28 metres wide. The marble starting line is still visible. Give the stadion a go — sprint the full length: this was the blue-ribbon event of the earliest Games.

The Workshop of Pheidias, west of the site, was definitively identified in 1958 thanks to a wine jug inscribed in Greek: "I belong to Pheidias." The workshop's dimensions match the cella of the Temple of Zeus exactly — the statue was first assembled here in separate pieces before being installed inside the temple. A Byzantine church was built over the workshop in the 5th century, then abandoned after the 6th-century earthquakes.

The Archaeological Museum of Olympia ranks among the finest in Greece — some place it on a par with the National Archaeological Museum in Athens for the quality of its collection. It holds the world's largest collection of ancient bronzes. Its star exhibits: the Hermes Carrying the Infant Dionysos by Praxiteles (4th century BC), the Nike of Paionios in Parian marble (2.11 metres tall, the first monumental winged Victory ever sculpted, dated to 421 BC), and the pediments from the Temple of Zeus. Allow at least 1 hour 15 minutes.

activities.text: Run the full length of the Olympic Stadium (212 metres) on the ancient track from the marble starting line: it's the ritual gesture every visitor makes, and it's all the more moving knowing it's exactly the same one athletes performed in the 8th century BC.

An evening stifado in a taverna in the modern village of Olympia: a stew of rabbit (or beef) simmered in red wine sauce with pearl onions, cinnamon and cloves. It's one of the finest slow-cooked dishes in Greek cuisine, and the Elis region has a particularly fragrant version. Pair it with a glass of mavrodaphne from Patras — this sweet red wine, produced just 90 km away, is reminiscent of a light port.

Evening walk along the banks of the Alpheios downstream from the site: the river winds between plane trees and reeds through a landscape free of cars and tourists. This is where ancient athletes came to purify themselves before the Games.

visits.text: The Prosphorion Tower dominates the entrance to Ouranoupoli's harbour — a 14th-century Byzantine fortification built to protect the metochion of Vatopedi Monastery, and one of the best-preserved medieval monuments in the region. It houses a small museum on the history of the Athos peninsula. It's also the rallying point for every cruise to Mount Athos: you'll recognise it by the quay packed with pilgrims and tourists in the morning.

Ouranoupoli — 'City of Heaven' in Greek — is the last town before the border of the Monastic Republic of Mount Athos, an autonomous theocratic state home to some 1,500 monks in 20 monasteries. Women and non-Orthodox laypeople are not permitted to enter (10 non-Orthodox per day, men only, by special permit). From the harbour, though, you can see the foothills of the peninsula rising from the sea.

activities.text: Take the coastal cruise departing from the harbour — boats leave daily at 10:30am to sail along the western coast of the Mount Athos peninsula and observe the monasteries clinging to the cliffs from the sea. The cruise lasts about 3 hours 45 minutes and passes monasteries such as Xenophontos and Simonos Petras. It's open to everyone, men and women, and is the only way for the vast majority of visitors to get close to the site. Book ahead in summer.

In the late afternoon, catch the small ferry to Ammouliani Island, 10 minutes from Ouranoupoli. This island, largely untouched by mass tourism, has crystal-clear waters and secluded beaches — notably Alikes beach, a spectacular crescent of golden sand. The return trip in the late afternoon leaves time for sunset over the harbour.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

visits.text: Take the ferry back to Hora Sfakion in the morning, retrieve your car, and head west through the White Mountains—allow about 2 hours with spectacular views over desert plateaus and gorges. In Paleochora, climb up to see the ruins of Castel Selino, the Venetian fortress built in 1282 by Duke Marino Gradonico. The castle was destroyed in 1539 by the pirate Barbarossa—only sections of walls perched at the tip of the peninsula remain, but the view over the two beaches on either side of the promontory is worth the climb.

10 km northeast of Paleochora, the Monastery of the 99 Holy Fathers of Azogires is partially built into a natural cave—discreet, barely mentioned in guidebooks, and often empty of tourists. Dating from the 19th century, it's surrounded by a centuries-old plane tree that locals claim is the largest on the island. A calm and authentic pause in the Cretan hinterland.

activities.text: Pachia Ammos Beach, west of the Paleochora peninsula, is the town's main beach: fine sand stretching several hundred meters, relatively calm waters on the east side. The town closes its alleys to traffic in the evening and restaurants spill out onto the cobblestones—it's the best time to stroll and dine on a terrace.

For the curious, the archaeological site of Lissos lies in a wild cove accessible by water taxi from Paleochora harbor (10 minutes). This ancient Roman city from the 1st-3rd centuries AD has left ruins scattered under centuries-old olive trees: temple, tombs, Roman baths, and 4th-century mosaics. Few visitors, a pebble beach for swimming after the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Southern Crete: Gorges, Deserted Beaches & End-of-the-World Villages

visits.text: Megalo Papigo and Mikro Papigo are the two most photographed villages in Zagori, separated by 3 km via a hiking trail. Their cobbled lanes, stone-and-slate houses, and centuries-old plane trees on the central square are perfect examples of the Epirote architecture classified as a UNESCO World Heritage site. Look up and you'll see the Astraka Towers dominating the landscape—vertical limestone columns connecting the summit of Mount Tymfi (2,497 m) to the Vikos Gorge.

Near Kipi, the Kalogeriko (or Plakidas) bridge is the iconic three-arched bridge of Zagori. Built in 1814 by Abbot Seraphim from the local monastery to replace a wooden bridge, it spans the Voidomatis River at 56 meters long and 6.7 meters high. Viewed from the surrounding hills, its three successive arches give it the appearance of a caterpillar in motion—a nickname that stuck. A few minutes away, the Kokkoris bridge, built in 1750, is framed by two sheer rock walls: the most spectacular setting in the region.

activities.text: Between Megalo and Mikro Papigo, the trail connecting the two villages passes by the Ovires (or Kolymbithres)—natural pools carved into the limestone rock by the Rogovo River. Legend has it that Ali Pasha himself came here to bathe. In summer, the water is cool and the cascading pools offer a unique swim. The trail easily loops the two villages in 2 hours.

On the return route, do the Kipi bridges loop on foot (10 km, 3 hours) to discover six single-arch stone bridges. The Kipi and Koukouli area has the densest concentration of stone bridges in all of Zagori—every configuration is represented: one, two, and three arches. These bridges were the only link between villages until the first paved roads arrived in the 1950s.

days_plan.itin_title: Epirus & Zagori: Gorges, Stone Villages and the River of the Underworld

visits.text: Parikia's old harbor greets you on arrival with its whitewashed windmills standing sentinel on the breakwater—Cycladic postcard perfection in the flesh. Behind the ferries and bustling quays, the old town weaves through pebble-paved lanes around a Venetian kastro built in 1260 from marble blocks salvaged from a nearby Temple of Athena: look carefully and you'll still spot ancient columns embedded in the walls. The old market follows the main street to the kastro and brims with tavernas, bakeries, and little boutiques. Get lost in this maze—the late afternoon light washing over the white facades is worth more than any guidebook.

The Panagia Ekatontapiliani basilica—'the church of a hundred doors'—ranks as Greece's third-largest early Christian sanctuary. Legend traces its foundation to the 4th century by Saint Helena, mother of Constantine the Great; the present building dates from the 6th century and was restored under Justinian. The complex sits within a 252-meter perimeter wall and shelters a main church measuring 40 by 25 meters, flanked by the oldest baptistery in the Christian East. Tradition holds that the hundredth door remains invisible and will open only when Constantinople is reclaimed. Visit early in the morning—the filtered light in the nave, the Doric columns salvaged from a Roman gymnasium, and the 15th-century iconostasis are worth the stop. It's also a major pilgrimage site for the Assumption, rivaling even Tinos.

Return along Paros's west coast from Antiparos—a discreet passage between the two islands, with the white marble quarries from antiquity visible on the slopes. Parikia rediscovered feels almost a relief after the wild anchorages of recent days: wifi, fresh water, provisions, and the lively Chora for a final dinner facing the sea.

activities.text: Swimming at Livadia Beach, the easiest beach to reach from the harbor—10 minutes on foot heading west. Fine sand, shallow water, perfect for a first plunge after the crossing.
Sheltered anchorage available at Krios Beach depending on conditions, just across from Parikia harbor—dinghy shuttle, good holding on sandy bottom.
Final quick swim depending on timing and weather—Krios beach, dinghy-accessible from anchorage, is perfect for a farewell plunge in still-turquoise water.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves
Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Panagia Ekatontapiliani—"the church of a hundred doors"—is the Cyclades' largest early Christian sanctuary and Greece's third largest. According to tradition, Saint Helen, mother of Emperor Constantine, vowed to build one during her stop at Paros in the 4th century. The current structure dates to the 6th century under Justinian. Its name comes from a legend: the church has 99 visible doors, and the hundredth will open the day Constantinople becomes Greek again. Don't miss the 4th-century baptistery attached to the church—it's the oldest and best-preserved in the Christian East.

Parikia's Kastro, built in the 13th century by Marco Sanudo, perches on the hill overlooking the port. Its walls are made from blocks of columns and lintels salvaged from ancient temples on the site—look carefully climbing the steps and you'll still see the column drums. The view from the top sweeps across the entire bay of Parikia.

Naoussa, in the island's north, is Paros's most charming fishing port: its white houses cascade down to the water, its tavernas spill onto the quay, and fishermen's caiques bob in the bay. A small 14th-century Venetian kastro guards the harbor entrance—it's half in the water, giving it a singular appearance.

Lefkes, the largest inland village, was Paros's capital during the Ottoman era. Its white marble lanes, blue-shuttered houses, and two grand churches make it one of the Cyclades' most beautiful villages. An 8th-century Byzantine marble path, partially restored, links Lefkes to neighboring villages.

The Valley of the Butterflies (Petaloudes), in the island's center, is a nature reserve where thousands of moths (Callimorpha quadripunctaria) gather each summer on the valley's damp trees. It's an unusual spectacle for the typically arid Cyclades.

activities.text: Kolymbithres beach, north of Naoussa, owes its charm to granite rock formations sculpted by erosion into coves and natural pools. The water is calm, deep blue, and the rocks create ideal underwater topography for snorkeling. Accessible by water taxi from Naoussa or by car.

Revythada is a signature Paros dish: chickpeas cooked very slowly in a potter's oven for hours, with no seasoning beyond olive oil and salt. The result is creamy, tender. Ask for it in Parikia or Naoussa tavernas—some only serve it on Sundays.

Gouna is a fishermen's specialty: mackerel split in half and sun-dried for several days, then grilled over charcoal. It's a typical Paros mezze, eaten with a glass of ouzo while gazing at the sea. Look for it in the port tavernas of Naoussa or Parikia.

Paros is one of the world's top kitesurfing destinations, with Golden Beach (Chryssi Akti) leading the way—the wind is consistent and predictable thanks to the Meltemi. Rental centers offer equipment and lessons for all levels.

places.name: Patmos

visits.text: The Monastery of Saint John the Theologian has commanded the heights above Chora since 1088—the year monk Christodoulos, commissioned by Byzantine Emperor Alexios I Komnenos, laid the first stones atop the ruins of an Artemis temple. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1999, the complex resembles a fortress from the outside—high grey walls, corner towers—but the interior holds a revelation: a library housing 4,000 documents including the celebrated Codex Purpureus, Saint Mark's Gospel inscribed in silver letters on purple-dyed parchment, one of the world's oldest illuminated manuscripts. Allow at least 90 minutes to explore the museum and central courtyard. In high season, arrive at opening to beat the tour groups.

Halfway between Skala port and Chora, the Cave of the Apocalypse is carved into living rock—this is where Saint John, exiled to the island by Emperor Domitian around AD 95, supposedly dictated Revelation to his disciple Prochoros. Look closely at the wall: a three-lobed fissure in the stone symbolizes, according to tradition, the voice of the Trinity. The atmosphere is otherworldly—cool and silent even in peak crowds—contrasting sharply with the island's blazing white sunlight. Plan for 45 minutes on site.

Chora, the medieval village that grew around the monastery starting in 1138, is one of the rare Greek settlements to have evolved continuously since the 12th century—UNESCO cites this unbroken lineage as a criterion for World Heritage status in its own right. Lose yourself in its cobbled lanes between whitewashed houses with wooden balconies: several mansions once belonged to wealthy Aegean sea captains and bear witness to past prosperity. In the evening, this is one of the island's finest spots to watch golden light settle over the rooftops.

Skala, Patmos's main port, is the island's beating heart: fishing boats, inter-island catamarans, waterfront tavernas and colorful shops. This is where you'll take the pulse of daily life—far removed from the monastery's contemplative atmosphere. If you're here on a Sunday, you'll often encounter pilgrims in traditional dress disembarking for Holy Thursday Mass, when the abbot reenacts the washing of the feet in the monastery courtyard.

In Chora's lanes, push open the doors to the Zoodochos Pigi monastery, founded in 1607, and the Annunciation (Evangelismos), founded in 1937—two active convents of nuns that prove Patmos remains a living religious center, extending well beyond the main monastery alone. The sisters receive visitors discreetly but genuinely: dress respectfully.

activities.text: Walk from Skala to Chora on the flagstone path that winds between fig trees and dry-stone walls—about 40 minutes. It's the same route pilgrims have followed for centuries. The panorama over the island's bays widens at every turn. If you prefer to drive, note that Chora's parking is tiny and fills quickly in July-August.

Take a day trip to Patmos's southern beaches—Psili Ammos is accessible only by boat or via a 45-minute hiking trail from Diakofti. The sand is fine and the bay sheltered, yielding water of rare calm. Water taxis depart from Skala throughout the morning.

'Pouggia,' small cakes filled with almonds and cinnamon, are Patmos's signature sweet, tied to monastic tradition. Look for them in Skala bakeries—available year-round, but especially tender when they emerge from the oven in the morning. 'Revithia fournou,' chickpeas baked slowly overnight with olive oil and herbs, appear on most Chora taverna menus on weekends.

Patmos revived viticulture in 2011 after more than a century of abandoned vineyards. The local Fokiano grape yields a mineral, rare white reminiscent of Cycladic wines—ask for it in Chora tavernas. It doesn't always appear on the written menu, but proprietors nearly always have a bottle tucked away.

visits.text: Gaios, the capital of Paxos, stretches along a natural channel sheltered by two islets: Agios Nikolaos, which bears the ruins of a Venetian fortress built between 1424 and 1510, and Panagia, home to a small monastery. The pastel-coloured waterfront with its Venetian-influenced buildings is one of the most charming in the Ionian Islands. According to Greek mythology, Poseidon struck off the southern tip of Corfu with his trident to create Paxos as a love retreat for himself and Amphitrite. Standing here, you can believe it.

The blue caves along Paxos' west coast are accessible only by sea — no road, no stairway. Local boats depart from Gaios or Lakka to cruise along the limestone cliffs, where sunlight filtered through the water casts blue and green reflections inside the cavities. Set aside half a day for the excursion and book ahead in July–August.

Lakka, at the northern tip of the island, nestles at the head of a horseshoe-shaped bay with perfectly still waters — sailing boats moor here by the dozen in summer. The village is tiny (a handful of tavernas, flower-lined lanes) but the waterfront atmosphere at sunset is hard to beat. If you have access to a small boat, rent one and explore the pebble beaches along the north coast, unreachable any other way.

Loggos is the most discreet of Paxos' three villages — a fishing port of a few dozen houses, with tavernas right on the water. It's the best address for a lunch of grilled fish in an authentic setting, well away from the organised excursions that flood Lakka in high season.

The Tripitos Arch, on the west coast of Paxos, is a natural limestone arch several metres high jutting out above the sea. It's reachable on foot from the village of Magazia (about 20 minutes). Few tourists make this detour — it's one of the most dramatic landscapes on the island.

activities.text: Book a boat trip to Antipaxos as soon as you arrive — the little sister island sits just 15 minutes to the south. Its beaches of Vrika and Voutoumi regularly rank among the finest in the Mediterranean — fine white sand and turquoise water that could pass for the Caribbean. The excursion typically takes a full day and includes a stop at the blue caves.

Set off on the footpaths that wind through the centuries-old olive groves in the island's interior. The Republic of Venice actively promoted olive cultivation on Paxos from the 15th century to secure its oil supply — the gnarled trees you'll see are often several hundred years old. The coastal trail from Loggos to Lakka (roughly 2 hours) offers magnificent views.

Paxos' bourdeto is a local twist on the Corfu classic: fish (scorpionfish or gurnard) simmered in a spicy tomato sauce, with a more pronounced kick than the Corfu version. Enjoy it at a harbour-side taverna in Gaios with a glass of tsipouro, the anise-scented grape marc spirit of the Ionians, served ice-cold.

Amygdalota from Paxos are small, soft almond-and-icing-sugar biscuits traditionally baked in the island's monasteries for religious feast days. You'll find them in the artisanal grocery shops of Gaios — pick up a box for the ferry ride the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: Ionian Islands Road Trip: Corfu, Paxos & Lefkada

visits.text: Preveli Monastery perches on a rocky spur overlooking the Libyan Sea—take time to stop on the terraces and contemplate the view. Founded in the 16th century, it played a heroic role during World War II: in 1941, the monks hid hundreds of Allied Australian and British soldiers after the Battle of Crete, helping evacuate them by submarine to Egypt. In retaliation, the Germans vandalized the monastery and tried to carry off the golden Holy Cross—legend says three failed attempts convinced them of a miracle. A memorial at the entrance depicts an armed abbot alongside a British soldier. Modest dress required.

Preveli Beach is one of Crete's most surprising: the Megalopotamos River descends from the Kourtaliotiko Gorge to flow into the sea amid a palm grove. You'll think you're in West Africa rather than the Mediterranean. The palm grove had been entirely destroyed by fire in 2010 and has since regrown. Access is via a path from the monastery parking lot (20-minute walk) or by boat from Plakias.

activities.text: On the road between Koxare and Asomatos, descend a few steps to the bottom of Kourtaliotiko Gorge to see the small church of Agios Nikolaos seemingly embedded in the rock. The vertical walls reach 600 meters high at this point. The road itself is already spectacular—pull over at the roadside to photograph the canyon plunging toward the Libyan Sea.

Plakias is a quiet little beach resort at the back of a beautiful bay. The main beach is long and breezy—perfect for swimming after a day on the road. For more tranquility, the coves of Ammoudi and Damnoni lie 5-6 km to the east and are much wilder. Dive centers offer outings on the clear southern coast seabeds.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Southern Crete: Gorges, Deserted Beaches & End-of-the-World Villages
Grand Circuit of Crete

visits.text: Entering the Kassandra peninsula, stop at Nea Fokea to see the Byzantine tower by the harbour. This 28-metre stone tower, built in 1407 to protect the metochion of the Saint Paul Monastery on Mount Athos, is the best preserved in all of Halkidiki — intact right up to the battlements. In 1821, it served as the headquarters of Emmanouil Papas, leader of the Greek uprising in Halkidiki. At its base lies the cave sanctuary of Saint Paul, a chapel hewn into the rock.

Cape Possidi, at the south-western tip of Kassandra, is a shifting tongue of sand that juts into the sea — its shape changes with the tides and the seasons. The 19th-century lighthouse at its end is one of the most photographed sunset spots in Halkidiki. The village of Afitos, perched on the ancient acropolis a few kilometres away, is worth a detour for its stone houses, cobbled lanes and views over the Toroneos Gulf.

activities.text: The beaches of Kassandra are among the most accessible in Halkidiki — several hold the Blue Flag, the sand is fine and the shallow waters are ideal for families. The coast around Possidi alternates between organised beaches with sunbeds and wild coves fringed with pines. Swim in the morning, before the buses from Thessaloniki arrive.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

visits.text: The Acheron is one of the five rivers of Hades in Greek mythology—and the only one that actually rises in the world of the living, in Epirus. For the ancient Greeks, this river that plunged into karst chasms before reappearing near the Ionian Sea could only be the boundary between the world of the living and the dead. Homer sends Odysseus to consult it in the Odyssey to question the seer Tiresias. The village of Glyki is the starting point for activities on the river.

A few kilometers from Mesopotamos, a hill bears the ruins of the Necromanteion of Ephyra—identified since 1958 as the oracle of the dead described by Homer in the Odyssey, though this identification remains debated among archaeologists. According to the site's interpretation, pilgrims descended into an underground labyrinth after purification and sacrifice rituals to 'receive' the souls of their dead. The 4th-century BC ruins are worth the visit, whatever interpretation you give them.

Nikopolis—'the city of victory'—was founded in 31 BC by Octavian Augustus on the very hill where he had pitched his camp during the naval Battle of Actium against Mark Antony and Cleopatra. It's the largest ancient city in Greece at 900 hectares. Its Victory Monument still bears anchor-shaped notches where bronze rams from ships captured from the enemy were sealed—it had 36 in all. This is also where Epictetus, the slave-philosopher founder of Stoicism, taught until 125 AD.

The Archaeological Museum of Nikopolis, inaugurated in 2009, is an essential introduction before exploring the site. It presents collections chronologically: from the Battle of Actium to the city's abandonment. Don't miss the early Christian mosaics from the basilicas—some are of exceptional quality and preservation—and fragments of Augustus's votive inscription, 54 meters long.

activities.text: The hike through the Acheron Gorge from Glyki is one of the most beautiful water hikes in Greece. The trail follows the river for 8 km round trip between limestone walls covered with plane trees and oleander. The water is transparent and cool—swimming possible in the natural pools along the way. Operators also offer canoe descent from Glyki, physically easier and equally spectacular.

Preveza, 7 km south of Nikopolis, is a quietly charming Ionian port town. In the evening, the pedestrian Spiliadou street comes alive around its fish and seafood tavernas—the Ambracian Gulf before you is the same one where the fate of the Roman world was decided on September 2, 31 BC. Aktion airport is 20 km away, across the strait.
