visits.text: The fortress of Niokastro commands Pylos from a headland overlooking the Bay of Navarino. Built by the Ottomans from 1573 — two years after their defeat at Lepanto — to secure the entrance to the bay, it forms an irregular triangle with a perimeter of 1,566 metres. Inside you'll find a star-shaped citadel with five bastions built by the Venetians, a mosque converted into a church, ramparts with magnificent views over the bay and the island of Sphacteria, and four museums including one devoted to underwater archaeology with headless Hellenistic statues recovered from the sea.

Voidokilia beach is one of the most perfect in the Mediterranean: an almost geometric omega (Ω) shape drawn by nature, fine sand, turquoise water, and the ruins of the Frankish Paleokastro perched on the cliff behind. The Gialova lagoon alongside it is home to 254 bird species — including flamingos depending on the season. To get there, drive around the lagoon from Gialova (5 km), leave the car and walk down to the beach.

activities.text: The Bay of Navarino is where, on 20 October 1827, 27 Allied warships (British, French and Russian) trapped and destroyed an Ottoman fleet of 80 to 90 vessels without losing a single ship. This victory forced Ibrahim Pasha to withdraw from the Peloponnese and made Greek independence inevitable. Local operators run boat trips around the bay to see the memorials on the island of Sphacteria and the wooden wrecks of Ottoman ships, still visible in calm weather.

Three Admirals Square in the heart of Pylos, shaded by centuries-old plane trees, is the centre of local life. The marble stele and period cannons honour the faces of Codrington, De Rigny and Hayden — the three Allied commanders at the battle. It's the perfect spot for an outdoor dinner with views of the lit-up harbour and the silhouette of Niokastro.

days_plan.itin_title: Western Peloponnese: Olympia, Three Venetian Fortresses and the Wild Mani

places.name: Pyrgos

visits.text: Pyrgos - its full name is Pyrgos Kallistis, 'the most beautiful tower' - is Santorini's former capital, the island's chief town in the 18th century. Perched at 365 metres altitude on a hill at the island's centre, it offers 360° views over the caldeira, vineyards and the Aegean. It's one of Santorini's least touristy villages, which makes it one of the most pleasant. Built around a Venetian Kasteli in a defensive labyrinth against pirates, its narrow lanes are designed to disorient the enemy - you'll get lost too, and that's the charm. Allow 2 hours for a leisurely tour.

Climb to Profitis Ilias Monastery, which crowns Santorini's highest point at 567 metres. Founded in 1711 by brothers Gabriel and Joachim Belonias, it served as a refuge for revolutionaries during the 1821 Greek War of Independence. From the terrace, on clear days, you'll glimpse Crete to the south and the islands of Ios, Anafi and Amorgos - it's the only place from which you'll realise Santorini's crescent shape around the active volcano. Inside, a small museum displays icons, relics and ancient manuscripts. Ignore the antennas and military installations occupying part of the summit: they don't spoil the view.

The Church Theotokaki, at the Kasteli's heart, is one of Santorini's oldest, dating from the 10th century. The Museum of Icons and Ecclesiastical Artifacts, installed in the former Agia Triada chapel, displays remarkable religious relics. Pyrgos counts over 40 churches and chapels for a village of just hundreds of residents - some Catholic, the majority Orthodox. Don't hesitate to push open unlocked doors.

Tour the vineyards encircling Pyrgos to understand something unique worldwide: Assyrtiko vines are pruned into low baskets called kouloura, woven close to the ground to resist the island's violent winds and capture nocturnal moisture. The oldest vines are over 200 years old and ungrafted - phylloxera never reached Santorini thanks to its volcanic soil. Several nearby estates offer tastings with caldeira views: allow at least an hour.

activities.text: The climb to Profitis Ilias Monastery from Pyrgos takes roughly 30 to 40 minutes on foot. The road winds through vineyards and Mediterranean scrub. If you drive, parking is available nearby. Go late afternoon for the light - and to avoid the few tourist coaches that pass in the morning.

When tasting at a winery, ask to compare a classic dry Assyrtiko, a Nykteri and a Vinsanto side by side: these are three radically different expressions of the same base grape, and the contrast is striking. Specify that you want to understand the differences in method - most estates have guides or explanatory sheets. Allow at least 1h30 to avoid being rushed.

Dine in Pyrgos rather than heading back down to Fira or Oia: the central square's tavernas serve slow-cooked dishes - sfougato (courgette and cheese omelette), moussaka, stifado - at prices significantly lower than the caldeira. If you keep your eyes open, you'll find a few cafes run by year-round residents, where Greek coffee is served as it is everywhere in Greece except Santorini.

Orthodox Easter in Pyrgos is the most spectacular event in the local calendar: the village illuminates with thousands of candles arranged on walls and roofs to create a luminous procession. It's one of Greece's most beautiful Easter celebrations. If your trip coincides with this date, book accommodation months in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: Santorini Road Trip: Caldeira, White Villages and Minoan Sites

map_keywords: Museum Santorini of the Past Pyrgos

visits.text: Climb to the Fortezza late in the day rather than mid-afternoon—the light on the sea from the western bastions is far more beautiful, and the heat less brutal. This Venetian fortress, whose construction began on 13 September 1573, was designed to shelter Rethymnon's entire population in case of Ottoman attack—but for lack of budget, only the military section was completed. Result in 1645: 8,500 people crammed into a space planned for far fewer, and the governor had to capitulate after only 23 days of siege. Spot the mosque of Sultan Ibrahim inside the walls—its massive dome is the direct legacy of the Ottoman period.

Get lost in the old town lanes without a precise plan—it's the best way to visit Rethymnon. Look for the Rimondi fountain (1626), with its lion heads spouting water in the heart of a shaded little square. Look up: the minarets piercing the rooftops recall two centuries of Ottoman presence, including the Grand Minaret, sole survivor standing from the Neratze mosque, converted into a concert hall. This blend of carved Venetian doorways and Ottoman moucharaby windows is unique in the Mediterranean—you'll see this nowhere else.

In the evening, head down to Rethymnon's Venetian harbour—smaller and far less crowded than Heraklion's, making it one of the island's most pleasant. The lighthouse at the jetty's end dates from the 19th-century Egyptian occupation. Settle into a sea-facing taverna for grilled fish: the menu is often simple, the produce fresh and the atmosphere, as night falls, worth lingering for.

Twenty-three kilometres south-east of Rethymnon, Arkadi monastery is the most emotionally charged historical site in all Crete. Its current fortress-like structure dates from the 16th century, with a church (katholikon) completed in 1587—a jewel of the Cretan Renaissance. But what made Arkadi famous throughout Europe is the drama of 8 November 1866: surrounded by the Ottoman army, superior Gabriel Marinakis ordered the powder magazine blown up rather than surrender. Of the 964 people sheltering there—fighters but mostly women and children—most perished. Plan to have shoulders and knees covered to enter.

activities.text: Start with the Fortezza in the morning before the heat, then descend into the old town lanes to wander. Cretan craft shops—embroidery, ceramics, silver jewellery—are concentrated around Arkadiou and Souliou streets. Rethymnon is less touristy than Heraklion or Chania, giving it a more relaxed pace.

The excursion to Arkadi monastery requires about half a day (1 hour each way by car + visit). Return via the hinterland village road—Episkopi village with its springs, or Margarites known for its pottery workshops, merit a 20-minute stop.

In the evening at the harbour, taste gamopilafo—literally 'wedding rice'. This traditional Cretan festive dish is prepared with lamb simmered long in its broth, in which rice then cooks until creamy, bound with butter and lemon juice. You'll find it in old town tavernas, often as the daily special.

For dessert, ask for mizithra with local honey. This fresh sheep's or goat's cheese, with a texture close to ricotta, is eaten on the spot in cheese shops or as dessert in most tavernas—drizzled with Cretan thyme honey and scattered with walnuts.

places.name: Rhodes

visits.text: Rhodes Old Town has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988, and it thoroughly deserves that recognition. The Knights of St. John occupied it from 1309 to 1522 and set about transforming it into an impregnable fortress—the 4 kilometers of ramparts you'll walk along bear witness to this. What makes the site exceptional is the layering: behind Gothic walls hide Ottoman mosques, Turkish baths, and Byzantine alleyways. Enter through the d'Amboise Gate late morning, before tour buses flood the narrow streets.

The Palace of the Grand Masters dominates the old town from the highest point of the citadel. Built in the 14th century, it contains 150 rooms, of which 24 are open to the public. What you absolutely can't miss inside: the mosaic floors, brought from Kos by the Italians during their controversial 1930s restoration. Worth knowing: in 1856, a gunpowder magazine explosion nearly destroyed the entire palace—what you see today is largely a Fascist reconstruction, intended to welcome Mussolini, who ultimately never visited. Look for the commemorative stele bearing his name near the ticket office.

The Street of the Knights (Odós Ippotón) is one of the best-preserved Gothic monuments in Europe. The Knights of the Order were organized into seven "tongues"—France, Italy, Spain, Provence, Auvergne, England, Germany—and each had its inn along this main thoroughfare. You'll see their facades at every turn. Take time to read the coats of arms carved above the doors: the Auvergne inn bears those of Guy de Blanchefort, Grand Master from 1512 to 1513.

The Archaeological Museum of Rhodes is housed in the former Great Hospital of the Knights, built between 1440 and 1489. The star of the collection? The Crouching Aphrodite, a Hellenistic marble statue of remarkable finesse. Allow 45 minutes to an hour for a complete tour. The combined ticket (valid 3 days) includes this museum, the Palace of the Grand Masters, and the church of Panagia tou Kastrou—a smart way to optimize your visits.

The Suleiman the Magnificent Mosque, built in 1522 just after the Ottoman conquest of Rhodes, visually marks the transition between two civilizations. Its pink minaret is visible from afar within the old town. It's closed to regular visits, but the exterior and surrounding square are worth seeing in the evening, when the light is soft and crowds thinner.

The Acropolis of Lindos perches on a cliff 116 meters above the sea—it's the third most-visited archaeological site in Greece, right after Athens and Delphi. The Temple of Athena Lindia, in its current form, dates from around 300 BC (replacing an earlier temple destroyed by fire in 392 BC). At the entrance, a rare detail: a warship's prow carved directly into the rock in the 2nd century BC, which served as a pedestal for a now-vanished bronze statue. Arrive before 9 AM—after that hour, the climb becomes a procession.

The village of Lindos, entirely pedestrian, is built in an amphitheater on a hillside. Its lanes paved with black-and-white pebble mosaics lead to the "captains' houses" from the 16th century, built by shipowners who had made fortunes in maritime trade. Push open the door of the Church of the Virgin (Panagia), founded in the 14th century and restored in 1489 by Grand Master d'Aubusson: its interior frescoes date from 1779 and are among the best-preserved on the island. Skip lunch on a terrace here—prices are noticeably higher than elsewhere on Rhodes.

St. Paul's Bay (Paralia Agios Pavlos), below Lindos, takes its name from the apostle Paul, who supposedly landed here during his journey to Rhodes. The water is a striking turquoise, enclosed on three sides by cliffs and the acropolis. It's also a popular wedding venue—if you're there on a weekday, you'll have better chances of finding the peace needed to enjoy it.

On the island's west coast, Kamiros is nicknamed the "Greek Pompeii" of Rhodes: a Doric city founded in the 5th century BC, abandoned after an earthquake, and rediscovered in 1852. You'll see a sophisticated water system—cisterns, pipes, fountains—that supplied the entire city. The stoa, terraced houses, and Temple of Athena are well-preserved enough to give you a precise idea of ancient urban planning. Allow one hour for the visit.

On the way back from Kamiros, the Valley of the Butterflies (Petaloudes) is worth a quick stop between June and September, when thousands of Panaxia quadripunctaria butterflies settle there in the coolness of plane trees and waterfalls. Off-season, the site remains pretty but the butterflies aren't there—check the timing before making the detour.

activities.text: Wander through the lanes of the lower town (Chora) following your instincts: craft shops, painted ceramic workshops, small tavernas tucked into courtyards. If you're there on a weekday morning, the covered market on Socratous Street offers a much more local atmosphere than the tourist boutiques along the waterfront.

Stroll along Mandraki harbor late in the day. At the harbor entrance stand two columns topped with bronze stag and doe—tradition claims the Colossus of Rhodes, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, stood at this spot (nothing proves it, but visitors love the story). Three Ottoman windmills and Fort Saint Nicholas complete the panorama. A café terrace facing the harbor at sunset—that's the best moment of the day.

Try pitaroudia, chickpea fritters typical of Rhodes, fried in olive oil with onion and dill. You'll find them in all the old town's tavernas, often served hot as an appetizer. Pair them with a glass of Athiri, the local white varietal: dry, light, slightly citrusy, perfect with mezze and fresh fish.

If you have energy after dinner, climb onto the ramparts from the Palace of the Grand Masters entrance (different hours from the palace, check before going). The walk offers plunging views over the old town and harbor you won't find anywhere else. Bring a flashlight—lighting is minimal.

The climb to Lindos acropolis is on foot from the village—bring good shoes and water, the slope is steep under the sun. Mules offer rides up, but several local sources discourage this option out of concern for the animals. The reward at the top: a 360° view over Lindos' two bays, the Aegean Sea, and on clear days, the Turkish coast.

After the acropolis, head down to swim in the main bay of Lindos (Main Beach). The water is clear, the beach organized with sunbeds and umbrellas. To avoid summer crowds, opt for neighboring St. Paul's Bay: smaller, less known, and just as beautiful.

Try souma before leaving: it's a traditional Rhodes fig eau-de-vie, sweet and fragrant, found in small grocers in Lindos village. Pair it with takakia, these little bites of fried octopus with lemon served by most waterfront tavernas. For wine, the local Mandilaria—fruity and light red—is perfect with grilled meats.

visits.text: Melissani Cave — also known as the 'Cave of the Nymphs' — is a limestone cenote whose ceiling collapsed during the 1953 earthquake, allowing sunlight to pour directly onto the underground lake. The lake stretches roughly 160 metres long and reaches depths of 30 to 40 metres; its surface sits one metre above sea level, fed by water that has travelled 15 km underground from the Katavothres in Argostoli. Archaeological excavations in 1962 uncovered a sanctuary dedicated to the god Pan on a small central islet, along with clay figurines and oil lamps dating from the 3rd–4th centuries BC. Plan your visit between 11 am and 1 pm: that's when the sun strikes the opening directly and transforms the water into a kaleidoscope of blues and violets. The tour is by rowboat and lasts 10 to 15 minutes.

Drogarati Cave, 5 km from Sami, was discovered around 300 years ago when an earthquake caused its entrance to collapse inward. It measures 65 metres long, 45 metres wide and 20 metres high, with a steady temperature of 18°C — a welcome relief in summer. The stalactites and stalagmites, some of which resemble dolphins according to speleologists, have formed over roughly 100 million years. The main chamber, known as the 'Hall of Apotheosis,' has such remarkable acoustics that it has hosted concerts — including, in 2014, a performance by the Bavarian Philharmonic who sailed in on a cruise ship docked at Argostoli. Bring a jumper even in summer, and wear sturdy shoes for the slippery steps.

Sami's harbour is Kefalonia's main ferry port, with connections to Patras and Ithaca. The town itself is modest but pleasant, with a handful of tavernas and cafés along the waterfront. Just next door lies Karavomylos, the village where the resurgent springs from the Katavothres emerge — the seawater that entered the ground in Argostoli reappears here, slightly brackish, after its 14-day underground journey.

Antisamos Beach, 4 km from Sami, is one of the most beautiful on Kefalonia: a long sweep of white pebbles framed by pine forest that tumbles down to the shore, with deep turquoise water. Several scenes from the 2001 film 'Captain Corelli's Mandolin' were shot right here. The beach is lifeguarded in summer but never overcrowded — the winding access road from the main highway puts off the lazier crowd. Bring your snorkelling mask: the seabed is rich.

activities.text: A combined visit to both caves (Melissani + Drogarati) works best as a half-day outing. Start early in the morning with Drogarati to beat the cruise-ship coaches, then head to Melissani around 11:30 am to catch the peak sunlight. Allow about an hour at each cave, including queuing time in high season.

After the caves, head to Antisamos for a swim. The water is crystal-clear and far less crowded than the beaches in the south. If you have a mask, dip your head below the surface and look around — the visibility is exceptional.

Riganada is a rustic dried bread rubbed with fresh tomato, drizzled with olive oil, topped with feta and scattered with dried oregano — a summer classic across the Ionian Islands. Dead simple, dead good. Order one with a chilled glass of Robola at a taverna on the Sami waterfront at the end of the day.

visits.text: Ferry Paros → Santorini, about 3 hours. Stay on deck as you approach the island: you'll sail along the caldera cliffs, streaked in black basalt, red, brown, and white pumice—the successive layers of a colossal eruption around 1600 BC that likely destroyed Minoan civilization. These cliffs rise 300 meters above you and continue underwater to 400 meters depth. Book ahead in summer.

Check in and set out to explore Fira, Santorini's capital. The clifftop promenade follows the caldera's edge for several hundred meters, with constant views of the Nea Kameni volcano at the center of the old crater. Don't miss the Three Bells of Fira—the small Orthodox church with its blue-and-white bell tower clinging to the cliff is one of Greece's most reproduced images. The Orthodox cathedral with its Venetian influences also merits a stop.

Before setting out to hike, head up to Imerovigli for the caldera's most open viewpoint—this village perched at 300 meters is the island's natural observation deck. Make the detour to Skaros Rock: this isolated promontory housed Santorini's fortified capital under the Venetians. Only a rocky spur above the void remains, but the 360° view across the caldera, volcano, and Oia in the distance is dizzying.

Use the morning to visit the Akrotiri archaeological site, Greece's Pompeii. This Minoan city was buried under ash during the 1600 BC eruption and rediscovered in the 1960s. Excavations uncovered entire streets, frescoes, and everyday objects perfectly preserved—no bodies, unlike Pompeii; the inhabitants had time to flee. The site is covered and open year-round.

activities.text: In the evening, take the KTEL bus to reach Oia, at the island's northern tip. This village perched on the caldera's ridge is world-famous for its sunsets. Winding lanes between cave houses and purple bougainvillea lead to Oia Castle, where the view across the caldera and the sun plunging into the Aegean is simply extraordinary. Arrive at least 45 minutes before sunset to secure a good spot—in high season, hundreds of people crowd onto the ramparts.

The Fira → Oia hike is the island's must-do activity. The ridge trail follows the caldera for 10.3 km—count on 3 to 4 hours, with 328 meters of elevation gain. All along you overlook the cliffs from 250-350 meters height, passing through Firostefani and Imerovigli. Set out before 8am to avoid heat and crowds. Bring at least 1.5 liters of water per person: there's virtually no water source along the route. Return from Oia by KTEL bus.

Upon arriving in Oia, rest at one of the cafés on the central square before descending to Amoudi port, on foot via the 214 steps that plunge beneath the village. This miniature fishing port, wedged into the cliff, is the ideal spot for a fresh-fish lunch with direct views across the caldera. Return to Fira by bus in early evening.

Book the catamaran before you even arrive in Santorini—in summer, they sell out days in advance. Departures are from Vlychada port or Fira's old port. First stop: Red Beach at Akrotiri, whose sand owes its color to a layer of solidified lava—jump in the water, the contrast is striking. Then head to the volcanic islet of Nea Kameni to walk up to the smoking craters, born from several eruptions between the 16th and 20th centuries. Next, a plunge at Palea Kameni's hot springs: the sulfurous water reaches 30 to 35°C; you must swim about thirty meters from the boat to reach them. Count on a full day, 5 to 7 hours total.

Last evening in Fira: climb from the old port on foot via the 588 steps of the mule track or by cable car, and treat yourself to dinner on the caldera clifftop. Fira's atmosphere is more accessible than Oia's, with more varied and less touristy spots—this is where locals live year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Greece Road Trip: Athens and the Cyclades

Greece Road Trip: Athens and the Cyclades

visits.text: Santorini—the caldera and its perched villages seen from the sea offer one of the Mediterranean's most spectacular maritime entrances. Vlychada harbor on the south coast is calmer than the crowded caldera and ringed by lunar-white cliffs. It's the best overnight anchorage. The volcanic sand beach of Perissa and Perivolos stretches 7 km: black sand burning hot (bring sandals), crystalline water, and gently sloping sandy bottom. In high season, it's one of the island's liveliest.

The Akrotiri archaeological site is Santorini's equivalent of Pompeii—a Minoan city buried under meters of volcanic ash during the catastrophic eruption around 1500 BC. Excavations began in 1967 under Spyridon Marinatos. Beneath a massive protective dome, you wander the ancient city streets: two-story houses with sewer systems, murals depicting daily life, pottery, tools. No bodies were found, suggesting inhabitants fled before the eruption.

Red Beach sits 5 minutes from Akrotiri on foot: a blood-red cliff plunges vertically into the sea, creating a striking contrast with the blue water. The beach is narrow and crowded; access is sometimes restricted due to rockfall risk in summer. Go early or enjoy it from the sea.

activities.text: Swimming on the volcanic beach of Perissa or Perivolos—the black sand absorbs heat, consider water shoes. Water is deep and clear.

Wine tasting in Santorini—the island's vineyards produce assyrtiko, a mineral white unique in the world, cultivated in basket-shaped (kouloura) vines on volcanic soil. Several estates are accessible from anchorage or by road.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Schinoussa is even smaller than Iraklia—8 km², fewer than 300 residents. Its main village, Chora (or Panagia), perches 1.5 km from Mersini port: a single street you can walk down in 5 minutes, lined with family tavernas and geranium-draped houses. Here, everyone knows everyone and newcomers quickly blend in. Come evening, Chora comes alive on the central square: locals and the Greek vacationers who make up most of the island's clientele gather over a warm rakomelo. Push open the door of kafeneio Xara—an institution.

Tsigouri beach is Schinoussa's most accessible, 600 meters from Chora via a paved road. Golden sand, shallow water, and tamarisks for shade—it's the only spot on the island offering this comfort. A beach bar serves drinks and mezedes. For a wilder experience, Psili Ammos beach is worth the 45-minute walk from Chora: a pristine bay with water of surreal clarity, no infrastructure whatsoever. Arrive early in summer.

activities.text: Swimming and lounging at Tsigouri—or swim out to the small islet 200 meters offshore for absolute solitude.
Short coastal hike to Livadi or Aligaria beach: Schinoussa's trails link the beaches one to another, all within 40 minutes on foot from Chora. No roads between them, just hillside paths.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Megalo Livadi is a village out of time in southwest Serifos, 30 minutes of curves from the port. This is where the island's grand industrial history played out—until 1963, Serifos's iron mines (exploited since antiquity, reactivated at large scale from 1880 by a Franco-German company) loaded their ore from this quay onto cargo ships bound for Europe. The rusted metal loading bridge still stands, cumbersome and poetic. In 1916, a miners' strike led to a bloody confrontation with gendarmes—4 miners killed. It's considered the first bloody labor strike in modern Greek history. A memorial on the quay commemorates this episode.

activities.text: Sunset from anchorage or the surrounding hills—Megalo Livadi bay is well sheltered and faces west. A small waterfront taverna serves good fresh fish.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Sifnos is the Cyclades' island of ceramics and gastronomy. Since at least the 9th century BC, the island has exported utilitarian pottery throughout the Mediterranean—Sifnos clay is rich in feldspars that speed vitrification. In the 18th and 19th centuries, about a hundred workshops ran at full tilt. Today a handful of potters perpetuate the tradition: at Platys Gialos and Vathy, you'll still see the old pottery buildings recognizable by their curved roof and elongated shape. Several workshops welcome visitors.

Platys Gialos is Sifnos's largest sandy beach—a long bay well exposed with Blue Flag, waterfront tavernas, and practical anchorage to the left of the bay. The White Tower sits 1.5 km northeast. A 2 km walk southeast leads to Chryssopigi beach, the island's most emblematic, with its little white monastery perched on a rocky spur—one of Sifnos's most photographed images.

activities.text: Swimming and paddleboarding at Platys Gialos: wide sheltered bay, shallow water, fine sand. SUP rental on the beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Ormos Vathy is one of the Cyclades' finest anchorages—a perfectly sheltered horseshoe bay with a small fishing village at the back. On nearby Tsopos beach, the Atsonios pottery has operated for 4 generations: it's one of the last workshops set up the old way, right on the beach, with wood-fired kilns and drying tables. Watch the potter work before choosing from the utilitarian ceramics and artisan pieces on display.

Apollonia, Sifnos's capital, perches in the hills at the island's center. It's a maze of paved lanes where you'll happily get lost among artisan shops, traditional tavernas, and small flower-filled squares. It was founded in the 6th century BC and bears the god Apollo's name. Come evening, it's the island's liveliest spot—open terraces, Greek music, warm ambiance without Mykonos excess.

activities.text: Walk in the island's south from Vathy to Faros—a fishing hamlet with its small sheltered beach in a former pottery port converted to a restaurant. The coastal trail skirts wild coves accessible on foot or by swim.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Sikinos is the least visited of the inhabited Cyclades—300 residents, minimal infrastructure, no hotel chains. Alopronia port is a peaceful fishing village with a small, calm sandy beach. Then take the road (or better, the paved mule path) climbing 4 km to the Kastro and Chora, perched at 280 meters—two quarters facing each other, the first lively with cafés and the town hall, the second a labyrinth of white lanes bordered by bougainvillea. Three windmills stand watch over the ensemble from the cliff edge, facing the sea.

activities.text: Climb to Zoodochou Pigi monastery, built in 1690 atop the Kastro: panoramic view over the archipelago and beyond. The island's most astonishing monument, however, is Episkopi—a 3rd-century Roman mausoleum converted to a Christian temple in the 17th century, accessible via trail from the Kastro. The place radiates a particular energy.
Swimming on Alopronia beach at the foot of the port—fine sand, calm water, and tamarisks. It's the island's most accessible beach, perfect for a pause before setting sail again.

days_plan.itin_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves

places.name: Sitia

visits.text: Sitia is the easternmost town in Crete, and the least touristy—it moves at its own rhythm. Built in an amphitheater facing the Aegean Sea, it was destroyed then abandoned in the 16th century before being repopulated from the mid-19th century onward, giving it a half-historic, half-living character. Climb to the Venetian fortress of Kazarma late in the afternoon for a panoramic view over the bay—the walk from the harbor takes barely 10 minutes and the coastal panorama is worth the effort. Below, the harbor hosts fishing boats and ferries to the Dodecanese.

Toplou Monastery sits about ten kilometers east of Sitia, in the middle of an arid, lunar landscape. It's one of Crete's most important and most fortified monasteries—its Turkish name 'Toplou' means 'with a cannon,' which sums up its character nicely: pillaged by the Knights of Malta in 1530, destroyed by earthquake, rebuilt in the 17th century. Its icon museum houses the masterpiece of Ioannis Kornaros: the icon 'Megas ei Kyrie' (Great art Thou, O Lord), dated 1770, a composition in 57 zones with hundreds of faces illustrating the Great Blessing of the Waters. The monastery also produces renowned organic wine and quality olive oil, sold at the entrance. Open in summer 9 AM to 1 PM and 2 to 6 PM, entrance €3.

The Vai palm forest, 24 kilometers east of Sitia, is Europe's largest natural palm forest, classified as a nature reserve. It shelters thousands of specimens of Phoenix theophrasti, the palm endemic to Crete and Turkey's southern coast—a species that grows nowhere else in Europe. The beach bordering the palm forest has earned the Blue Flag. According to legend, the palms grew from date pits tossed by Egyptian sailors, Saracen pirates, or Mithridates' army... Arrive early: in summer the beach is overrun by mid-morning.

Three kilometers north of Vai, take time to climb up to the site of Itanos: an ancient Phoenician city whose ruins—temples, basilicas, cisterns—sprawl across a sandy hill that's almost deserted. The city was famous in antiquity for its trade in purple dye, the red color extracted from murex shells. You'll very likely have the site to yourself. Below lie two wild beaches in isolated coves—ideal for snorkeling in crystalline waters, far from any infrastructure.

If you have a full day to devote to Minoan Crete, head down to Kato Zakros (45 kilometers from Sitia) for the Palace of Zakros—the fourth-largest Minoan palace in Crete after Knossos, Malia, and Phaistos. Built around 1900 BC, it faced eastward: excavations have yielded elephant tusks, ivory, stone vases, and exotic objects from Egypt and the Near East. You can reach it directly by car, or through the Gorge of the Dead (8-kilometer hike from the village of Zakros). Excavations, begun in the 1960s, still regularly reveal new rooms.

activities.text: Explore Sitia's harbor in the evening: tavernas come alive around 8 PM, locals play cards in traditional cafés, and the promenade runs along the sea to Sitia Beach, a long strip of fine sand. Sitiots are renowned in Crete for their warmth and musical culture—you might stumble upon an impromptu terrace concert. Local raki (tsikoudia) automatically accompanies the welcome meze at authentic tavernas.

To reach Zakros Palace on foot, take the Gorge of the Dead starting from the village of Zakros: 8 kilometers descending through a limestone canyon with vertiginous walls, dotted with caves that served as burial sites in the Minoan era—hence the name. It's one of the most beautiful hikes in eastern Crete, shaded and cool even in summer. Allow 3 hours, wear closed shoes, and arrange to have a car waiting at Kato Zakros below—otherwise you'll climb back up those 8 kilometers on foot.

At the end of a meal in a Sitia taverna, order xerotigana: ribbons of dough fried in oil, drizzled with honey, and sprinkled with sesame or crushed walnuts. It's the traditional festive dessert of eastern Crete, served at weddings and baptisms—and at good tavernas in summer. Pair them with a red Liatiko, the indigenous grape of the region, sweet and fragrant with notes of dried grapes.

visits.text: Skiathos town is a compact, lively little capital whose cobbled lanes wind their way down to the double harbour. It's the most tourist-oriented island in the Sporades — in summer the atmosphere is cosmopolitan and the nightlife runs until dawn along Papadiamanti Street. If you prefer a quieter pace, avoid July–August or arrive in the morning to see the sights before the cruise-ship crowds pour in.

The Bourtzi peninsula, linked to the old harbour by a short footbridge, holds a 13th-century Venetian fortification built by the Ghisi brothers to defend the island from pirates. Ancient trees cover the headland, making it a pleasant spot on a warm evening — open-air concerts are sometimes held here in summer. The view from the tip across both harbours is one of the most photogenic on the island.

Evangelistria Monastery, founded in the 18th century on a hillside 4 km above town, is the most historically charged site on Skiathos. It was here, in September 1807, that the first Greek flag — a white cross on a blue field — was designed, woven, blessed and raised in the presence of the revolutionary hero Theodoros Kolokotronis. The monks who still live in the monastery produce wine, olive oil and honey. The museum housed in the old olive press displays the loom said, by tradition, to have woven that first flag. Take the marked trail from town rather than the road — it's more enjoyable and no longer.

Kastro is the former fortified capital of Skiathos, perched on a rocky headland at the northern end of the island. The islanders retreated here in the 14th century to escape pirate raids and didn't return to the coast until after Greek independence in 1829. A handful of churches still cling to the cliffs, including the Chapel of the Nativity of Christ, and the views over the Aegean are striking. Reachable by hiking (3 km of track then a trail) or by boat from the harbour.

The Papadiamantis Museum, at number 12 on the street of the same name near the port, occupies the birthplace of Alexandros Papadiamantis, Greece's greatest 19th-century novelist, born on Skiathos in 1851. The two-storey house retains its period interior virtually intact: original furniture, personal library, everyday objects. The cave known as the 'Murderess's Cave' near Lalaria takes its name from one of Papadiamantis's short stories. Open Tuesday to Sunday, 9:30 am to 1:30 pm and 5:15 pm to 8 pm.

Koukounaries is Skiathos's postcard beach — regularly ranked among the finest in Greece, with 1,200 metres of fine golden sand backed by a protected Aleppo pine forest. Behind the beach lies the Strofilia wetland, a refuge for rare migratory birds including swans and herons. In high season the sun loungers are claimed early — arrive before 9 am or come in the late afternoon.

Lalaria is Skiathos's show-stopping beach: reachable only by boat from the old harbour, it curves in a half-moon of smooth white pebbles beneath sheer limestone cliffs that plunge into water of an almost unreal blue-green. A natural rock arch sculpted by the sea is the focal point of every photograph on the island. Boats run daily from the port, but if the northerly winds pick up the crossing is cancelled. Bring water and food — there's nothing on site.

The sea caves near Lalaria are every bit as worthwhile as the beach itself. The Blue Cave (Galazia) owes its name to the extraordinary colour of the water inside when the sun hits — reflections off the limestone walls produce an almost supernatural blue glow. The Dark Cave (Skotini), roughly 20 metres deep, can only be entered by small boat because the opening is so narrow. Most excursions to Lalaria include a pass through these caves.

Troulos, 8 km west of town, is a family-friendly beach with shallow water and a relaxed atmosphere. Pines reach almost to the sand, and a few tavernas serve grilled fish. Facing it is the cluster of Troulonisia islets. It's the ideal beach with children if Koukounaries feels too crowded.

activities.text: Papadiamanti Street, the main artery of Skiathos town, comes alive in the evening with craft shops, tavernas spilling tables onto the pavement, and ouzo bars. Stroll down from around 7 pm when the locals start to emerge — the summer crowds don't descend until after 9 pm. It's also the best spot to order a mezze platter: taramosalata, tyrokafteri, chargrilled octopus.

Astakomakaronada, the signature dish of the Sporades, is spaghetti with fresh lobster in a lightly spiced tomato sauce. You'll find it at almost every taverna along the old harbour — ask whether the lobster is from the morning catch, as local fishermen deliver directly at dawn. It's one of the best freshness-to-value deals on the island.

Tsipouro, a Greek spirit distilled from grape pomace, is the Sporades' drink of choice. On Skiathos it's served cold, in small glasses, alongside mezze — chargrilled octopus, feta, herb-marinated olives. The monks at Evangelistria produce a small-batch artisanal version available from the monastery shop.

Book a half-day boat trip from Skiathos old harbour to Lalaria and the sea caves — it's the simplest and most enjoyable way to get there. Departures are usually at 9 am or 10 am. In July–August, book the day before: places sell out fast. Options include swimming and snorkelling stops in coves that are impossible to reach on foot.

Revithada, a thick chickpea soup slow-baked with olive oil, onion and herbs, is a Sporades vegetarian classic often served on Sundays in tavernas. It's a warming winter recipe, though some places keep it on the menu year-round. With crusty bread and olives on the side, it makes a complete — and very affordable — lunch.

Retsina is a white wine infused with pine resin, made according to a tradition stretching back millennia — the resin originally prevented oxidation inside clay amphorae. Its distinctive, slightly terpenic taste pairs beautifully with grilled fish and mezze. It's the cheapest and most authentically Greek bottle you'll be poured at any Sporades taverna.

days_plan.itin_title: Sporades Island Hop: Skiathos, Skopelos & Alonissos
Sporades Island Hop: Skiathos, Skopelos & Alonissos

visits.text: Skopelos town is built amphitheatre-style around a deep bay — white houses with red-brown tile roofs tumble down toward the harbour in a setting the filming of Mamma Mia made famous worldwide. The town alone counts 123 Orthodox churches and chapels — nearly as many as days in the year — some dating back to the Byzantine era. Wander the cobbled alleys of the upper town: no two facades are alike.

The Kastro of Skopelos, on the hill above the harbour, is the highest point in town. The Venetian and Byzantine ruins here offer the best panoramic view over the bay, the ferries coming and going, and — on a clear day — the outline of Alonissos on the horizon. Head up in the late afternoon: the light is ideal and the atmosphere far calmer than midday.

The chapel of Agios Ioannis Kastri is perched on top of a sheer rock face plunging into the sea, a few kilometres north of Skopelos near the village of Glossa. To reach it from the car park below you must climb 106 steps carved into the rock. This is where the outdoor wedding scenes from Mamma Mia were filmed in 2007. Arrive early in the morning or after 5 pm to avoid the stream of fans who descend all day long in summer. The view over the Aegean and across to Alonissos is worth the climb even if you've never seen the film.

The village of Glossa, perched in the hills of Skopelos, is one of the best-preserved mountain settlements in the Sporades. Its Macedonian architecture — overhanging roofs, stone houses, a plane-tree-shaded square — is a marked contrast to the white Cycladic-style buildings elsewhere on the island. The locals still tend olive groves and plum orchards. The central square with its cafés is the perfect stop on the way to the Agios Ioannis chapel.

Panormos is a sheltered seaside village tucked into a natural bay, with a fine-sand beach fringed by pines. It's a favourite anchorage for sailors, and its waterfront tavernas serve fresh fish in a peaceful setting. Less crowded than the beaches on the southern coast, it's the perfect spot for a feet-in-the-water lunch.

The beaches of Kastani and Milia are two of the most recognisable Mamma Mia filming locations. Kastani, in particular, hosted the film's longest scene. Both coves — pebbles or sand depending on the section, turquoise water, a pine forest that slopes right down to the shore — rank among the most beautiful on Skopelos quite apart from their cinematic fame. Milia tends to be breezier and is ideal for snorkelling.

The Sendoukia are an archaeological mystery in the hills above Skopelos: four large sarcophagi carved straight into the bedrock at the top of a ridge, linked by local legend to the Greek pirate Glaucus who is said to have ruled these waters. Their exact date and purpose are still debated by scholars. The path up is unmarked — ask in Glossa for directions. The view from the site alone justifies the effort.

activities.text: The kalderimi trails — ancient paved paths that once linked Skopelos's monasteries — are the best way to explore the island's wooded interior. The route to the 18th-century Evangelismos Monastery on Mount Palouki winds through a pine forest with sweeping sea views. Set off in the morning to beat the heat and bring water.

The tyropita on Skopelos is unlike any you'll find elsewhere: here, local cheese — a fresh, lightly salted sheep's-milk variety — is wrapped in handmade puff pastry, not factory-produced phyllo. It's the island's go-to breakfast, sold in bakeries from 7 am. Don't leave without eating one straight from the oven, still warm.

Skopelos plums carry a protected designation of origin and are the island's agricultural pride. Family orchards produce plums preserved in syrup, sun-dried, or turned into liqueur. You'll find these products in the shops around town — they're the best edible souvenir to bring home from Skopelos.

The ideal day along Skopelos's west coast: head out by car in the morning to Panormos for a waterfront coffee, drive down to Milia for a swim, then move on to Kastani for the afternoon. Finish at the smaller cove of Hovolo, quieter and ringed by flat rocks perfect for snorkelling. Boat-hire outfits in Skopelos town also rent small motorboats so you can explore these coves at your own pace.

Astakos — lobster simply grilled with lemon and olive oil — is served at the fishermen's tavernas in Panormos and at small harbours like Agnontas. Ask whether it's fresh from the morning: Skopelos fishermen have a reputation for delivering straight to the local tavernas. Pair it with garlic-rubbed toasted bread.

Loukoumades — small, light doughnuts fried to order, drenched in honey, scattered with crushed walnuts and cinnamon — are the street dessert of the Sporades. You'll find them at the kiosks on Skopelos harbour from around 6 pm. They're crisp on the outside, airy inside, and best eaten standing up with sticky fingers, the way the locals do.

days_plan.itin_title: Sporades Island Hop: Skiathos, Skopelos & Alonissos
Sporades Island Hop: Skiathos, Skopelos & Alonissos

visits.text: The Temple of Poseidon raises its 15 surviving Doric columns on a promontory 200 feet above the sea, at the southern tip of Attica. Built between 444 and 440 BC—at the same time as the Parthenon, as part of Pericles' grand program—it served both as a sanctuary to the god of the seas and as a landmark for sailors returning to Piraeus from the Mediterranean. On clear days, you'll make out the islands of Kea, Kythnos, and in good weather, as far as Serifos in the distance. Time your arrival for sunset: the orange light on the white marble is spectacular.

On one of the temple columns, look for Lord Byron's carved name, who visited the site in 1810. The English poet was so captivated by Sounion that he recorded his reaction in verses from Don Juan (1819)—an ode to this marble promontory battered by waves that he described as the ideal place to listen to the sea in silence. This kind of graffiti would be a crime today—at the time, Byron was simply an enthusiastic Romantic tourist.

Take time to explore the site beyond the main temple: the cape also encompasses the remains of ancient fortifications and, on the adjacent hill, the foundations of a Temple of Athena Sounion destroyed in the 1st century AD. Less documented, this second sanctuary confirms that Sounion was a major cult site—not just a navigation point for sailors.

As you walk the promontory, keep in mind that you're standing on one of Greece's most mythologically charged places: according to legend, this is where King Aegeus threw himself into the sea, believing his son Theseus dead after the expedition against the Minotaur. Theseus had forgotten to change his black sails to white ones to signal his victory. The sea has borne the king's name ever since. Homer himself mentions Sounion in the Odyssey as 'the sacred promontory of Athens.'

activities.text: The coastal road from Athens hugs the Athenian Riviera for 44 miles—a succession of beaches, coves, and seaside resorts. Plan to leave in the morning to enjoy the water before the summer crowds arrive. Vouliagmeni and Varkiza beaches are the most popular with Athenians.

At Vouliagmeni, make a stop at the thermal lake of the same name: a turquoise body of water fed by underground hot springs, set in a partially open cave. The water hovers around 72-84°F year-round. There's an entrance fee but the swim is worth it, especially if you've spent several days at archaeological sites.

Arrive at Cape Sounion about an hour before sunset. The site stays open until sunset—check the exact time depending on the season. Warning: there's no shade at the site, bring a hat and water. The terrain is rocky, avoid light sandals.

After sunset, the tavernas along the Sounion coast serve ultra-fresh locally caught fish: grilled octopus with oregano and lemon, Mediterranean fish soup, calamari and scampi. An ouzo or a Greek Alfa beer while watching the last light fade over the Aegean Sea: hard to beat as an end to the day.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in Athens: Acropolis, Museums & Neighborhoods
Greece: Athens, Delphi and the Meteora

visits.text: The Archaeological Museum of Sparta, housed since 1875 in a neoclassical building — the first purpose-built provincial museum in all of Greece — gathers the lion's share of finds from Laconia, spanning the Neolithic to the Roman era. Its centrepiece is the marble bust of King Leonidas (5th century BC), wearing the crested hoplite helmet. The other treasure is unique in Greece: a collection of votive terracotta masks worn during initiation rites at the Sanctuary of Artemis Orthia — old women, bearded warriors, satyrs, gorgons. Allow about 45 minutes.

The statue of Leonidas, erected in 1968 at the entrance to the modern city's stadium, depicts the king standing 3 metres tall, helmeted, shield on arm. The pedestal bears a replica of ΜΟΛΩΝ ΛΑΒΕ — "come and take them" — the answer Leonidas is said to have given the Persian King Xerxes when ordered to surrender his weapons at Thermopylae in 480 BC. It's the quintessential Sparta photo: snap it, then continue to the archaeological site just behind.

The archaeological site of Sparta sometimes disappoints those expecting an Athenian Acropolis — and that's entirely the point. The Spartans believed their courage was the only rampart they needed and never built grand fortifications or monumental architecture. You'll wander through an olive grove dotted with scattered remains: fragments of the ancient theatre, the shell of a 10th-century Byzantine basilica, and a mysterious circular archaic structure 41 metres in diameter whose purpose scholars still debate.

The Sanctuary of Artemis Orthia lies 800 metres northeast of the stadium, near the River Eurotas, and is free to visit. This is where, from the 9th century BC onwards, Spartan initiation rites took place — most famously the ritual flogging of adolescent boys before the assembled citizens, a test of endurance and discipline. The Romans were so fascinated that in the 3rd century AD they built an amphitheatre on the spot to accommodate the tourists who came to watch.

The Eurotas valley stretches below Sparta, green and fertile, lined with orange and olive groves. This mythological river of Laconia — on whose banks Leda was seduced by Zeus in the form of a swan — flows peacefully between Mount Taygetus and Mount Parnon. An hour's drive south through the valley reveals mountain panoramas that make it instantly clear why Sparta, with no defensive walls, was so difficult to conquer.

activities.text: Modern Sparta was re-founded in 1834 by order of King Otto, two years after Greek independence, on a grid plan of wide avenues and citrus trees. This clean, unhurried town, far from tourist crowds, is a pleasant place to explore on foot — sit at a café on the square, pop into unpretentious tavernas. In the evening, the central square comes alive with locals gathering at bars shaded by orange trees: a thoroughly Greek provincial atmosphere.

The Mani's orange sausages are Laconia's signature charcuterie: minced pork seasoned with orange zest and spices, then slowly smoked over myrtle wood. You'll find them in local delicatessens and tavernas around Sparta. Pair them with ladera — seasonal vegetables simmered gently in olive oil and tomato, no meat, a lunchtime staple Greeks never tire of.

visits.text: Entering Gialos harbor, the spectacle hits you all at once: hundreds of neoclassical houses in shades of ochre, pink, blue, and yellow rising in tiers up the hillside, reflected in the harbor water. One hour by ferry from Rhodes, Symi owes this architecture to its 19th-century prosperity, when the island was a major shipbuilding center producing up to 500 vessels a year. The village of Gialos is a classified historic monument—facades are protected in their original state. Give yourself time on the waterfront before climbing toward Chorio.

Chorio, the upper village, is reached from Gialos via the Kali Strata—literally "the beautiful stairs"—500 steps lined with the island's finest manor houses. The climb is worth it as much for the sculpted facades as for the views over the harbor that open at each landing. At the top, the ruins of the 15th-century Knights' castle dominate everything, and Chorio's quiet lanes contrast with the bustle of Gialos below.

Panormitis Monastery, in the island's south, is one of the Dodecanese's most important pilgrimage sites. Founded in the 18th century, it's dedicated to the Archangel Michael, patron saint of sailors. Its baroque bell tower rises above a sheltered bay accessible only by sea or by winding road. Inside, the walls are covered with ex-votos offered by grateful sailors—bells, silver boats, objects recovered after shipwrecks. Several boat excursions from Gialos stop here.

The Symi Maritime Museum, housed in the old neoclassical shipyard on Kampos square, traces the history of this island of sailors and sponge divers. Diving suits, archival photos, 19th-century diving equipment: you understand here just how dangerous this fishing was—decompression accidents were frequent and left many men disabled. It's a short visit (30 to 45 minutes) but gives crucial context to what you see around you.

Symi's beaches are mostly pebbles rather than sand, accessible mainly by taxi-boat from Gialos. Agios Georgios Dyssalonas, Nanou, and Agia Marina are among the island's most beautiful coves—turquoise waters, cliff backdrops, no roads leading to them. Check at the harbor in the morning for departures and the last return boat.

activities.text: Position yourself on the ferry deck as you approach Symi—the entrance to Gialos harbor with its amphitheater of houses is one of the Dodecanese's most photographed moments. If you're spending the night on the island, enjoy the evening calm when day-trippers from Rhodes have caught their boat back: tavernas empty by half and lanes regain their silence.

Take a taxi-boat from Gialos to reach one of the isolated coves on the island's south or east coast—Nanou or Agia Marina are best for swimming. It's the only way to access these bays unreachable by road. Ask at the harbor about the last return boat before departing.

Symi's specialty is tiny local shrimp (garides Symis)—minuscule, caught in the deep waters around the island, crispy and delicious eaten whole. They're served fried with a squeeze of lemon and a glass of ouzo. Every harbor taverna offers them—quality varies, but it's the dish you can't miss. Finish with galaktoboureko, that pastry of semolina cream wrapped in phyllo, soaked in lemon syrup.

days_plan.itin_title: Dodecanese Journey: Rhodes, Symi & Kos

visits.text: To reach Thasos, take the ferry from Keramoti, 35 km south-east of Kavala — the crossing takes just 35 minutes and departures run every 30 minutes in season. It's by far the quickest and cheapest option. Once in Limenas, the capital, head down to the ancient agora: it sits right in the heart of the modern town, surrounded by the ruins of sanctuaries dedicated to Heracles, Dionysus, Artemis and Poseidon, with a medieval tower occupying one corner. Information panels at the entrance show a reconstruction of what the square once looked like — helpful for grasping the scale.

The ancient theatre of Thasos, built into a natural hollow in the hillside north-east of town, dates in its earliest phases from the late 4th century BC. In the 1st century AD it was converted into an arena for gladiatorial combat. It has been fully restored in recent years using white marble from Thasos — the same stone used in antiquity, quarried on the island. The view from the upper tiers over the harbour of Limenas and the Aegean is one of the finest on the island.

The Archaeological Museum of Thasos, founded in 1935 and renovated between 1989 and 2000, is the oldest museum in the Eastern Macedonia and Thrace region. Its centrepiece is the Kouros of Thasos: an archaic statue standing 3.5 metres tall, depicting a young man carrying a ram — a masterpiece of the 6th century BC discovered in 1911 in five pieces inside a Byzantine wall, where it had been reused as building material. The museum also holds a fine head of Alexander the Great and an Aphrodite with a Dolphin regularly lent to major international exhibitions.

The acropolis of Thasos dominates the town from its rocky spur. Climb up from the theatre along the signposted path: you'll pass the sanctuaries of Apollo and Athena, then the Sanctuary of Pan carved directly into the rock face. Along the way, look for the Cyclopean marble ramparts — some blocks are fitted so precisely that you still can't slide a credit card between them. The view from the top sweeps across the entire northern coastline of the island.

The village of Panagia, perched in the mountains 10 km south of Limenas, is one of the most beautiful on the island: stone fountains, centuries-old plane trees, tile-roofed houses and cobbled lanes winding up the hillside. It's the birthplace of the local dance tradition and one of the best-preserved villages in Eastern Macedonia. Arrive on a Sunday morning to see the locals in traditional dress after church.

The village of Theologos, 25 km south-west of Limenas, was the island's medieval capital during Genoese and Turkish rule. It was where the population retreated to escape pirates. Its narrow lanes, stone houses with walled gardens and old fountains set it apart from the modernised coastal villages. Allow 45 minutes for a full circuit.

The marble quarries of Alyki, on the cape of the same name 32 km south-east of Limenas, are an exceptional archaeological site: the extraction of Thasian white marble — one of the most prized in antiquity — is documented here from the Archaic period through to Byzantine times. Half-cut blocks have been waiting in the rock for twenty centuries. This white marble with its glittering crystals was used, among other things, to build the Arch of Galerius in Thessaloniki. From the beach at Alyki, submerged blocks are visible beneath the water.

activities.text: Swim at Makryammos, the closest beach to Limenas, just 2 km to the east: a crescent-shaped cove with fine sand, emerald-green water and maritime pines reaching right down to the sea. It's the postcard image of Thasos — the blend of dense vegetation and crystal-clear water that earned the island its nickname of 'the Emerald Isle'.

In the evening, settle into a café on the harbour at Limenas and order a local retsina — the pine-resinated white wine produced on the island, slightly bitter, best drunk chilled with olives and goat's cheese. It's here that you begin to understand why ancient Greece regulated wine production by law: Thasian amphorae stamped with quality-control seals have been found across the entire Mediterranean basin.

Drive around the island: the coastal road covers 95 km and can be comfortably done in a day with stops. Don't miss Golden Beach (Chrisi Amoudia) on the east coast — a long stretch of golden sand lapped by deep-blue water, widely considered one of the finest beaches in the northern Aegean. Behind the beach, archaeologists uncovered a well dating from the 3rd century BC, and on the headland above, the ruins of a 4th-century BC lighthouse.

Tuck into spit-roasted goat with mountain herbs at a taverna in Theologos or Panagia: it's the island's traditional feast dish, oven-baked or spit-roasted with wild herbs gathered from the slopes of Mount Ipsarion. The mountain-village tavernas are cheaper and more authentic than those on the beaches.

visits.text: Thebes (Thiva in modern Greek) lies 50 km from Athens—roughly an hour's drive. The modern city is built directly atop the ancient Cadmea—the acropolis of the mythical city—which explains why ruins emerge randomly among ordinary streets. According to mythology, this is where Heracles and Dionysus were born, where Oedipus ruled, and where in 335 BC Alexander the Great destroyed the city after a rebellion, either massacring or enslaving all 30,000 inhabitants. The renovated Archaeological Museum is remarkable: the painted funeral steles from ancient Demetrias are exceptionally rare in Greece, and Linear B tablets (Mycenaean script from the 15th century BC) testify to the site's antiquity. Plan for 1h30.

About 500 meters east of the museum, Megalo Kastelli Park contains Mycenaean burial chambers from the 14th-13th centuries BC, dubbed the 'Tomb of Oedipus's Children' by local tradition—a legendary attribution, but the architecture is genuine. Along Pindarou Street (named after the Theban poet Pindar, 518-438 BC), remnants of ancient Cadmea peek out between houses. The medieval tower visible at the museum entrance dates from 13th-century Frankish occupation.

activities.text: Thebes is little-touristed—precisely what makes it an authentic stop. The central square's cafés are frequented exclusively by locals. Lunch at a downtown taverna before heading toward Distomo in the afternoon: the detour to Osios Loukas takes 45 minutes from Thebes.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Greece & Gulf Coast

visits.text: Jour 1: Marvel at the iconic White Tower, Thessaloniki's most recognizable landmark rising 34 meters on the stunning waterfront promenade. This cylindrical Ottoman fortification, once known as the 'Tower of Blood' for its dark history as a prison, now houses a fascinating museum chronicling the city's rich heritage from antiquity to modern times. Climb to the top for panoramic views stretching across the Thermaic Gulf and the city skyline.

Jour 1: Stand in awe before the Arch of Galerius, a magnificent triumphal arch built in 303 AD to celebrate Emperor Galerius's victory over the Persians. The marble reliefs depicting battle scenes and symbols of Roman power remain remarkably preserved after 1,700 years. This monumental gateway once connected to a vast imperial palace complex along the ancient Via Egnatia.

Jour 1: Enter the Rotunda of Agios Georgios, a UNESCO World Heritage Site and possibly one of the oldest Christian churches in the world, built around 306 AD. Originally intended as Emperor Galerius's mausoleum, this massive domed structure features early Byzantine mosaics of exceptional beauty. The 6-meter thick walls have withstood earthquakes for seventeen centuries, preserving this architectural marvel.

Jour 1: Explore the Roman Forum and Agora, the ancient heart of Thessaloniki's social and political life unearthed only in the 1960s. The remarkably preserved Odeion theater, built around 200 AD, once hosted poetry recitals and musical performances for the aristocracy. Walk among the remains of Roman baths and marketplaces that reveal daily life in this thriving ancient city.

Jour 1: Discover the Palace of Galerius ruins, an open-air museum revealing the grandeur of imperial Roman architecture. The surviving mosaics and wall fragments hint at the opulence that once characterized this vast complex connected to the Rotunda and Arch. The recently restored Apsidal Hall provides fascinating context through exhibits and reconstructions of the palace's original splendor.

Jour 2: Wander through Ano Poli, Thessaloniki's enchanting Upper Town that miraculously survived the devastating 1917 fire that destroyed two-thirds of the city. This atmospheric neighborhood of winding cobblestone streets, colorful Ottoman-era houses, and traditional tavernas preserves the authentic character of old Thessaloniki. Discover UNESCO-protected Byzantine churches including the 5th-century Church of Hosios David and the 14th-century Vlatadon Monastery hidden among the labyrinthine lanes.

Jour 2: Climb to the Trigonion Tower, known as the 'Balcony of Thessaloniki' for its spectacular panoramic views over the entire city and Aegean Sea. This 15th-century artillery tower along the Byzantine fortifications offers the most sweeping vistas in the city, particularly magical at sunset. The well-preserved defensive walls stretching from this point encircle the historic Upper Town.

Jour 2: Explore the imposing Heptapyrgion fortress, the 'Fortress of Seven Towers' crowning the highest point of the city walls. This Byzantine citadel turned Ottoman fort served as Thessaloniki's main prison until 1989, its cells still bearing witness to turbulent history. The panoramic views from this strategic position reveal why it was crucial for the city's defense for centuries.

Jour 2: Enter the Basilica of Agios Dimitrios, the largest church in Greece dedicated to the city's patron saint who was martyred on this very spot in 306 AD. This UNESCO World Heritage Site preserves stunning early Byzantine mosaics depicting Saint Demetrius and church officials from the 7th century. Descend to the atmospheric crypt where the saint was imprisoned and executed, later becoming a pilgrimage site renowned for its miraculous myrrh.

Jour 2: Visit the Hagia Sophia of Thessaloniki, a 7th-century church modeled on its famous namesake in Constantinople and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The magnificently restored Byzantine mosaics dating from the 10th to 12th centuries rank among the most breathtaking in Greece. This architectural treasure survived centuries as a mosque and multiple fires, its dome only reconstructed in 1980.

activities.text: Jour 1: Stroll along Thessaloniki's magnificent seaside promenade stretching from the White Tower to the port, where locals gather for evening walks and coffee. Pause at The Umbrellas, George Zongolopoulos's iconic 1997 sculpture of stainless steel umbrellas suspended 13 meters high, a symbol of modern Thessaloniki. This waterfront boulevard offers perfect sunset views over the Thermaic Gulf with Mount Olympus visible on clear days.

Jour 1: Photograph the imposing bronze statue of Alexander the Great astride his legendary warhorse Bucephalus in the Garden of Alexander. This 6-meter masterpiece by sculptor Evangelos Moustakas commemorates the Macedonian king born in nearby Pella who conquered an empire spanning three continents. The dramatic pose captures the warrior spirit of history's most celebrated military commander.

Jour 2: Immerse yourself in Greek history at the Archaeological Museum of Thessaloniki, housing treasures spanning from prehistory to late antiquity. Marvel at the Aravissos Hoard of gold artifacts from 4500 BC, Macedonian royal jewelry, and a striking statue of Emperor Augustus. The eight permanent exhibitions illuminate the remarkable civilizations that flourished in Macedonia over millennia.

Jour 2: Explore the Museum of Byzantine Culture, an award-winning modern museum presenting Macedonian treasures from early Christian through post-Byzantine periods. Eleven thoughtfully curated exhibitions showcase stunning mosaics, religious artifacts, and artworks revealing how Hellenistic culture merged with Eastern Orthodox Christianity. This comprehensive collection brings vividly to life the Byzantine Empire's thousand-year impact on Thessaloniki.

Jour 2: Experience the vibrant atmosphere of Aristotelous Square, Thessaloniki's magnificent central plaza overlooking the waterfront. This architectural triumph, designed after the 1917 fire to rival great European squares, features elegant neoclassical arcades housing cafés and restaurants. Linger over Greek coffee as locals have done for generations in this beloved heart of the city.

Jour 2: Discover the colorful Ladadika district, a revitalized Ottoman-era bazaar now bursting with tavernas, bars, and nightlife. The restored former olive oil warehouses create an atmospheric maze of cobbled streets perfect for sampling Greek cuisine and experiencing traditional rebetiko music. This compact neighborhood transforms after dark into Thessaloniki's liveliest entertainment quarter.

Greek Macedonia: Mount Olympus, Ancient Capitals & Byzantine Treasures
Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace

Greece Grand Tour: 21 Days from North to South

visits.text: First stop on the road: the Corinth Canal. Dug between 1882 and 1893 (plans date back to antiquity — Nero himself launched construction in 67 AD before giving up), it connects the Gulf of Corinth to the Aegean Sea over a length of 6.4 km. What really strikes you are the sheer vertical walls: 79 meters of limestone sliced clean. The width is only 21 meters — enough for small cruise ships, but not for supertankers. Park on the road bridge and look straight down: vertigo guaranteed.

The Theatre of Epidaurus is often described as the most perfect in antiquity, and for once, that's not an exaggeration. Built around 330 BC by the Argive architect Polykleitos the Younger, it originally had 34 rows of seats (6,200 capacity), with 21 additional rows added in Roman times to bring the total to 12,000 spectators. Climb to the very top row and ask someone at the bottom to whisper or crumple a sheet of paper: you'll hear it perfectly from 22 meters above. Researchers still debate the exact acoustic explanation — the limestone may filter out low frequencies while letting high ones through. Arrive early in the morning to beat the cruise ship coaches from Athens.

The Sanctuary of Asclepius, god of medicine, is considered the first major healing center of antiquity — the "Lourdes" of the ancient Greek world. The sick traveled from across Greece to practice incubation: they slept in the abaton (a 70-meter-long portico) hoping that Asclepius would appear in their dreams to reveal a cure. The complex also included a gymnasium, baths, a 180-meter stadium and a library — embodying the idea that body and soul heal together. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988.

The small Archaeological Museum of Epidaurus, at the entrance to the site, displays ancient medical instruments, stelae carved with accounts of miraculous healings, and a remarkable statue of Hygieia dating from the 4th century BC. The inscriptions on the stelae are particularly gripping: they describe cures, visions and resurrections — a blend of real medicine and faith. Allow 30 to 45 minutes.

End the day in Nafplio, the first capital of independent Greece in 1829, nestled at the head of a sheltered gulf. Its Venetian center is one of the most beautiful in the Peloponnese: cobblestone lanes, ochre and burgundy façades, cafés lining the local Syntagma Square. If you still have energy, climb up to Palamidi Fortress — 999 steps according to some, 216 according to others. The view over the bay and the floating fort of Bourtzi is worth every step.

Tiryns is often overlooked in favor of Mycenae — unfairly so. This Mycenaean citadel from the 14th century BC boasts the best-preserved Cyclopean walls in the ancient world: up to 14 meters thick, with vaulted galleries carved into the walls themselves. The ancient Greeks believed only the Cyclopes could have assembled such massive blocks. Homer called it "Tiryns of the mighty walls." It's a UNESCO site and far less crowded than Mycenae: chances are you'll have the place to yourself.

Mycenae is the city of Agamemnon, the king who led the Greeks against Troy. Founded, according to legend, by Perseus, it reached its peak between 1400 and 1200 BC and influenced the entire eastern Mediterranean. Entering through the Lion Gate, erected around 1250 BC, you pass beneath the oldest monumental relief sculpture in Europe: two lionesses (their metal heads are lost) flanking a Minoan-style column. The Cyclopean rampart stones weigh several tonnes each and are fitted together without mortar. Arrive early — in peak season, the parking lot fills up by 9 AM.

Don't miss the Treasury of Atreus, 300 meters from the main gate — it's actually a royal tholos tomb dating from 1250 BC. You enter through a 36-meter open-air corridor (the dromos), flanked by walls 6 meters high. The circular chamber measures 14.50 meters in diameter and 13.20 meters in height: it was the largest dome in the Western world until the Pantheon in Rome was built five centuries later. The acoustics inside are eerie and mesmerizing — try clapping your hands.

Head back down to Nafplio to explore its Venetian old town. Palamidi Fortress, perched 216 meters above the harbor, was built by the Venetians between 1711 and 1714 in a record time of three years. From its upper bastion, the view sweeps across the entire Gulf of Argolis. If you're only going to climb one fortress at sunset on this trip, make it this one: the golden light on the gulf is stunning.

activities.text: In summer, the Athens & Epidaurus Festival programs performances of ancient tragedies and comedies on Friday and Saturday evenings at the Theatre of Epidaurus. Aeschylus, Sophocles and Euripides performed in their original setting, under the open sky, with the Argolid mountains as a backdrop — an experience unlike anything else. Check the program before your trip and book well in advance.

The bay of Tolo, a small seaside resort a few kilometers from Nafplio, makes the ideal base for the night. The waterfront tavernas serve grilled fish straight off the morning boats. Order some psarosoupa (fish soup) and saganaki (fried cheese) with a glass of local white wine.

Nafplio deserves a proper on-foot exploration. Cross Syntagma Square, lined with Venetian and Ottoman houses, pass the Vourliotis Mosque (now a cinema), then walk along the harbor to the Bourtzi Fort, built on a tiny island in the middle of the bay. Water taxis shuttle you there in just a few minutes.

Argolid cuisine is generous: lamb kleftiko (slow-braised lamb), crispy spanakopita, local cheeses drizzled with Peloponnesian honey. The tavernas along Nafplio's harbor are among the best in the region — look for the ones without a laminated menu posted by the door.

visits.text: Trikala is little known to foreign travellers — which makes it one of the best "real" towns in Thessaly. The Byzantine fortress on the hill above the city has ancient origins: this is the site of the Sanctuary of Asclepius, the Greek god of medicine whose mythical birthplace was Trikala. The current fortress dates from the 4th century, with later Byzantine and Ottoman modifications. The Ottoman clock tower, still standing at the eastern corner, has become the symbol of the city.

The Varousi quarter, at the foot of the fortress, is the old Ottoman neighbourhood with its narrow lanes, overhanging houses, and old churches. The Asclepieion of Trikala — the remains of the ancient healing sanctuary dedicated to Asclepius — is visible in the park below the fortress. The Koursoum Mosque (16th c.), one of the best preserved in Greece, stands in the lower town — it's no longer active but its Ottoman architecture is remarkable.

On the banks of the Litheos river, the Matsopoulos Mill (19th c.) is a singular piece of industrial heritage: it was the first flour and pasta factory in Greece, built in imposing stone and still standing. The pedestrian bridge crossing the Litheos opposite the mill is where locals gather in the evening — with views over the water and the lights of the old town.

activities.text: Trikala is known for its spiced sausage (loukaniko Trikalinon) seasoned with herbs from nearby Mount Koziakas, its mountain feta cheese, and its tsipouro. The tavernas in the centre are affordable and packed with locals — a welcome break after the tourist prices of Kalambaka. The Tuesday morning market in the lower town draws producers from across the region.
