visits.text: Tripoli, the capital of Arcadia at 650 metres above sea level, deserves a brief stop for its Plateia Areos: a grand neoclassical square with fountains, open-air cafés and the archaeological museum building designed by architect Ernst Ziller — the same man who drew up the Royal Palace in Athens. Tripoli may not look like much at first glance, but it makes an ideal base for exploring the mountain heartland of Arcadia.

The Tripoli Archaeological Museum houses collections from ancient Arcadia: sculptures, inscriptions, ceramics and mosaics from local sites such as Tegea and Mantinea. The Mycenaean finds from Arcadia are displayed here too, offering a glimpse into a region that most classical itineraries overlook entirely. Allow 40 to 50 minutes.

Dimitsana, a mountain village 50 km from Tripoli, clings to a rocky spur high above the Lousios Gorge. Its cobbled lanes, stone tower-houses and abundance of churches make it one of the most beautiful villages in Arcadia. The library founded in 1764 once held 5,000 volumes before the 1821 revolution — when, according to the memoirs of Kolokotronis, the pages were torn out and used to make cartridges with locally produced gunpowder.

The Open-Air Water Power Museum of Dimitsana, opened in 1997, is the only one of its kind in Greece. It showcases restored buildings housing a flour mill, a tannery, a gunpowder mill (which supplied the 1821 revolutionaries with ammunition) and a distillery. The working hydraulic mechanisms, explained with scale models, paint a vivid picture of pre-industrial life in Arcadia.

activities.text: Hike through the Lousios Gorge between the monasteries of Prodromos and Philosophos: a 45-minute to one-hour trail squeezed between blackened limestone cliffs, above a river whose waters Pausanias once described as the coldest in Greece. The Monastery of Prodromos (16th century), still home to monks, is literally glued to the cliff face — its wooden balconies jut out over a sheer drop of several dozen metres. The Monastery of Philosophos (rebuilt in 1691) is the easier starting point.

Tasting session in Dimitsana or Stemnitsa: hilopites — small square egg noodles dried in the sun — and trachanas, a preparation of flour and fermented dried milk. These are the two quintessential mountain dishes of Arcadia, most often cooked into hearty winter soups. In summer, tavernas serve them lighter, topped with grated cheese. Arcadian fir honey, dark and thick, is among the finest in Greece.

days_plan.itin_title: Peloponnese Road Trip: Mycenae, Epidaurus & Olympia

visits.text: Porto Katsiki ('Goat Port') is one of the most photographed beaches in the Mediterranean. A concave limestone cliff drops sheer onto a strip of white pebbles and sand, lapped by water of an almost unreal blue-turquoise hue — locals attribute the colour to the geological composition of the seabed. From the car park at the top of the cliff, roughly a hundred steps cut into the rock lead down to the beach. Arrive before 10am in July–August: after that, the beach fills quickly and the staircase becomes a queue.

Egremni, a few kilometres north of Porto Katsiki, stretches for nearly 2.5 km of golden pebbles at the foot of 150-metre cliffs. The earthquake of 17 November 2015 destroyed the old stairway of 347 steps and buried part of the beach under a landslide. Since 2022, a new stairway has been rebuilt, but the beach is still mainly reached by boat from Vassiliki — which keeps it wilder and less crowded than Porto Katsiki. The waves can be powerful: exercise caution with children.

Vassiliki is a world-class windsurfing destination — the reliable afternoon thermals that blow across the bay from the open sea draw competitors and enthusiasts from around the globe. In the morning, the bay is a mirror-flat playground for beginners; from 2pm, the wind picks up and sails multiply. Dozens of windsurf and kitesurf schools cater to all levels.

Karya, a mountain village in the centre of the island at 550 metres altitude, is famous throughout Greece for its handmade embroidery — the village women carry on a tradition dating back to the 19th century, producing intricate floral patterns. The local folklore museum tells the story. In summer, an artisan market livens up the central square. The view from the heights takes in both coasts of the island.

Kathisma beach, halfway between the capital and Porto Katsiki on the west coast, is the largest organised beach on the island: golden sand, sunbeds, beach bars and occasionally paragliding sessions from the neighbouring hill. If Porto Katsiki feels too chaotic, Kathisma offers the same turquoise water with a little more comfort.

activities.text: Take a windsurfing lesson in Vassiliki — it's one of Europe's most renowned spots to learn or improve. Conditions are close to ideal: calm mornings for the basics, steady thermal winds in the afternoon for practice. Book your slot the day before — schools fill up fast in high season.

Head out early by boat from Vassiliki to Egremni and Porto Katsiki — the perspective from the sea on those white cliffs plunging straight down is completely different from the view at the top. Water taxis and small excursion boats leave from the harbour from 9am.

Savoro is a traditional preservation dish of Corfiot and Lefkadian fishermen: fish (sardines, mackerel) is fried, then marinated in vinegar, raisins and rosemary. The result is a surprisingly tangy-sweet preparation served cold — perfect for a light lunch after the beach.

Pasteli, a sesame-and-honey bar, is the traditional beach snack of the Ionian Islands — people have been eating it since ancient Greece. Simple, energising and utterly unprocessed. Pick up a few from a grocery shop in Vassiliki before heading down to Porto Katsiki: you'll need the fuel for the climb back up.

visits.text: Step into ancient history at the Archaeological Site of Pella, birthplace of Alexander the Great and capital of the Macedonian kingdom. This was where Aristotle tutored the young prince who would conquer an empire stretching from Greece to India. Remarkable floor mosaics depicting hunting scenes and mythological figures reveal the sophistication of Macedonian art at its zenith.

Explore the Pella Archaeological Museum housing extraordinary finds from the ancient capital, including exquisite mosaics, royal artifacts, and everyday objects. The collections illuminate life in the kingdom that produced history's most legendary conqueror and his father Philip II. Standing where Alexander walked before embarking on his world-changing campaigns creates an unforgettable connection to antiquity.

activities.text: Descend into the UNESCO World Heritage Royal Tombs of Aigai at Vergina, the ancient Macedonian capital where Philip II, father of Alexander the Great, lies buried. The underground museum preserves the magnificent intact tomb with its stunning frescoes, golden larnax containing Philip's remains, and priceless royal treasures. This extraordinary archaeological discovery ranks among the most important of the 20th century.

visits.text: Volos stretches between the sea and the first slopes of Mount Pelion — a setting that gives it a character unlike anywhere else in Greece. Start with the Athanasakeion Archaeological Museum, built in 1909 thanks to the fortune of Alexios Athanasakis, a Pelion merchant who made his wealth in Egypt. The collection will surprise you: the painted funerary stelae from ancient Demetrias, their colours still vivid, are an absolute rarity in Greece — the pigments survived because the stelae were buried. You'll also see Neolithic pieces from the sites of Dimini and Sesklo, the two oldest organized settlements in Europe (6800 BC), located less than 15 km from the city.

The old Palia quarter, a former Byzantine fortress fortified in the 4th century, grew around the port from the 19th century onwards. The Ottomans held it from 1423 until the liberation of Volos in 1881 — nearly 460 years. Behind the train station, the Volos City Museum occupies the former Papantou tobacco warehouse (1920), restored in 2014: the glass floor on the first level reveals 6th–7th-century water channels beneath your feet. The building alone is worth a visit for its industrial architecture.

activities.text: In the evening, Volos reveals its true self through its tsipouradika — tsipouro bars, named after the local grape spirit, where you eat mezze while sipping slowly. It's an institution here: Volos is widely regarded as the Greek capital of tsipouro. The waterfront (Argonauts' promenade) is packed with these places, at their best around aperitif hour. The city owes its mythological name to Iolcos, from where Jason and the Argonauts are said to have set sail in search of the Golden Fleece — a myth that looms over the entire region.

days_plan.itin_title: Thessaly & Meteora

visits.text: The old town of Xanthi (Palia Poli) is one of the largest preserved traditional settlements in Greece: 380,000 m² declared a protected zone in 1976, with over 1,200 listed buildings. It was rebuilt after two devastating earthquakes in 1829 and reached its zenith between 1880 and 1925 thanks to basma tobacco — an exceptionally aromatic variety that supplied imperial courts and European markets. The Epirote and Macedonian craftsmen who built it blended Baroque, Neoclassical, Romanesque and Ottoman elements into a result the Greeks nicknamed 'Little Paris'.

Walk up the main street to Demokratias Square to see the clock tower, erected in 1870 by the Pomak notable Hadji Emin Aga. The Greek authorities wanted to demolish it in 1972 — protests by Muslim residents forced a reversal. It stands as a symbol of Xanthi's particular brand of coexistence, where Greek Orthodox Christians, Turks and Pomaks have shared the same streets for centuries.

The History and Folklore Museum of Xanthi occupies the Kougioumtzoglou House, a former double residence built in the 1860s by the Kougioumtzoglou brothers, major tobacco merchants. It's a rare example of a three-storey twin house in the local style. The collections feature traditional costumes, embroidered textiles, craft tools and archives from the tobacco era. Allow an hour to do it justice.

Near the train station, the former tobacco warehouses (the Mandra) bear witness to Xanthi's economic might at the turn of the 20th century. The most notable, shaped like the Greek letter Pi (Π), housed the Ottoman tobacco monopoly's operations until 1912. It's currently being converted into a cultural venue. If you drive or bus through the neighbourhood, its imposing red-brick outline is visible from the road.

activities.text: Wander through Xanthi's traditional bazaar, in the lanes around Antika Square: this is where you'll find the open-air cafés, spice-scented groceries, leather workshops and Turkish-coffee shopfronts. The atmosphere in the morning is particularly authentic — locals gather here before heading to work. A thick Greek coffee, served with a glass of cool water, is the ritual opener.

Day trip into the Rhodope Mountains to the Pomak villages, about an hour's drive north: Sminthi with its white mosque and stone lanes, Echinos set amid deep forest, Ottoman bridges over mountain torrents. These villages, tucked into the folds of the Rhodopes, have preserved an architecture and way of life virtually unchanged since the 19th century. The road itself is spectacular — forested gorges, pristine rivers, not a tourist in sight.

Try soutzouk loukoum, Xanthi's signature sweet: Turkish delight flavoured with walnuts or rose, sometimes coated in a layer of toasted hazelnuts. It's the Thracian version of a widespread Ottoman confection, but Xanthi's sweet shops have a well-established regional reputation. Pair it with a lightly anise-flavoured Thracian tsipouro — milder than ouzo.

days_plan.itin_title: Thrace Road Trip: Kavala, Thasos & Philippi

visits.text: Zakynthos Town — dubbed 'Il Fiore di Levante' (the Flower of the East) by the Venetians — was completely destroyed in the 1953 earthquake and rebuilt in a neo-Venetian style with arcaded streets and colonnaded façades. Solomos Square, the largest in town, opens directly onto the Ionian Sea. Here you'll find the Byzantine Museum, a statue of the poet Dionysios Solomos (who penned the Greek national anthem in 1823), and the town hall. This is where the action is in the morning; in the evening, head up to Bohali.

The Church of Agios Dionysios is dedicated to the island's patron saint. Founded in 1708, it houses his relics in a silver reliquary and features a gilded carved iconostasis along with fine Byzantine frescoes restored after 1953. The bell tower overlooking the harbour is one of the defining landmarks of Zakynthos. Every year on 24 August and 17 December, thousands of worshippers gather here for the saint's feast days.

The Byzantine Museum of Zakynthos, on Solomos Square, is one of the richest in the Ionians. It brings together carved wooden Byzantine icons, original frescoes from the 15th to 17th centuries rescued from churches destroyed in 1953, and 18th-century paintings by artists of the Ionian School such as Doxaras, Koutouzis and Kandounis. Some canvases depict the town before the earthquake — a striking contrast with what it looks like today. Allow about 1 hour 30 minutes.

The Venetian Castle of Bohali, set on the hillside above the town, was built in the 15th century by the Venetians on even older foundations. The pine-shaded enclosure overlooks the harbour from 150 metres — from the crumbling ramparts you can take in the entire bay, the port and the mountains of the interior in a single sweep. Drive up or walk (25 minutes from Solomos Square). At sunset, locals from the village of Bohali settle on café terraces with a glass in hand — join them.

The National Marine Park of Zakynthos (NMPZ) was established to protect the Caretta caretta sea turtles. It encompasses Laganas Bay and the nesting beaches on the island's south coast. Turtles lay their eggs between June and August on these shores — access is regulated, but glass-bottom boat excursions let you observe them in their natural habitat without disturbing them. If you're visiting in summer, don't miss a trip out on the water.

Navagio Bay — also known as 'Smuggler's Cove' — is the most photographed spot in all of Greece. It owes its name to the wreck of the cargo ship Panagiotis, which ran aground here on the night of 2 October 1980 after being chased by the Greek navy during a storm. The vessel had left Argostoli loaded with contraband goods. White limestone cliffs rise over 200 metres above the beach of white sand and turquoise water. Note: since 2022, landing on the beach has been banned due to rockfall hazards — but you can approach by boat and swim in the bay. For the view from above, take the road from Anafonitria or Volimes to the free clifftop viewing platform.

The Blue Caves are a series of arches and tunnels carved by the sea into the white limestone cliffs on the island's north coast, between the port of Agios Nikolaos and Cape Skinari. Their name comes from the electric blue colour the water takes on inside, the result of sunlight reflecting off the submerged limestone — a phenomenon strikingly similar to the Blue Grotto on Capri. The rock walls and even your body underwater glow vivid blue. They are only accessible by small boat (50 passengers maximum to enter the arches). Depart from the port of Agios Nikolaos or from Cape Skinari.

Cape Skinari is the northernmost tip of Zakynthos, facing Kefalonia — on a clear day, Mount Ainos on the neighbouring island is perfectly visible. The lighthouse dates from 1897 and stands 9.5 metres tall. This is the departure point for boat excursions to the Blue Caves and Navagio Bay. The traditional windmills on the cape have become one of the island's most recognisable symbols.

Anafonitria Monastery, set inland in the northwest, dates from the 15th century. It is one of the oldest on the island and is famous for having sheltered Saint Dionysios as a monk before he became the patron saint of Zakynthos. It is surrounded by centuries-old olive groves and a medieval defence tower that still stands. The atmosphere here is peaceful, far removed from the beaches.

Xigia Beach is a natural curiosity: sulphur springs rise directly into the sea from the rocks, giving the water a milky tint and a faint but distinctive smell. The therapeutic properties of these sulphurous waters have been known locally for a long time. The beach is small, wild and rarely crowded — a fine alternative to Navagio for anyone looking for a different kind of swim.

activities.text: Roma Street, the main pedestrian thoroughfare leading off Saint Mark's Square, is lined with local shops where you can pick up mandolato nougat (almonds, honey, egg whites — the island's signature sweet), ladotyri (a hard cheese aged in olive oil, Zakynthos PDO) and olive-oil products. It's also the place for evening outings — the tavernas kick into gear around 8 pm.

Facing Saint Mark's Square stands the Solomos and Kalvos Museum, housed in the island's former casino and open since 1968. On the ground floor lie the tombs of the two poets — Dionysios Solomos, author of the national anthem, and Andreas Kalvos. Upstairs: manuscripts, personal belongings and period documents. A must for anyone interested in Greek literature.

Fritoura is the local evening tradition: small fish — mackerel, sardines, anchovies — fried in olive oil and served with a squeeze of lemon. Simple, no-nonsense, excellent. The Zakynthos waterfront is lined with tavernas serving fritoura as a starter alongside Verdea — the local white wine, dry and lively, made from grape varieties found nowhere else.

Verdea is the PDO white wine of Zakynthos, made from indigenous varieties (Pavlos, Goustolidi) grown nowhere else in Greece. Dry, with bright acidity and a faint bitterness on the finish — a world apart from the soft whites produced across most of the islands. Order it at the taverna with the fritoura or local cheeses.

The ideal plan for this last day: leave early in the morning for Cape Skinari and board a small boat to the Blue Caves (allow 2 hours). Then head inland to Volimes and Anafonitria, winding through olive groves and mountain villages. In the afternoon, make the detour to the Navagio viewpoint for the photo, then finish at Xigia Beach for a dip in the sulphurous waters before driving back to Zakynthos Town.

The village of Volimes, up in the hills, is known for its textile artisans — rugs, embroidery and lace are displayed in the small shops lining the main street. It's also where you'll find the best local produce: mountain honey, olive oil and handmade mandolato nougat. The vendors are often the producers themselves.

The combined boat trip covering the Blue Caves and Navagio Bay (seen from the sea) takes 3 to 4 hours depending on the option chosen. Boats depart from the port of Agios Nikolaos (10 minutes from Navagio) or from the main port of Zakynthos Town (90 minutes). Book the evening before in summer — spots go fast. Small boats carrying 12 to 50 passengers can enter the caves; the larger vessels can only circle them from outside.

Ladotyri is the PDO cheese of Zakynthos: a hard cheese made from sheep's and goat's milk, aged in olive oil — hence the name (lado = oil, tyri = cheese). It has a firm texture, a dark rind and a powerful, slightly fruity flavour. Eat it at the end of a meal with local honey, or diced as an appetiser.
